description.description: A Study in Scarlet, the 1887 Sherlock Holmes novel by Arthur Conan Doyle that introduces Holmes and Watson and centres on the Lauriston Gardens murders involving Enoch Drebber and Joseph Stangerson.

The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes, the first Arthur Conan Doyle short-story collection featuring Sherlock Holmes and Watson, including A Scandal in Bohemia, The Red-Headed League, and The Blue Carbuncle.

The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin, Gentleman-Burglar, the first Lupin short-story collection by Maurice Leblanc presenting Lupin's early escapades including his arrest aboard La Provence and the Queen's Necklace affair.
A Scandal in Bohemia, a Sherlock Holmes short story in which Holmes is hired by the King of Bohemia to recover a compromising photograph held by the opera singer Irene Adler.
The Red-Headed League, a Sherlock Holmes short story in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes in which a bogus league paying red-haired men to copy encyclopaedias conceals a bank-tunnel robbery scheme.
The Adventure of the Blue Carbuncle, a short story in 'The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes' in which Holmes traces a stolen blue carbuncle gem recovered from a goose's crop at Christmas.

The Arrest of Arsène Lupin, the opening story of The Extraordinary Adventures in which Lupin is caught aboard the transatlantic liner La Provence by Inspector Ganimard after stealing jewels during the crossing.
Sherlock Holmes Arrives Too Late, a story from The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin featuring Herlock Sholmes, Leblanc's parodic stand-in for Holmes, who is outwitted by Lupin.

The Lauriston Gardens murders, the opening crime in A Study in Scarlet in which Enoch Drebber is found dead in an empty Brixton house with no apparent cause, initiating Holmes and Watson's first collaboration.

The Bohemia photograph case, the investigation in A Scandal in Bohemia in which Holmes is commissioned to recover a compromising photograph from Irene Adler to protect the reputation of the King of Bohemia.

The Red-Headed League bank plot, the criminal scheme in which John Clay and associates lure red-haired pawnbroker Jabez Wilson away from his shop each day so they can tunnel into the City and Suburban Bank beneath it.
The Transatlantic Lupin arrest, the event in The Arrest of Arsène Lupin in which Inspector Ganimard apprehends Lupin aboard the liner La Provence after a jewel theft during the Atlantic crossing.
The Queen's Necklace affair, the case in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsene Lupin, Gentleman-Burglar, centred on the theft of Queen Marie-Antoinette's historic diamond necklace.
Sherlock Holmes, the world-famous consulting detective created by Arthur Conan Doyle, who investigates crimes across London and beyond with his companion Dr. Watson.
Dr. John H. Watson, army doctor and loyal friend to Sherlock Holmes, who serves as the primary narrator and chronicler of Holmes's cases across multiple collections.
Young Stamford, the mutual acquaintance who introduces Watson to Sherlock Holmes at St. Bartholomew's Hospital in A Study in Scarlet.

Jefferson Hope, the American cabman and avenger in A Study in Scarlet who travels from Utah to London to kill Enoch Drebber and Joseph Stangerson in retribution for the death of Lucy Ferrier in the Salt Lake Valley.

Enoch Drebber, a former Mormon elder and the first murder victim in A Study in Scarlet, found dead at Lauriston Gardens in Brixton as part of Jefferson Hope's revenge for the forced marriage of Lucy Ferrier.

Joseph Stangerson, one of the two Utah Mormons murdered in London by Jefferson Hope as revenge for the death of Lucy Ferrier, his killing part of the Lauriston Gardens murders investigated in A Study in Scarlet.

Irene Adler, the American opera singer in A Scandal in Bohemia who outwits Sherlock Holmes by discovering his fire-alarm ruse and escaping with the compromising photograph of herself and the King of Bohemia.
The King of Bohemia, the royal client who hires Sherlock Holmes to recover a compromising photograph held by Irene Adler, his former lover, in A Scandal in Bohemia.

Jabez Wilson, the red-haired pawnbroker in The Red-Headed League who is lured away from his Saxe-Coburg Square shop each day by a bogus league paying him to copy encyclopaedias while John Clay tunnels into the bank below.

John Clay, the master criminal who invents the Red-Headed League to lure Jabez Wilson away from his pawnbroker's shop while tunnelling into the vault of the City and Suburban Bank below, in The Red-Headed League.
Arsène Lupin, the gentleman-burglar and master of disguise created by Maurice Leblanc, who outwits police and rival detectives across a series of French adventures.

Ganimard, the persistent but repeatedly outfoxed French police inspector who is Arsène Lupin's principal pursuer throughout the Leblanc stories, having arrested Lupin aboard La Provence only to see him escape again.
Herlock Sholmes (Holmlock Shears), Leblanc's thinly veiled pastiche of Sherlock Holmes who appears in the Arsène Lupin stories as a formidable English detective consistently outwitted by Lupin.

Watson narrator role, the narrative function by which Dr. John H. Watson records and publishes Sherlock Holmes's cases across A Study in Scarlet, The Sign of the Four, and the major story collections.
221B Baker Street, the London lodgings of Sherlock Holmes and Dr. Watson, serving as Holmes's base of operations and consulting rooms throughout the major collections.
Lauriston Gardens, the Brixton street in A Study in Scarlet where Enoch Drebber is found murdered in an empty house, the scene of the opening crime that brings Holmes and Watson together.
London, the primary urban setting of A Study in Scarlet and the wider Sherlock Holmes canon, the city where Holmes and Watson maintain their Baker Street base.
The transatlantic French ocean liner aboard which Arsène Lupin commits a jewel theft and is subsequently identified and arrested by Inspector Ganimard in The Arrest of Arsene Lupin.
Holmes's newly devised chemical reagent capable of detecting bloodstains on surfaces, demonstrated in his Baker Street laboratory as a key forensic advance in A Study in Scarlet.
The wedding ring left at the Lauriston Gardens murder scene by Jefferson Hope, used by Holmes as an evidential clue pointing to the murderer's domestic history in A Study in Scarlet.

The Irene Adler photograph, the compromising image of Irene Adler with the King of Bohemia in A Scandal in Bohemia that the King wishes to recover before his royal marriage and that Adler uses as a safeguard.
The Red-Headed League advertisement, a fraudulent notice used in The Red-Headed League to lure Jabez Wilson away from his pawnshop so that criminals could tunnel into a nearby bank vault.

The Queen's Necklace, Marie-Antoinette's famous historic diamond necklace whose theft is the central mystery of The Queen's Necklace from The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsene Lupin, Gentleman-Burglar.

The Red-Headed League, the fictitious organisation invented by John Clay and publicised by newspaper advertisement to lure the red-headed Jabez Wilson into a decoy encyclopaedia-copying job, masking the bank-tunnel plot in The Red-Headed League.
Jefferson Hope's long-pursued revenge upon Enoch Drebber and Joseph Stangerson for the abduction and death of Lucy Ferrier, culminating in the murders at Lauriston Gardens in A Study in Scarlet.
Arsène Lupin's arrest aboard the transatlantic liner SS La Provence, identified by a telegram from Inspector Ganimard, in The Arrest of Arsène Lupin.

The Drebber and Stangerson murders, the twin killings at the heart of A Study in Scarlet in which Jefferson Hope murders two Mormon elders in revenge for the abduction and forced marriage of Lucy Ferrier.

The discovery of Enoch Drebber's body at Lauriston Gardens, the opening event of A Study in Scarlet in which a man is found dead in an empty Brixton house with the word RACHE written in blood on the wall.
The wedding ring found at the Lauriston Gardens murder scene — a vital clue linking the victim to his past — examined by Holmes in A Study in Scarlet.

Jefferson Hope's years-long revenge motive in A Study in Scarlet, the obsessive pursuit of Enoch Drebber and Joseph Stangerson to London to avenge the death of his beloved Lucy Ferrier, caused by their actions in Utah.

Holmes's new blood-stain reagent, first demonstrated in the laboratory scene at the opening of A Study in Scarlet, a chemical test capable of detecting the presence of human blood with reliability superior to earlier tests.
A trap used by Sherlock Holmes in A Study in Scarlet, placing a newspaper advertisement for a found wedding ring to lure the murderer into revealing himself.

The Bohemia photograph leverage scheme, the threat in A Scandal in Bohemia by which Irene Adler retains a compromising photograph of herself with the King of Bohemia as a safeguard against any royal reprisals.
The motive in A Scandal in Bohemia: the King of Bohemia seeks to recover a compromising photograph held by Irene Adler that threatens his royal reputation.

Holmes's staged fire alarm in A Scandal in Bohemia—throwing a smoke rocket into Irene Adler's drawing room and raising a cry of fire—to trick her into instinctively revealing the hiding place of the compromising photograph.

The staged fire alarm thrown by Holmes into Irene Adler's drawing room in A Scandal in Bohemia, causing her to instinctively rush to protect the hiding place of the compromising photograph of herself and the King of Bohemia.

The Red-Headed League bank tunnel scheme, the criminal plan in The Red-Headed League by which John Clay and associates dig a tunnel from Jabez Wilson's Saxe-Coburg Square shop into the vault of the City and Suburban Bank.

The fraudulent Red-Headed League encyclopedia-copying job used by John Clay to keep Jabez Wilson away from his shop while a tunnel was dug into the City and Suburban Bank below, in The Red-Headed League.
The plot device in The Red-Headed League by which Jabez Wilson is lured away from his pawnbroker's shop via a decoy encyclopedia-copying job, allowing John Clay to tunnel into the bank below.
The cellar of the City and Suburban Bank in Covent Garden, below which John Clay and his accomplice tunnel from Jabez Wilson's pawnshop in The Red-Headed League.
Arsène Lupin's theft of jewels aboard the transatlantic liner SS La Provence, the opening crime in The Arrest of Arsene Lupin from The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsene Lupin, Gentleman-Burglar.
The Bernard d'Andrézy persona adopted by Arsène Lupin aboard the SS La Provence during the events of The Arrest of Arsene Lupin.
The moment in The Arrest of Arsene Lupin when Inspector Ganimard identifies the passenger Arsène Lupin aboard the transatlantic liner La Provence, leading to his arrest.
Arsène Lupin's motive of public notoriety and rivalry, framing his contest with Holmlock Shears as France versus England and revelling in the fame his exploits bring.
The theft of Queen Marie-Antoinette's diamond necklace, a celebrated crime investigated by Arsène Lupin in The Queen's Necklace from The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsene Lupin, Gentleman-Burglar.

Inspector Tobias Gregson, a Scotland Yard detective described as energetic and gallant within his limitations who appears in A Study in Scarlet and later in The Adventure of the Red Circle and The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge.
Inspector Lestrade, a Scotland Yard detective who appears throughout the Sherlock Holmes canon as a capable but conventional police officer who frequently calls on Holmes's superior reasoning.
Constable John Rance, the police officer who first discovers the body at the Lauriston Gardens crime scene in A Study in Scarlet.
John Ferrier, a settler in the Salt Lake Valley whose backstory is central to the motivation of Jefferson Hope in A Study in Scarlet.
Lucy Ferrier, the adopted daughter of John Ferrier in Salt Lake Valley whose forced marriage and death provide Jefferson Hope with his motive for revenge in A Study in Scarlet.

The word RACHE written in blood on the wall at the Lauriston Gardens murder scene, correctly identified by Holmes as the German word for revenge rather than an unfinished woman's name as Scotland Yard assumed, in A Study in Scarlet.
A twin pillbox containing one poisoned and one harmless pill, used by the murderer in A Study in Scarlet to give the victim a fatal choice.
The cab-track and boot footprints found by Sherlock Holmes at the Lauriston Gardens murder scene, used to deduce key physical characteristics of the killer Jefferson Hope in A Study in Scarlet.

Salt Lake Valley (Hope backstory), the Utah setting in the backstory of A Study in Scarlet where Jefferson Hope witnesses Lucy Ferrier's forced marriage to Enoch Drebber and swears revenge that he eventually takes in London.
The King of Bohemia, who engages Sherlock Holmes to recover a compromising photograph held by Irene Adler in A Scandal in Bohemia.
Godfrey Norton, the lawyer who marries Irene Adler and whose marriage ceremony Holmes inadvertently witnesses in A Scandal in Bohemia.
Vincent Spaulding, the alias used by the criminal John Clay while working as Jabez Wilson's assistant in order to dig a tunnel to the bank in The Red-Headed League.
Duncan Ross, an associate of John Clay who manages the fraudulent Red-Headed League office in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes to keep Jabez Wilson occupied while the bank tunnel is dug.
Inspector Peter Jones, the Scotland Yard detective who collaborates with Holmes and Merryweather in the bank cellar to catch John Clay in The Red-Headed League.
Mr. Merryweather, the chairman of the City and Suburban Bank who joins Holmes and Watson in the cellar vigil in The Red-Headed League to foil John Clay's tunnel robbery.

Briony Lodge in St. John's Wood, the home of Irene Adler where the compromising photograph of her and the King of Bohemia is concealed and where Holmes conducts his staged fire-alarm investigation in A Scandal in Bohemia.
The City and Suburban Bank, whose vaults John Clay and the Red-Headed League plot to rob by tunnelling from Jabez Wilson's adjacent shop, as investigated by Holmes in The Red-Headed League.

The Red-Headed League newspaper advertisement placed by John Clay to lure the red-headed Jabez Wilson into a fake encyclopaedia-copying job, providing cover for the tunnel being dug into the City and Suburban Bank below, in The Red-Headed League.

The pages of the Encyclopaedia Britannica that Jabez Wilson is paid to copy out at the Red-Headed League office — a ruse to keep him away from his shop while John Clay tunnels into the bank below in The Red-Headed League.
La Provence, the transatlantic French ocean liner on which Arsène Lupin commits jewel thefts and is identified and arrested by Inspector Ganimard, as described in The Arrest of Arsene Lupin.
Queen Marie-Antoinette's historic diamond necklace, the object stolen and at the centre of the plot in The Queen's Necklace from The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
The Rozaine traveller persona adopted by Arsène Lupin aboard the transatlantic liner La Provence, one of his disguises during the events of The Arrest of Arsene Lupin.
The telegram sent by Inspector Ganimard to identify Arsène Lupin as a passenger aboard the transatlantic liner La Provence, leading to his arrest in The Arrest of Arsène Lupin.

The Hound of the Baskervilles, a Sherlock Holmes novel by Arthur Conan Doyle in which Holmes investigates the legend of a phantom hound on Dartmoor and uncovers Jack Stapleton's scheme to murder Sir Henry Baskerville with a phosphorus-painted dog.

The Baskerville curse investigation, the central case of The Hound of the Baskervilles, in which Sherlock Holmes is called in to investigate the seemingly supernatural threat hanging over the Baskerville family on Dartmoor.

The Innocence of Father Brown, the first Father Brown collection by G. K. Chesterton, opening with the Blue Cross story in which Father Brown outwits the criminal Flambeau through priestly moral inference.

The Blue Cross case, in The Innocence of Father Brown, in which Father Brown frustrates Flambeau's attempt to steal a valuable sapphire cross and assists Inspector Aristide Valentin in apprehending the master thief.
Father Brown, a mild-mannered Catholic priest and amateur detective created by G. K. Chesterton, who unravels crimes through keen moral and psychological insight.
The Red Thumb Mark, a Dr. Thorndyke novel by R. Austin Freeman in which Thorndyke challenges fingerprint evidence by exposing a forged red thumb print.
A criminal case in which a forged thumb-print is used as evidence, investigated by Dr. Thorndyke in The Red Thumb Mark.
Dr. John Thorndyke, a medico-legal expert and forensic detective created by R. Austin Freeman, who solves crimes through rigorous scientific and medical analysis.

The Amateur Cracksman, the first Raffles collection by E. W. Hornung in which the gentleman-cricketer A. J. Raffles begins his burgling career from his Albany chambers, with the Ides of March story initiating Bunny into the life.
The Ides of March, the opening case of The Amateur Cracksman, in which Bunny Manders first becomes Raffles's criminal accomplice in a jewellery theft.
A. J. Raffles, a celebrated amateur cricketer who is secretly a gentleman burglar, known as the 'amateur cracksman,' whose exploits are narrated by his accomplice Bunny Manders.
The Baskerville hound plot, the scheme in The Hound of the Baskervilles in which Jack Stapleton uses a phosphorus-painted hound to terrorise and kill the Baskerville heirs on Dartmoor.
Sir Henry Baskerville, the Canadian heir who arrives to claim the Baskerville estate on Dartmoor and becomes the target of Jack Stapleton's hound plot in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Jack Stapleton, the villain of The Hound of the Baskervilles who orchestrates the hound plot against Sir Henry Baskerville on the Dartmoor moor.
Baskerville Hall, the ancient Devonshire seat of the Baskerville family, near the treacherous Grimpen Mire, which is the setting for the hound plot in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Grimpen Mire, the treacherous boggy wasteland near Baskerville Hall on Dartmoor, where Stapleton hides the hound and eventually meets his end in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

The phosphorus paint applied to the body of a real hound to create the terrifying spectral appearance of the Baskerville curse beast, used by Stapleton to frighten and kill his victims in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

The Blue Cross theft plot, the scheme in the opening Father Brown story in which the criminal Flambeau impersonates a priest to steal Father Brown's Blue Cross relic, only to be outwitted through the priest's moral inference.
Flambeau, once the most famous criminal in France who later became a private detective in England after his reform, serving as Father Brown's ally across several Chesterton collections.
Aristide Valentin, the celebrated French detective who pursues Flambeau in The Blue Cross from The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Blue Cross, a valuable relic carried by Father Brown in the story of the same name and targeted for theft by the master criminal Flambeau.

Father Brown's method of moral inference, used in The Blue Cross to detect Flambeau's imposture by noticing that the supposed priest made statements logically absurd and contrary to rational Christian thought.
The central crime of The Red Thumb Mark, in which a blood thumb-print is forged to falsely implicate Reuben Hornby, investigated and disproved by Dr. Thorndyke.
Reuben Hornby, a man falsely accused on the basis of a forged red thumb-mark in Dr. Thorndyke's novel The Red Thumb Mark.

The bloody thumb print found at the diamond-theft scene in The Red Thumb Mark, proved by Dr. Thorndyke's laboratory reconstruction to be a forgery rather than the genuine impression of the accused Reuben Hornby.
Dr. Thorndyke's forensic comparison of finger-prints used to challenge the validity of prosecution evidence in The Red Thumb Mark.

Dr. Thorndyke's laboratory reconstruction method, applied in The Red Thumb Mark to demonstrate scientifically that the bloody thumb print used as evidence against Reuben Hornby was a manufactured forgery.
The Raffles jewellery theft, the inaugural burglary in The Ides of March that launches the Raffles series, in which A. J. Raffles steals jewellery from Albany to pay off Bunny's gambling debts.

Bunny Manders, the loyal but morally conflicted companion and narrator of A. J. Raffles's criminal escapades, who follows Raffles into burglary while acknowledging he could do little more than keep up with him.
The Albany, Raffles's fashionable bachelor chambers in Piccadilly that serve as his London base throughout the Raffles saga.
A. J. Raffles's burglary method of gaining social access to targets by attending their events over months — such as sitting behind Lady Lochmaben at first nights — before stealing their jewels.

Raffles's sporting theft motive, the philosophy articulated in The Amateur Cracksman by which A. J. Raffles views burglary as the only game that is always exciting and always full of danger, treating crime as a sport rather than a profession.
Arsène Lupin, a novelisation by Edgar Jepson and Maurice Leblanc in which Lupin operates under the identity of the Duke of Charmerace before being unmasked by Chief-Inspector Guerchard.

Maurice Leblanc, the French author who created Arsène Lupin and wrote or co-wrote all the major Lupin novels and story collections, including The Blonde Lady, The Hollow Needle, and The Crystal Stopper.
Edgar Jepson, co-adapter and co-author of the English novelization of Arsène Lupin, credited alongside Maurice Leblanc on the title page.

The Arsène Lupin saga, the body of French detective fiction by Maurice Leblanc featuring the gentleman-burglar Arsène Lupin across novels and story collections including The Extraordinary Adventures, The Blonde Lady, The Hollow Needle, and The Crystal Stopper.

Arsène Lupin's sustained impersonation of the deceased Duke of Charmerace, claiming his title, estates, and social standing while concealing his true identity as the escaped convict Lupin, in the Arsène Lupin novelization.
The real Duke of Charmerace, a deceased grand seigneur whose remarkable physical resemblance to Arsène Lupin prompts Lupin to usurp his identity three years after the Duke's death, in Arsène Lupin.

Chief-Inspector Guerchard, the head of the Detective Department of the Préfecture de Police in the Arsène Lupin novelisation, Lupin's sworn adversary who repeatedly fails to arrest him despite closing in on the Gournay-Martin burglary case.

Sonia Kritchnoff, a young Russian-born secretary employed by the Gournay-Martins in the Arsène Lupin novelisation, whose desperate poverty drives her to petty thefts and who eventually escapes under Guerchard's watch.
Germaine Gournay-Martin, heiress to the Gournay-Martin millions, who is announced as the bride of the Duke of Charmerace in the novelization Arsène Lupin.

M. Gournay-Martin, a big, flabby, red-faced wealthy Paris collector who owns treasures including the coronet of the Princesse de Lamballe and whose house is burgled by Arsène Lupin in the novelization Arsene Lupin.

Victoire, Arsène Lupin's devoted housekeeper who tends to him and keeps watch at his Paris lodgings, pacing the room and keeping out of sight of the windows in moments of danger, seen in the novelization Arsene Lupin.
M. Charolais, a persona adopted by Arsène Lupin — presented as a retired brewer and chevalier of the Legion of Honour from Rennes — in the novelization Arsène Lupin.
M. Formery, the plump and pink examining magistrate who investigates the Gournay-Martin house burglary and does not get on well with Guerchard, in the Arsène Lupin novelization.

Irma, a servant in the Gournay-Martin household in Lupin's novelization, who tells investigators she saw Sonia Kritchnoff standing by the bureau, implicating Sonia in the theft of items from the house.

Bernard Charolais, the third son of M. Charolais in Lupin's novelization, described by his father as destined for the Bar while Bernard himself surreptitiously grabs bric-a-brac from display cabinets, revealing the family's true criminal nature.

The Château de Charmerace in Ile-et-Vilaine, Brittany, the ancestral seat of the Dukes of Charmerace and the opening scene of Arsène Lupin, where Gournay-Martin's household receives Lupin's warning letter.

The wide-fronted Paris house of M. Gournay-Martin, where Lupin's burglars confine their attention to the two drawing-rooms and make their entry through a pierced chimney wall, in the Arsène Lupin novelization.
34B University Street, London, the charming ducal residence where Arsène Lupin lives in his persona as the Duke of Charmerace and where police come to arrest him in the Arsène Lupin novelization.
A neighbouring house in Arsène Lupin whose party wall Lupin's gang secretly breached to create a four-foot-square hidden exit, used to escape after the Gournay-Martin Paris burglary.
A jewelled coronet that had belonged to the Princesse de Lamballe, coveted and stolen by Arsène Lupin from the Gournay-Martin collection in his own novelization.

A pearl pendant presented by the Duke of Charmerace to Germaine Gournay-Martin in Lupin's novelization, reported stolen the same day—part of Lupin's systematic operation against the Gournay-Martin household.

Arsène Lupin's advance notice to M. Gournay-Martin in his own novelization, listing the specific pictures and objects he intends to steal from the Paris house and requesting they be packed and forwarded to him.

Lupin's mocking telegram in his own novelization, sent after the coronet theft and apologising for missing an appointment at the Acacias, the only communication he leaves behind regarding the stolen coronet.

An imitation coronet prepared in advance by Lupin at Charmerace in his own novelization, substituted for the real Lamballe coronet during the confusion of Guerchard's investigation so that only the fake was seized.
The Gournay-Martin collection in Lupin's novelization, comprising a Gainsborough painting, tapestries, cabinets, and a clock, all taken by Lupin from the Paris house.
Blue chalk 'Arsène Lupin' signatures written neatly on the walls of the Gournay-Martin Paris house, recovered by Guerchard as evidence linking the burglar to Lupin himself.
A single deliberate plaster footprint pressed onto the drawing-room carpet of the Gournay-Martin house in Arsène Lupin, planted by the burglars as a misleading false clue.
A spray of fresh pink salvias from Charmerace's hothouses, found on the stairs of the Gournay-Martin house in Arsène Lupin, linking the Duke (Lupin) to the burglary.
A gold-tipped Mercedes cigarette found at the burglary scene in Arsène Lupin, traced exclusively to the Duke of Charmerace's supply and linking him to the crime.
A piece of blue chalk discovered in Victoire's pocket by Guerchard in Arsène Lupin, used as proof that she was Lupin's accomplice who wrote the chalk signatures during the Gournay-Martin burglary.
The genuine death certificate of the real Duke of Charmerace, carried by Lupin as documentary insurance to prevent Guerchard from exposing his impersonation in Arsène Lupin.
The Gournay-Martin Paris house burglary, a theft attributed to Arsène Lupin in which the Gournay-Martin mansion is ransacked and Lupin's name is found written in blue chalk at the scene.

The Charolais gang's theft of two of Gournay-Martin's motor-cars at Château de Charmerace, tying up the servants before driving away, as part of Arsène Lupin's broader operation in the Arsène Lupin novelization.
Arsène Lupin's usurpation of the identity of the deceased Duke of Charmerace, enabled by a striking physical resemblance, as exposed by Chief-Inspector Guerchard in the novelization Arsene Lupin.

The theft of the Lamballe coronet, an episode in the Arsène Lupin novelisation in which Lupin, already having substituted a fake coronet at Charmerace, calmly retrieves the original from under Guerchard's distracted watch.
Sonia Kritchnoff's repeated petty thefts from the Gournay-Martin household in Arsène Lupin, driven by poverty, though she stops stealing after falling in love.

Arsène Lupin's pre-prepared technique of piercing a concealed opening in a chimney wall large enough to remove furniture, employed in the Gournay-Martin house burglary in the Arsène Lupin novelization.
Lupin's signature tactic of sending advance warning letters to intended victims before a theft in Arsène Lupin, a fondness Ganimard notes sometimes causes Lupin unexpected operational difficulties.

Lupin's method of total impersonation in Arsène Lupin, described by Ganimard as so complete that Lupin genuinely becomes the person he is pretending to be — thinking and feeling as that character — with no apparent limit to his transformations.

Arsène Lupin's ruse in the first Charmerace burglary, in which he disguised gang members as soldiers and had a motorist stand them drinks laced with a sedative, allowing the robbery to proceed while the guards slept.

Lupin's overwhelming love for Sonia Kritchnoff in his own novelization, which breaks his self-control and causes him to spring at Guerchard like a tiger when Guerchard threatens to use her as a lever against him.
Arsène Lupin's self-declared motive in his own novelization: contempt for wealthy society combined with the ambition that, since he cannot be a great artist or soldier, he will be a great thief.

Sonia Kritchnoff's motive in Lupin's novelization: extreme poverty in Paris—starvation in sight of bread in shop windows—initially driving her to steal to remain respectable, then to maintain the appearances she had established.

The Charmerace identity scheme investigation, the central thread of the Arsène Lupin novelisation in which Lupin operates for an extended period as the Duke of Charmerace before Chief-Inspector Guerchard finally unmasks him.

The sleight-of-hand swap of the Lamballe coronet performed by Lupin under Gournay-Martin's very eyes in Arsène Lupin, enabling Lupin to slip away undetected after substituting a fake for the real jewel.
The moment Lupin bitterly holds out his wrists to be handcuffed by Detective Guerchard in Arsène Lupin, after his Charmerace identity scheme is exposed.
Sonia Kritchnoff's escape from under Guerchard's nose in Arsène Lupin, engineered by the Duke who introduces her to an empty room and then secures her release from custody.

The revelation in the novelization Arsene Lupin that Arsène Lupin is truly the Duke of Charmerace, confirmed in the confrontation where Guerchard addresses him as 'Lupin' and he responds with the ambiguous bearing of the Duke.
In Arsène Lupin, Victoire is exposed as Lupin's accomplice when Guerchard finds blue chalk in her pocket — the same chalk used to sign 'Arsène Lupin' on the walls of the burglarised houses.
Arsène Lupin's role in his own novelization as France's most whimsical, audacious, and genial thief, who has kept the police at bay for ten years through disguise and impersonation.
The Blonde Lady, an Arsène Lupin story collection by Maurice Leblanc recording the duel of wits between Lupin and the English detective Herlock Sholmes over the blue diamond and related thefts.
Alexander Teixeira De Mattos, the English translator of several Arsène Lupin novels by Maurice Leblanc, including The Blonde Lady, The Crystal Stopper, and The Hollow Needle.
Wilson, the friend, confidant, and assistant to Herlock Sholmes (the Lupin series' parody of Watson) who accompanies him to France in The Blonde Lady.
The Blonde Lady, the mysterious fair-haired friend and accomplice of Arsène Lupin, later identified as Clotilde Destange, in The Blonde Lady.

Clotilde Destange, the eccentric daughter of the architect Destange in The Blonde Lady, who is revealed by Arsène Lupin's investigation to be the murderess of Baron d'Hautrec and the thief of the blue diamond.
Lucien Destange, a famous architect who uses his knowledge of secret passages in buildings of his own design to assist Arsène Lupin's operations, in The Blonde Lady.
M. Gerbois, a mathematics professor at Versailles College who discovers a lottery ticket at a second-hand dealer's that brings him into conflict with Arsène Lupin in The Blonde Lady.
Suzanne Gerbois, the daughter of Professor Gerbois who serves willingly as Arsène Lupin's hostage — carried off in a fair-haired lady's car — as part of the lottery ticket scheme in The Blonde Lady.
Baron d'Hautrec, a former French Ambassador found murdered in his home, next to whom the Blue Diamond is stolen in The Blonde Lady.
Maître Detinan, an influential radical deputy and man of the highest integrity in The Blonde Lady, who acts as Arsène Lupin's trusted legal adviser despite never having formally met him.
Alice Demun, the governess at the Imblevalle household who acts as an accomplice of Arsène Lupin and Bresson in The Blonde Lady.
The Maxime Bermond persona, an alias under which Arsène Lupin is warmly welcomed by M. Destange in The Blonde Lady, officially recorded in a police file linking Maxime Bermond to Lupin.

The Felix Davey persona, under which Arsène Lupin occupies a fourth-floor flat in The Blonde Lady, with the police believing Felix Davey to be merely one of Lupin's accomplices rather than Lupin himself.
The M. Stickmann persona used by Holmlock Shears in The Blonde Lady to gain entry to M. Destange's house posing as a secretary sent to continue architectural work.

The Blonde Lady Duel of Wits, the overarching case in The Blonde Lady collection in which Arsène Lupin matches wits against the English detective Herlock Sholmes over the blue diamond and Destange's secret files.
The theft of a small Jewish lamp from between a twelfth-century Virgin and a chased-silver tabernacle, carried out under the direction of Arsène Lupin in The Blonde Lady.

The Blonde Lady thefts, a series of crimes in The Blonde Lady engineered by Arsène Lupin through the mysterious Antoinette Brehat—the blonde lady—centred on the blue diamond once belonging to the French royal crown.

Arsène Lupin's extortion scheme in which he holds lottery ticket No. 514 Series 23 stolen from M. Gerbois's Napoleon writing-desk, demanding a share of the prize as the price of surrender, in The Blonde Lady.

The murder of Baron d'Hautrec at his Paris residence at 134 Avenue Henri-Martin, with a strand of golden hair found in his hand linking the crime to Clotilde Destange, as investigated in The Blonde Lady.

The Blue Diamond, a magnificent stone of enormous size and incomparable blue clarity that was once part of the French royal crown, stolen and used as a pawn in Arsène Lupin's schemes in The Blonde Lady.
Lottery ticket No. 514, Series 23 in 'The Blonde Lady', the winning ticket for one million francs in the Press-Association Lottery, which Lupin uses as the basis of an extortion scheme.
A yew-and-mahogany writing desk with acanthus-leaf capitals that had belonged to Marie Walewska in 'The Blonde Lady', recovered as part of the lottery-ticket extortion scheme.
An ancient Jewish oil lamp consisting of a stem and oil reservoir, a rare antique stolen by Lupin in the second episode of 'The Blonde Lady.'

An exact imitation of the real blue diamond, made in advance by Lupin and substituted in the Comtesse de Crozon's tooth-powder box in 'The Blonde Lady', so that the genuine stone could be quietly retained.
134 Avenue Henri-Martin, Paris, the house Baron d'Hautrec inherited from his brother six months before his murder, the crime scene in The Blonde Lady.
25 Rue Clapeyron, Paris, the address from which Suzanne Gerbois is taken hostage by the Blonde Lady in The Blonde Lady, separated from the Boulevard des Batignolles by a corner house.
Château de Crozon in 'The Blonde Lady', built by the architect Lucien Destange in 1877 and the setting of the blue diamond theft from the Comtesse de Crozon during a house party.
The Hôtel Destange at the corner of Place Malesherbes and Rue Montchanin in 'The Blonde Lady', the magnificent private house of architect Lucien Destange and a centre of Lupin's operations.
40 Rue Chalgrin, a narrow-fronted house near the Étoile in Paris in 'The Blonde Lady', visited on foot by Lupin and a companion during the investigation into Herlock Sholmes's network.
The yacht Hirondelle in The Blonde Lady, Lupin's private escape vessel that leaves the Seine estuary and enters the Channel by three o'clock after his second arrest and escape.

Lucien Destange's architectural method, deduced by Herlock Sholmes in The Blonde Lady, by which three houses built to similar plans by the same architect contain secret passages that enabled Arsène Lupin to commit seemingly impossible crimes.
A telephone impersonation trap in 'The Blonde Lady' in which a voice is used to lure a target away, a ruse that both Herlock Sholmes and Lupin recognise simultaneously when they exchange a glance.
Lupin's ruse in 'The Blonde Lady' of secretly advancing Herlock Sholmes's watch by one hour while sitting beside him, causing Sholmes to leave earlier than intended and miss a key event.
Lupin's motive for negotiating with Herlock Sholmes in 'The Blonde Lady': his love for Clotilde Destange, which causes him to put her liberty above his own safety when Sholmes threatens her.
The discovery in The Blonde Lady that all three crime-scene houses bear the 'Destange, architect' inscription, proving Lupin exploited Destange's hidden-passage designs across multiple properties.
The diamond recovered at the Château de Crozon in The Blonde Lady is an imitation produced by a new process of compressing diamond-dust under sufficient heat, with the real stone still hidden.
In The Blonde Lady, Herlock Sholmes arrests Lupin a second time, but Lupin snaps his handcuffs by sheer muscular effort and escapes before being taken to prison.

The kidnapping of Suzanne Gerbois from Versailles in a car driven by a fair-haired woman, taken as Arsène Lupin's hostage to force her father's compliance in the lottery ticket scheme in The Blonde Lady.

The first episode of 'The Blonde Lady', titled 'The Blonde Lady', opening with chapter I ('Number 514, Series 23') and tracing the lottery-ticket extortion scheme and Lupin's rivalry with Holmlock Shears.
The second episode of 'The Blonde Lady', titled 'The Jewish Lamp', the second major instalment of Lupin's contest with Holmlock Shears.

Arsène Lupin's criminal organisation in 'The Blonde Lady', a disciplined association of members who shadow their chief's movements as bodyguards, described as having boundless power under such a leader.
Red garnet stones from Clotilde Destange's brooch, found by Herlock Sholmes in Destange's cellar and confirmed by a matching box of a dozen similar garnets in The Blonde Lady.
A handful of golden hair clutched in Baron d'Hautrec's dead fingers in The Blonde Lady, identified as Mlle Antoinette Brehat's (Clotilde's) hair and used to implicate her in the murder.
File M.B. discovered by Herlock Sholmes in Destange's cupboard in The Blonde Lady, a fifteen-page dossier connecting Clotilde Destange to Lupin's hidden-passage operations.
The real blue diamond concealed inside the hollow knob of Lupin's walking cane in The Blonde Lady, revealed by unscrewing the knob and turning the iron ferrule.

The Crystal Stopper, an Arsène Lupin novel by Maurice Leblanc in which Lupin races against time to save his associate Gilbert from execution by recovering a crystal stopper concealing a blackmail list held by the corrupt deputy Daubrecq.

Daubrecq, a stout, bald deputy and master blackmailer in The Crystal Stopper who holds a list of twenty-seven corrupt politicians concealed within a crystal stopper, using it to extort immunity for himself.

Clarisse Mergy, the mother of Lupin's imprisoned associate Gilbert in The Crystal Stopper, a woman formerly known as Clarisse Darcel who allies with Lupin to recover the crystal stopper and save her son from execution.

Gilbert, a young member of Lupin's gang in The Crystal Stopper, later revealed to be the son of Clarisse Mergy under his real name Antoine Mergy, who faces execution at the Santé Prison and whose fate drives the novel's action.
Vaucheray, a saturnine, bitter, and unscrupulous older member of Lupin's gang in The Crystal Stopper who participates in the Villa Marie-Thérèse burglary and is condemned alongside Gilbert.

Prasville, the famous Prefect of Police in The Crystal Stopper who becomes entangled in the crystal stopper affair, having had a personal encounter with the deputy Daubrecq two years earlier on the Place du Palais-Bourbon.

The Marquis d'Albufex, a Bonapartist deputy and former political agent for Prince Napoleon in The Crystal Stopper who is arrested for complicity in the Two-Seas Canal Company bribery affair and subsequently opens his veins in his cell at the Santé.
Léonard, Daubrecq the deputy's manservant in The Crystal Stopper, found lying on his back with a dagger in his throat after being killed by Vaucheray and Gilbert during the burglary.
The Growler, one of Arsène Lupin's two gang oarsmen who rows to the opposite bank during an operation, responding to Lupin's call and named alongside The Masher in The Crystal Stopper.
The Masher, one of Arsène Lupin's two gang oarsmen who goes to the gate on the Avenue de Ceinture during an operation, named alongside The Growler in The Crystal Stopper.

Sébastiani, a Corsican huntsman recommended to the Duc de Montmaur by the Marquis d'Albufex and living a few miles from the chateau, investigated in connection with the Marquis in The Crystal Stopper.
Jacques Mergy, Clarisse Mergy's young son of six or seven in 'The Crystal Stopper', placed in an easy-chair by Lupin and unwittingly instrumental in delivering a key object to his mother.

Langeroux, a deputy and leader of the independent left in The Crystal Stopper, a poor man with a large family who visited Daubrecq the night before and is numbered among The Twenty-Seven compromised by the canal company bribery affair.

Victorien Mergy, a French deputy whose name appears on Daubrecq's list of the Twenty-seven implicated in the Two-Seas Canal bribery scandal, who shot himself in the lobby of the Chamber of Deputies three years before the events of The Crystal Stopper.

Stanislas Vorenglade, a man in 'The Crystal Stopper' who, tired of living in poverty, attempts to extort money from Prasville by threatening to reveal that Prasville's name is connected via Vorenglade himself to the Canal affair.

Clémence, the portress at Daubrecq's address in the Square Lamartine in 'The Crystal Stopper', entirely devoted to Lupin and placed there by him to provide access and intelligence on Daubrecq's movements.
Achille, the manservant who looked after Arsène Lupin's flat in Paris under the name Michel Beaumont, as seen in The Crystal Stopper.
The Michel Beaumont persona, under which Arsène Lupin keeps a flat in the Rue Chateaubriand in The Crystal Stopper, looked after by his manservant Achille.
The M. Nicole persona, in which Arsène Lupin poses as a Master of Arts and private tutor living at 25 Place de Clichy in The Crystal Stopper, introduced to the household as young Jacques's tutor.
The Dr. Vernes persona, a disguise used by Arsène Lupin in The Crystal Stopper, presenting himself as a stout elderly physician from Saint-Germain to gain access to Daubrecq the deputy.
A hollow crystal decanter stopper used by Daubrecq to conceal the list of the Twenty-seven, its gilded knob hiding a rolled piece of paper inside, central to the plot of The Crystal Stopper.

Daubrecq's artificial glass eye in 'The Crystal Stopper', revealed to be hollow and containing a tiny ball of paper on which the Twenty-seven list is recorded, hidden in plain sight in his eye socket.

The hidden list, the document at the centre of The Crystal Stopper containing the twenty-seven names of politicians bribed in the Two-Seas Canal Company scandal, which Daubrecq conceals within a crystal stopper to use as a blackmail instrument.

An unopened sealed packet of Maryland tobacco—the only brand Daubrecq smokes—used in 'The Crystal Stopper' to conceal the crystal stopper, inserted by loosening the government revenue band and re-sealing the packet.
A decoy crystal stopper in The Crystal Stopper — an exact replica of Daubrecq's original, identifiable by a scratch at the bottom of the stem but containing no hidden list.
A crumpled draft letter found under Daubrecq's writing table in The Crystal Stopper, revealing his plan to hollow out a crystal stopper to conceal the list of the Twenty-seven.

A torn invoice half found by Lupin in the ashes of Daubrecq's fireplace in 'The Crystal Stopper', its Stourbridge origin indicating that the crystal stopper was made in that English glass-blowing town.
A Cross of Lorraine watermark on the letter-paper on which the list of the Twenty-seven was written, identified by Lupin as a key link in The Crystal Stopper.

A small ivory fragment found near the Marquis d'Albufex in The Crystal Stopper, whose knob — viewed at the right angle — reveals the profile of Napoleon, linking d'Albufex to the missing crystal stopper.

The crystal stopper case, the central mystery of Leblanc's novel The Crystal Stopper in which Lupin seeks to recover the glass stopper that contains Daubrecq's blackmail list of twenty-seven corrupt politicians in order to save Gilbert from the guillotine.

The burglary of Villa Marie-Thérèse at Enghien and the murder of the manservant Léonard — stabbed with a dagger — carried out by Vaucheray as part of Arsène Lupin's gang's operations, central to The Crystal Stopper.

Daubrecq's systematic blackmail of twenty-seven French deputies and officials implicated in the Two-Seas Canal bribery scandal, using a list concealed in a hollow crystal stopper to extort money, the central plot of The Crystal Stopper.

Daubrecq's murder of an opera-singer in The Crystal Stopper, in which Daubrecq killed the woman on the very day she married Louis Prasville — having persecuted her with his attentions for some days beforehand.
A bribery scheme involving twenty-seven deputies and officials of the Two-Seas Canal Company, whose names Daubrecq uses for blackmail in The Crystal Stopper.

The Marquis d'Albufex's kidnapping and torture of Daubrecq, who is chained to a truckle-bed in Château de Mortepierre's Lovers' Tower and subjected to rack, to extract the list of deputies in The Crystal Stopper.
Arsène Lupin's sniper shots fired from a house opposite the Boulevard Arago scaffold, mortally wounding Vaucheray to prevent disclosure on the day of execution in The Crystal Stopper.

Clarisse Mergy's sole declared motive throughout 'The Crystal Stopper': revenge against Daubrecq for destroying her husband and corrupting her son Jacques, which she describes as the preoccupation of her entire life.

Lupin's declared primary objective in 'The Crystal Stopper': to free his loyal accomplice Gilbert from the death sentence, driven by personal affection and his conviction that Gilbert was fundamentally an honest man.
Daubrecq's twin motives in 'The Crystal Stopper' of political power over the Twenty-seven deputies and personal revenge against Clarisse for her rejection of him more than twenty years earlier.

A break-in technique used by Vaucheray at Daubrecq's instruction in 'The Crystal Stopper', involving panels cut out of a door to allow an acrobat to slip through in preparation for the contest against Daubrecq.

Lupin's bare-hands cliff ascent to reach the Mortepierre Lovers' Tower in The Crystal Stopper, climbing first by ladder and then over rough rock with bruised nails and bleeding fingers to free a prisoner.
Lupin's technique of overpowering Daubrecq with chloroform at a Nice hotel in The Crystal Stopper, administering small doses every few hours to exploit Daubrecq's known weakness to the drug.

Daubrecq's concealment method in 'The Crystal Stopper' of hiding the crystal stopper inside a sealed packet of Maryland tobacco—his exclusive brand—by loosening the government revenue band and re-inserting it with the stopper inside.

Daubrecq's method of concealing the Twenty-seven list inside a hollow glass eye worn in place of a missing natural eye, as revealed in 'The Crystal Stopper' when Lupin deduces the list cannot exist away from Daubrecq's person.
The night-time raid on Villa Marie-Thérèse at Enghien that opens 'The Crystal Stopper', conducted from two boats moored at the garden pier while lights flicker in the darkened house.
The arrest of Gilbert and Vaucheray in 'The Crystal Stopper', the first time the authorities held declared accomplices of Lupin in custody, seizing Gilbert immediately and binding him firmly.

The assize-court verdict in 'The Crystal Stopper' in which the jury, after long deliberation, sentences both Gilbert and Vaucheray to death, the president asking each in turn whether they have anything more to say.
Lupin's cliff-top action at Mortepierre in 'The Crystal Stopper', in which he saves a prisoner from the drop but is immediately stabbed in the shoulder by Daubrecq and collapses to the ground.
Daubrecq's suicide at the Gare du Nord in 'The Crystal Stopper', shooting himself in the head within fifty steps of leaving Lupin, as he had no escape and no further power.

Two months after his death sentence was commuted to penal servitude for life, Gilbert escapes from the penal colony at Île de Ré in 'The Crystal Stopper', the fulfilment of Lupin's long campaign to save him.
In The Crystal Stopper, Daubrecq spent six months disguised as dying Germineaux's secretary in order to steal the list of the Twenty-seven from him.
Gilbert, held in the Santé in The Crystal Stopper, is in truth Antoine Mergy, a young man who concealed his real name and eventually returned to farm land in Algeria under it.

The disclosure in 'The Crystal Stopper' that Daubrecq secretly lured Gilbert away from school and led him into a criminal life as deliberate revenge against Clarisse, acting on the day after her husband expelled the boy.
The discovery that the list of deputies found in the tobacco packet is a decoy forgery and that Daubrecq still holds the real list he originally stole, revealed in The Crystal Stopper.
The revelation in 'The Crystal Stopper' that Prasville is implicated in the Canal affair not by direct entry on the Twenty-seven list but through Vorenglade, who uses this knowledge to extort him.
Villa Marie-Thérèse at Enghien in The Crystal Stopper, Daubrecq's country house burglarised by Lupin's gang at the story's opening and the scene of a murder discovered in the pantry.
Square Lamartine, Paris, Daubrecq's winter address in The Crystal Stopper, to which he moved immediately after Lupin's gang burglarised his Villa Marie-Thérèse at Enghien.

Château de Mortepierre in The Crystal Stopper, a fortress on a loop of the river Ligier whose dungeon, known locally as the Lovers' Tower, serves as the Marquis d'Albufex's prison for the kidnapped Daubrecq.
The Santé Prison, Paris, where Gilbert is held awaiting execution in The Crystal Stopper, with all communication between him and Lupin strictly cut off by the prison and court authorities.

A hotel on the hills above Nice between the Vallon de Saint-Silvestre and the surrounding countryside in 'The Crystal Stopper', Daubrecq's final retreat, where Clarisse takes room No. 130 on the second floor to surveil him.
A flat in the Rue Chateaubriand, Paris, one of Lupin's most frequently used residences in The Crystal Stopper, where he operates under the alias Michel Beaumont.

The Boulevard Arago scaffold site in Paris in The Crystal Stopper, the public square outside the prison walls where Gilbert's execution is scheduled and from which Lupin fires sniper shots to disrupt it.
The unnamed narrator of 'The Crystal Stopper', Lupin's confidant, who records the story from Lupin's own account long after the events and attests to the trust Lupin placed in him.
Arsène Lupin's criminal gang, whose members include Gilbert, The Growler, The Masher, and Vaucheray, described in The Crystal Stopper as held together by countless acts of devotion.

The Twenty-seven, the network of twenty-seven French deputies and officials who accepted bribes in the Two-Seas Canal Company scandal and whose names Daubrecq holds on a hidden list as the instrument of his blackmail in The Crystal Stopper.
The Hollow Needle, an Arsène Lupin novel by Maurice Leblanc in which Lupin's secret royal treasure hidden inside the hollow sea-stack at Étretat is uncovered by young detective Isidore Beautrelet.

Isidore Beautrelet, a sixth-form pupil at the Lycée Janson-de-Sailly who applies a hypothetico-deductive method to unmask Arsène Lupin and ultimately discover the secret of the Hollow Needle at Étretat.

Raymonde de Saint-Véran, the niece of the Comte de Gesvres who had lived at the Château d'Ambrumésy for two years, who fires a shot at the intruders the night of the murder and later becomes the object of Arsène Lupin's obsessive love in The Hollow Needle.
Comte de Gesvres, the owner of Château d'Ambrumésy for some twenty years who lives there with his daughter Suzanne, at the scene of the burglary and murder in The Hollow Needle.
Suzanne de Gesvres, the delicate, fair-haired daughter of the Count of Gesvres, who nurses the wounded Isidore Beautrelet for nights on end in The Hollow Needle.
Jean Daval, the private secretary of the Comte de Gesvres at the Château d'Ambrumésy, whose dead body is discovered by Raymonde de Saint-Véran during the burglary that opens The Hollow Needle.
M. Filleul, the ironic Examining Magistrate who investigates the crime scene in The Hollow Needle, noting that the drawing room was not the object of the theft.
Brédoux, a misshapen clerk to the examining magistrate who secretly serves as an accomplice of Arsène Lupin in The Hollow Needle.
Ephraim B. Harlington, the alias used by the secretary of the American millionaire Cooley, who corresponds with characters in The Hollow Needle.
Louis Valméras, residing at 34 Rue du Mont-Thabor, who is revealed by Isidore Beautrelet to be none other than Arsène Lupin operating under a false identity in The Hollow Needle.
M. Massiban, a member of the Academy of Inscriptions and Belles-Lettres whose identity and historic book are appropriated by Arsène Lupin as a persona in The Hollow Needle.
Dr. Delattre, a well-known surgeon who is abducted and compelled to perform an emergency operation — used by Arsène Lupin for his own purposes — in The Hollow Needle.
The Étienne de Vaudreix persona, under which Arsène Lupin lived in Paris for twelve months in The Hollow Needle before being unmasked.
The Baron Anfredi persona, an Italian identity adopted by Arsène Lupin to rent a property for the summer season in The Hollow Needle, suspected by Isidore Beautrelet to be Lupin himself.

The Louis Valméras persona, a childhood identity resumed by Arsène Lupin in The Hollow Needle, so convincing that even when confronted, Beautrelet finds himself forced to accept that the man before him is Valméras.
The Massiban impostor persona used by Arsène Lupin in The Hollow Needle to impersonate the Academy scholar and gain possession of the historic book containing the secret of the Hollow Needle.
The Hollow Needle secret, the central mystery of the Lupin novel of the same name, revealing that the sea-stack at Étretat is hollow and has served for centuries as a French royal treasure vault.

The Ambrumésy burglary and murder of Jean Daval, the opening crime in The Hollow Needle in which Arsène Lupin commits a daring burglary at the Château d'Ambrumésy and Jean Daval is murdered, drawing Isidore Beautrelet into the investigation.
Arsène Lupin's substitution of four counterfeit Rubens canvases for the genuine paintings at Château d'Ambrumésy, executed by a painter named Charpenais, in The Hollow Needle.
Arsène Lupin's audacious theft of an entire Gothic chapel at Château d'Ambrumésy, removed stone by stone with its population of statues replaced by stucco replicas, in The Hollow Needle.

The abduction of Raymonde de Saint-Véran from the Château d'Ambrumésy by Arsène Lupin, driven by desperate grief after her disappearance and his obsessive love for her, described in The Hollow Needle.

The simultaneous kidnapping of Inspector Ganimard and the English detective Holmlock Shears (Herlock Sholmes) by Arsène Lupin, reported in newspaper announcements alongside the Château d'Ambrumésy events in The Hollow Needle.
An episode in 'The Hollow Needle' in which Lupin, shot and half-naked, lies as a motionless form on the floor of an abbey crypt where Beautrelet discovers him hidden.
The moment student detective Isidore Beautrelet discovers that the Étretat Needle is hollow, finding a thread of smoke rising from a crevice in the clifftop in The Hollow Needle.
Inspector Ganimard's armed assault on the Hollow Needle of Étretat in The Hollow Needle, during which he confronts Lupin at gunpoint inside the secret cavern.

The Château d'Ambrumésy, a famous medieval Norman monastery restored as an aristocratic estate belonging to the Comte de Gesvres, where the burglary, the murder of Jean Daval, and the abduction of Raymonde de Saint-Véran take place at the opening of The Hollow Needle.

The Aiguille d'Étretat (Hollow Needle), the colossal sea-stack over eighty yards high off the Normandy coast that is revealed in Leblanc's The Hollow Needle to be hollow and to contain a royal treasure vault.
The Chambre des Demoiselles, a coastal cave at Étretat known to local folk, which conceals the carved D and F letters pointing to the location of the Hollow Needle in The Hollow Needle.

Fort de Fréfossé, a ruined clifftop fortification at Étretat in The Hollow Needle, whose initial 'F' paired with 'D for Demoiselles' forms part of Beautrelet's geographic triangulation pointing toward the Needle.
Château de l'Aiguille on the Creuse in 'The Hollow Needle', built and named by Louis XIV as a deliberate decoy to mislead seekers of the real Hollow Needle, the ancient royal treasure cache.
Étretat, the Norman coastal town near the Aiguille Creuse sea-stack, which Isidore Beautrelet investigates to find Arsène Lupin's secret royal treasure refuge in The Hollow Needle.
The Lycée Janson-de-Sailly in Paris, the sixth-form school attended by Isidore Beautrelet in 'The Hollow Needle', where classmates nickname him the rival of Holmlock Shears.

The secret of the Aiguille d'Étretat—a hollow sea-stack off the Normandy coast serving as a royal refuge and treasure-house accumulated by French kings over centuries—bequeathed by Arsène Lupin to France at the end of The Hollow Needle.
A folded paper covered in figures, dots, and signs found at Étretat, which encodes the secret of the Hollow Needle and is the key document pursued by Beautrelet and Lupin in The Hollow Needle.

A soft buff leather chauffeur's cap found near the Ambrumésy estate in 'The Hollow Needle', the only real piece of physical evidence from the crime scene, lost when it is inadvertently carried away on the head of an impersonator.

Four paintings by the copyist Charpenais hung in place of stolen originals at Ambrumésy in 'The Hollow Needle', their canvases and frames faithfully duplicating the Rubens originals, detected by Beautrelet as fakes.

Marie Antoinette's book of hours, displayed at the Musée Carnavalet, which contains a hidden sheet bearing the queen's signature and the words 'Arsène Lupin', pointing Beautrelet toward the Hollow Needle secret.

A 1679 privately printed pamphlet titled The Mystery of the Hollow Needle in The Hollow Needle, one of a hundred copies condemned by Louis XIV, which reveals the secret of the Étretat Needle to Beautrelet.

The carved letters D and F — standing for Demoiselles and Fréfossé — found in the granite floor of the Chambre des Demoiselles cave, confirming Beautrelet's geographic triangulation of the Hollow Needle's location.
In The Hollow Needle, Lupin has made the Hollow Needle of Étretat his personal stronghold, the latest in a line of possessors stretching from Julius Caesar through the Kings of France to himself.

The secret of the Hollow Needle was transmitted in succession through the Kings of England and then of France — from Julius Caesar through Charlemagne, William the Conqueror, Richard Coeur-de-Lion, Louis XI, Francis I, Henry IV, and Napoleon — until it reached Lupin.
Beautrelet's hypothetico-deductive method in The Hollow Needle: first grasp the general hang of a case, then form a reasonable hypothesis, then test it without mercy against every available fact.

Beautrelet's vowel-substitution decryption of the Étretat cryptogram — noting that no figure exceeds 5 — to produce the French text directing searchers to the Chambre des Demoiselles and the Hollow Needle in The Hollow Needle.

Beautrelet's triangulation placing the Hollow Needle in the old kingdom of Neustria using Rouen, Dieppe, and Le Havre as the three points of an enclosing triangle, narrowing the search to the Étretat coast in The Hollow Needle.

Lupin's love for Raymonde de Saint-Véran in 'The Hollow Needle', which he openly admits while acknowledging the mortification of not being loved in return, having fallen for her during his convalescence under her nursing care.
Jean Daval's motive for turning thief in 'The Hollow Needle': financial greed and a love affair with Madame Verdier, whose constant need for money drove him to steal from the Ambrumésy estate.

Isidore Beautrelet's role in 'The Hollow Needle' as a schoolboy amateur detective whose classmates call him the rival of Holmlock Shears and who single-handedly tracks Arsène Lupin to the Hollow Needle.
John Thorndyke's Cases, a short-story collection by R. Austin Freeman narrated by Dr. Christopher Jervis, in which Dr. Thorndyke solves crimes through forensic science and microscopic analysis.

R. Austin Freeman, the British author who created Dr. John Thorndyke and pioneered the forensic detective story, authoring John Thorndyke's Cases, The Red Thumb Mark, The Vanishing Man, and The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke.
The Dr. Thorndyke saga, a series of medico-legal mystery fiction featuring Dr. John Thorndyke, including John Thorndyke's Cases, The Red Thumb Mark, and The Vanishing Man, by R. Austin Freeman.
Dr. Christopher Jervis, a medical doctor and Dr. Thorndyke's companion and narrative voice in John Thorndyke's Cases and other Freeman works, who assists Thorndyke in his forensic investigations.

Polton, Dr. Thorndyke's deft and resourceful laboratory assistant who appears across John Thorndyke's Cases and other Freeman works, maintaining and operating the scientific apparatus such as Marsh's arsenic-detection device.

Dr. Christopher Jervis's narrator role, in which he relates the cases of Dr. John Thorndyke in John Thorndyke's Cases and The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke, serving as Thorndyke's assistant and chronicler.
The Man with the Nailed Shoes, the opening story of John Thorndyke's Cases, set in the remote East Coast village of Little Sudbury and centring on the Charles Hearn murder investigation.
The Stranger's Latchkey, a Dr. Thorndyke story from John Thorndyke's Cases in which Thorndyke investigates the Freddy Haldean abduction case after taking up his legal practice.
The Anthropologist at Large, a Dr. Thorndyke story from John Thorndyke's Cases in which Thorndyke applies anthropometric methods to solve the Isaac Löwe Japanese art robbery.
The Blue Sequin, a story in John Thorndyke's Cases in which Dr. Thorndyke investigates the death of Edith Grant at a railway platform, applying his laboratory methods to resolve the case.
The Moabite Cipher, a story in John Thorndyke's Cases in which Dr. Thorndyke deciphers a Moabite script cipher and uses Marsh's apparatus arsenic test to resolve a Piccadilly jewel theft.
The Mandarin's Pearl, a Dr. Thorndyke story from John Thorndyke's Cases in which the solicitor Mr. Brodribb consults Thorndyke about the suspicious death of Fred Calverley.
The Aluminium Dagger, a Dr. Thorndyke story from John Thorndyke's Cases in which Thorndyke investigates the murder of Alfred Hartridge.
A Message from the Deep Sea, a Dr. Thorndyke story from John Thorndyke's Cases set in the Whitechapel Road area and centring on the Minna Adler murder investigation.

The Charles Hearn murder investigation, a Dr. Thorndyke case in The Man with the Nailed Shoes in which Hearn is found dead from a morphia poisoning and a stab wound, initially attributed to Alfred Draper, with Thorndyke exposing the Jezzard gang's staged frame-up.

The Freddy Haldean abduction, a Dr. Thorndyke case in The Stranger's Latchkey in which a child is abducted by Percy Haldean from an upper-window vantage point, with Thorndyke identifying the culprit through microscopic analysis of hair and dust.

The Isaac Löwe Japanese art robbery, a Dr. Thorndyke case known as The Anthropologist at Large in which Thorndyke identifies the Japanese burglar Futashima through transverse cross-section hair analysis and skull-shape anthropometry.

The Edith Grant death investigation, a Dr. Thorndyke case known as The Blue Sequin in which Thorndyke proves the fashionably-dressed woman found dead at Woldhurst was killed by a steer horn projectile, exonerating Harold Stopford.

The Moabite cipher and Piccadilly jewel theft, a Dr. Thorndyke case in John Thorndyke's Cases in which Thorndyke deciphers a primitive Semitic inscription that conceals the location of stolen goods, revealed by water-based secret writing.

The Fred Calverley suicide and Mandarin's Pearl conspiracy, in which Dr. Thorndyke proves that Captain Raggerton and Alfred Calverley conspired to drive Fred Calverley to suicide by fabricating the curse of the Mandarin's Pearl.

The Alfred Hartridge murder investigation, a Dr. Thorndyke case known as The Aluminium Dagger in which Thorndyke demonstrates through ballistic analysis that the victim was actually shot by Leonard Wolfe using a powerful compressed-air rifle disguised as an aluminium dagger.

The Minna Adler murder, a Dr. Thorndyke case in John Thorndyke's Cases in which the throat-slashing death of a young German woman in Whitechapel is solved through analysis of deep-sea Foraminifera sand on the pillow and planted hair.

A forensic method used by Dr. Thorndyke in 'The Man with the Nailed Shoes' to determine identity and gait from footprint evidence, noting that enormous-looking prints were made by a person of normal stride.
Plaster-of-Paris footprint casting technique used by Dr. Thorndyke in The Man with the Nailed Shoes to take direct impressions from the shore and produce cast facsimiles as evidence.

Dr. Thorndyke's forensic technique in 'The Man with the Nailed Shoes' of superimposing photographic transparencies of shoe moulds to show that the number and pattern of nails in two shoes are identical, proving the same person made both sets of prints.
A thorough post-mortem examination conducted by Dr. Thorndyke in The Man with the Nailed Shoes, weighing the body and examining all organs to avoid confirming a predetermined hypothesis.
Microscopic analysis of hair and dust used by Dr. Thorndyke to identify unusually thick hairs and trace evidential properties of dust, as demonstrated in the Freddy Haldean abduction case.

A forensic technique employed by Dr. Thorndyke's assistant Polton in 'The Anthropologist at Large' using a patent dust-extractor of his own manufacture to collect and analyse powder specimens for evidence.

Skull shape anthropometry using callipers to measure cephalic index, employed by Dr. Thorndyke in The Anthropologist at Large to identify the racial origin of a hat's owner in the Isaac Löwe Japanese art robbery case.

Thorndyke's transverse microscopic cross-section of hair fibres to distinguish racial types by the circular or oval shape of the section, applied in the Isaac Löwe Japanese art robbery and the Minna Adler murder cases.
Fingerprint development using a powder insufflator, employed by Dr. Thorndyke in multiple cases including the Edith Grant death investigation, to reveal latent prints on surfaces.
Marsh's apparatus arsenic test, a forensic chemistry technique used by Dr. Thorndyke in The Moabite Cipher to detect arsenic via a black stain that fades when treated with hypochlorite solution.
A technique used by Dr. Thorndyke in The Moabite Cipher, revealing hidden writing on a document by immersing it in water, causing the secret message to appear and then vanish again on drying.

Thorndyke's identification of deep-sea foraminifera sand from the Eastern Mediterranean floor, traced to Turkey-sponge warehouses, as a clue localising a suspect in the Minna Adler murder in A Message from the Deep Sea.
Fingerprint casting from candle-wax impressions used by Dr. Thorndyke in A Message from the Deep Sea, taking three moulds from clear right thumb and forefinger impressions left in warm wax.

Thorndyke's demonstration that an aluminium dagger was fired from a Chassepôt rifle by matching the linear rifling scratches on its hilt to the grooves of the barrel, solving the murder of Alfred Hartridge in The Aluminium Dagger.

Alfred Draper, the alias assumed by a discharged convict who changed his name and sought a quiet retreat in The Man with the Nailed Shoes, becoming entangled in Jezzard's gang's staged murder of Hearn.
Arthur Jezzard, the leader of the gang that staged the murder of Hearn in The Man with the Nailed Shoes, whose own body was later found washed up on the beach.

Percy Haldean, the brother-in-law identified by the inspector in The Stranger's Latchkey as the man who abducted Freddy for inheritance reasons, recognised by description down to his stick and pocket-comb.
Futashima, a Japanese burglar living at 13 Birket Street, Limehouse, and employed at Badcomb and Martin's, who is identified by Dr. Thorndyke as responsible for the Isaac Löwe Japanese art robbery.
Harold Stopford, an Associate of the Royal Academy and a painter whose brother is suspected in the Edith Grant death investigation in The Blue Sequin.

Fred Calverley, a vulnerable young man shattered by a railway accident and manipulated by his companion Raggerton in The Mandarin's Pearl, who leaves a farewell note indicating he has been driven to suicide.

Captain Raggerton, a scheming associate of Alfred Calverley in The Mandarin's Pearl who insinuated himself into the life of the vulnerable Fred Calverley and was clearly the leading spirit in a plot to exploit him.

Leonard Wolfe (alias Thomas Barlow), the sole beneficiary of Alfred Hartridge's £20,000 estate in The Aluminium Dagger, who was arrested at Eltham while burying in his garden the large compressed-air rifle used in the remote-control murder.
Paul Petrofsky, the murderer of Minna Adler in A Message from the Deep Sea, whose fingerprints on a candle-wax impression are matched by Dr. Thorndyke to those taken when Petrofsky was arrested.

Inspector Badger, a Scotland Yard detective who trails suspects near Rathbone Place and challenges access to crime scenes, encountered by Dr. Thorndyke in The Moabite Cipher and the John Bellingham disappearance in The Vanishing Man.

Superintendent Miller of Scotland Yard, who visits Dr. Thorndyke to consult on multiple cases and assists in the arrest of suspects on Thorndyke's direction, appearing in The Moabite Cipher and A Message from the Deep Sea among others.
Professor Poppelbaum, a British Museum scholar consulted to decipher the Moabite cipher in the Dr. Thorndyke story of that name, who introduces himself to Thorndyke and Jervis when they meet.

The Jezzard gang's staged murder of Hearn to frame Draper, a Dr. Thorndyke case in The Man with the Nailed Shoes in which the gang administers morphia and then frames Alfred Draper by placing specially nailed shoes on the corpse.
Percy Haldean's abduction of his young nephew Freddy from a London house, watching him from an upper window and spiriting him away in a cab to alter the inheritance in The Stranger's Latchkey.

Captain Raggerton and Alfred Calverley's conspiracy to drive Fred Calverley to suicide by fabricating the story of a murdered mandarin and stolen pearl, uncovered by Thorndyke in The Mandarin's Pearl.

Leonard Wolfe's remote murder of Alfred Hartridge using a compressed-air rifle fired through a window, with an aluminium-hilted dagger left as a misleading clue, as revealed by Thorndyke in The Aluminium Dagger.

Paul Petrofsky's premeditated murder of Minna Adler, in which he planted a shed hair tress in the dead woman's hand to mislead investigators, as resolved by Dr. Thorndyke in A Message from the Deep Sea.

A pair of shoes with hob-nails arranged in a diamond-and-cross pattern in 'The Man with the Nailed Shoes', the key forensic object linking a murderer to footprints at the crime scene through Thorndyke's photographic superposition technique.

A singular weapon in 'The Aluminium Dagger' with an aluminium hilt—a material never previously seen by the investigators—engraved with the word TRADITORE, linked to the remote-rifle murder of Alfred Hartridge by Leonard Wolfe.

A half-sheet of notepaper covered on both sides with Moabite or Phoenician characters reading from right to left in 'The Moabite Cipher', used to encode instructions for a Piccadilly jewel theft, deciphered by Dr. Thorndyke.

An ebony pendant set with a single pear-shaped pearl over three-quarters of an inch long in 'The Mandarin's Pearl', pronounced genuine and worth up to a thousand pounds, which is the object of Alfred Calverley and Raggerton's conspiracy.

A tiny blue sequin from Edith Grant's hat recovered from the bloodstained tip of a steer's horn in 'The Blue Sequin', constituting the key physical evidence that Dr. Thorndyke uses to reconstruct the cause of her death.

The left horn of a steer in 'The Blue Sequin' that accidentally struck and killed Edith Grant, bearing dried blood, brain tissue, and an embedded blue sequin from her hat—the physical evidence reconstructed by Dr. Thorndyke.
Deep-sea Foraminifera sand from the Eastern Mediterranean found on a victim's pillow in A Message from the Deep Sea, traced to Turkey sponges to identify the murderer's trade.
A tress of shed hair with roots at both ends placed in Minna Adler's dead hand in A Message from the Deep Sea, proven by Thorndyke to be planted combings rather than hair torn from the murderer.
A soft-metal washer bearing the rifling marks of the aluminium dagger's barrel, crafted by Polton and used by Thorndyke to prove the dagger was also a firearm in The Aluminium Dagger.
Morphia found in Charles Hearn's stomach at post-mortem, confirmed by Thorndyke in The Man with the Nailed Shoes as the cause of death, with the poison traced to a local pharmacist.

Little Sundersley, a particularly remote village on the East Coast of England in The Man with the Nailed Shoes in John Thorndyke's Cases, the isolated setting of Thorndyke's morphia poisoning investigation.

Woldhurst, a small Kent town on a branch railway line in The Blue Sequin in John Thorndyke's Cases, where Edith Grant's death is initially treated as murder by Stopford before Thorndyke proves it was a cattle-truck accident.
The Inner Temple, London, location of Thorndyke's chambers, whose soft-voiced bell opens several of his cases including The Aluminium Dagger in John Thorndyke's Cases.
The Whitechapel Road area of East London in A Message from the Deep Sea in John Thorndyke's Cases, the neighbourhood where Minna Adler is murdered near the London Hospital.

The motive of the Jezzard gang in 'The Man with the Nailed Shoes': silencing Hearn as a potential informant by staging his murder as an accident, in exchange for Hearn's promise of a share of future criminal proceeds.

Leonard Wolfe's motive in 'The Aluminium Dagger': under an agreement with Alfred Hartridge, Wolfe was to marry Hester Greene and inherit the Hartridge estate, making Hartridge's death the only way to secure the inheritance.

The motive behind Paul Petrofsky's murder of Minna Adler in 'A Message from the Deep Sea': Miriam's furious jealousy after Moses broke off his engagement to her in favour of Minna, a passion so intense it led to violence.

The forensic finding that the chest wound inflicted on Hearn was post-mortem — the blood-clot and absence of vital reaction proving he had already been dead from morphia for a quarter to half an hour before being stabbed, in The Man with the Nailed Shoes.

In The Blue Sequin, Thorndyke demonstrates that Edith Grant was accidentally killed by a steer horn projecting from a passing cattle truck, the only hypothesis consistent with all the physical evidence, exonerating Harold Stopford of murder.

Thorndyke proves in A Message from the Deep Sea that the tress of hair in Minna Adler's hand was planted after death — it lay loosely in a rigid palm and had roots at both ends, indicating fallen combings rather than hair torn from a killer.

The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke (originally published as The Singing Bone), an R. Austin Freeman short-story collection featuring cases including the Oscar Brodski murder and the Girdler lighthouse disappearance.

The Case of Oscar Brodski, the opening story of The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke and Freeman's experiment in the inverted detective narrative, in which Thorndyke reconstructs the murder of the diamond merchant Oscar Brodski by Silas Hickler.

A Case of Premeditation, a Dr. Thorndyke story in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke in which Thorndyke investigates the carefully planned knife-murder of the blackmailer ex-warder Pratt by Rufus Pembury, who used General O'Gorman's bloodhounds as a deliberate blind.

The Echo of a Mutiny, a Dr. Thorndyke story in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke in which Thorndyke uses microscopic identification of mole hair, acorn barnacle, and serpula deposits to prove that Tom Jeffreys murdered the lighthouse-keeper Amos Todd at the Girdler Sand.

A Wastrel's Romance, a Dr. Thorndyke story in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke in which Thorndyke uses microscopic analysis of coat dust to identify the location of a chloroform attack on Mrs. Chater at Willowdale.

The Old Lag, a Dr. Thorndyke story in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke in which Thorndyke proves Frank Belfield's innocence in the murder of the fence Caldwell by exposing Joseph Woodthorpe's scheme using forged fingerprint stamps and camouflaged evidence.

Silas Hickler, the cheerful-faced, round-faced murderer in The Case of Oscar Brodski who kills the diamond merchant Oscar Brodski for his diamonds and is eventually arrested when he steps ashore from the steamer.

Oscar Brodski, a well-known diamond merchant of Hatton Garden who is murdered by Hickler for his diamonds and whose body is placed on a railway line, investigated by Thorndyke in The Case of Oscar Brodski.
Mr. Boscovitch, a figure identified by Thorndyke at the crime scene in The Case of Oscar Brodski as the traveller who was to meet Oscar Brodski and travel on with him to Amsterdam.

Rufus Pembury (alias Francis Dobbs), the intelligent and strong-charactered murderer in A Case of Premeditation who kills the blackmailer ex-warder Pratt in a premeditated knife attack and attempts to use General O'Gorman's bloodhounds to cover his escape.

Ex-Warder Pratt, a retired prison warder employed as steward by General O'Gorman, who recognises the fugitive Francis Dobbs (Rufus Pembury) and blackmails him, ultimately murdered by Pembury using a musk-scented knife in A Case of Premeditation.
Jack Ellis, a supernumerary rural detective at Baysford Police Station, cleared of a crime when Thorndyke observes that both his forefingers are intact in A Case of Premeditation.
General O'Gorman, a former governor of Dartmoor Prison who employed a character as steward of his property at Baysford, appearing in A Case of Premeditation in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke.
