Tom Jeffreys (alias Jeffrey Rorke), the fugitive murderer in The Echo of a Mutiny who hides at the Girdler Sand lighthouse under a false name out of fear of denunciation by Amos Todd, whom he ultimately kills.
Amos Todd (alias James Brown), a criminal who turned Queen's Evidence against his accomplice Jeffreys and was subsequently murdered by him at the Girdler Sand lighthouse in The Echo of a Mutiny.
Captain Grumpass, an Elder Brother of Trinity House and a former client of Thorndyke's who had become a personal friend, appearing in The Echo of a Mutiny.

Augustus Bailey, a confidence trickster who lived by his wits under aliases including Captain Rowland and Geoffrey Harrington-Baillie, responsible for a chloroform attack on Mrs. Chater in A Wastrel's Romance.
Mrs. Jehu B. Chater, an extravagantly wealthy American woman of great distinction who is the crowning guest at the party in A Wastrel's Romance from The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke.
Frank Belfield, a reformed ex-convict who is falsely framed for the Camberwell murder of Caldwell by Joseph Woodthorpe using forged fingerprints, exonerated by Dr. Thorndyke in The Old Lag.

Joseph Woodthorpe, a former prison warder turned zoo keeper who murders Caldwell in Camberwell and forges Frank Belfield's fingerprints using photographs of official prison forms, as exposed by Dr. Thorndyke in The Old Lag.

The Oscar Brodski murder investigation, a Dr. Thorndyke case in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke in which the forensic detective proves the diamond merchant Brodski was murdered by Silas Hickler through analysis of boot soles, blood-flow direction, and broken spectacle lenses.

The murder of ex-warder Pratt, a Dr. Thorndyke case known as A Case of Premeditation in which Rufus Pembury stabs his blackmailer seven times in the back and uses bloodhounds as a blind to cover his tracks.

The disappearance of James Brown at the Girdler lighthouse, a Dr. Thorndyke case in The Echo of a Mutiny in which Brown vanishes from the screw-pile lighthouse on Girdler Sand, later proved to have been murdered by the fugitive Tom Jeffreys.

The chloroform attack on Mrs. Chater at Willowdale, a Dr. Thorndyke case in A Wastrel's Romance in which a woman bejewelled with diamonds is attacked from behind with a chloroform pad at the house Willowdale in Raynesford, with Thorndyke identifying the attacker through microscopic analysis of coat dust.

The Camberwell murder of Caldwell, a Dr. Thorndyke case known as The Old Lag in which Thorndyke proves Frank Belfield's innocence by demonstrating that forged fingerprint stamps and a handkerchief stained with camel's blood were planted to frame him.

Silas Hickler's murder of diamond merchant Oscar Brodski—strangling him, then placing his neck across a railway rail to simulate a fatal accident—to steal his diamonds, reconstructed by Dr. Thorndyke in The Case of Oscar Brodski.

Rufus Pembury's premeditated scheme to murder blackmailer Pratt using a musk-scented decoy knife planted to mislead General O'Gorman's bloodhounds, while the actual killing was carried out with a duplicate knife purchased separately, as reconstructed by Dr. Thorndyke in A Case of Premeditation.

Tom Jeffreys's murder of Amos Todd at the Girdler lighthouse by pushing him backwards off a height into the water and then sinking their boat by removing the hull plug, investigated by Dr. Thorndyke in The Echo of a Mutiny.

Joseph Woodthorpe's murder of Caldwell in Camberwell and his elaborate scheme of forging Frank Belfield's fingerprints from photographs of official prison finger-print forms to frame him for the crime, exposed by Dr. Thorndyke in The Old Lag.

Thorndyke's inference that Oscar Brodski was in the upright position when killed — from the blood having run down toward the chin — proving the murder occurred while he sat or stood, not lying on a railway track, in The Case of Oscar Brodski.
Microscopic analysis of textile fibres — including red wool, blue cotton, and yellow jute — used by Dr. Thorndyke in The Case of Oscar Brodski to link a crime scene to the murder location.

Thorndyke's reconstruction of Oscar Brodski's broken spectacle lenses to show that the surplus glass fragments are from a soft, separately planted source — proving the murder scene had been staged in The Case of Oscar Brodski.

Thorndyke's examination of Oscar Brodski's boot soles showing no sand or earth, proving he was killed indoors on a carpeted floor and carried to the railway rather than having walked there himself, in The Case of Oscar Brodski.

Thorndyke's argument that bloodhound tracking is logically flawed because it proves only olfactory similarity between people, not identity, demonstrated when a planted musk scent leads the dog to an innocent man in A Case of Premeditation.

Pipe physiognomy analysis used by Dr. Thorndyke in The Echo of a Mutiny to identify the victim by the personal wear patterns and tobacco residue of a pipe, establishing that a body exchange had taken place.

Microscopic identification of distinctive zigzag mole hairs on a pipe used by Dr. Thorndyke in The Echo of a Mutiny to establish that James Brown's pipe had been exchanged, confirming his identity in the disappearance investigation.

Thorndyke's identification of acorn barnacle shells and serpula tube fragments embedded in a scalp wound, matching them to the fouled piles of the Girdler lighthouse to pinpoint the crime scene in The Echo of a Mutiny.

Thorndyke's microscopic analysis of the unique composite factory dust found on Augustus Bailey's coat, identifying the particular combination of industries at Dockhead that produced it and so localising his dwelling in A Wastrel's Romance.

Thorndyke's proof that certain finger-prints are forgeries by demonstrating that the palmar surfaces of all five digits cannot be simultaneously in contact with a surface, and by detecting a post-mortem scar change in the thumb used, in The Old Lag.

Thorndyke's microscopic differentiation of blood by measuring corpuscle shape — circular in most mammals, oval with nuclei in birds, and oval in camels — applied to identify pheasant blood and exonerate a suspect in the Camberwell murder of Caldwell.

Extra glass fragments remaining after Brodski's broken spectacle lenses are reconstructed in The Case of Oscar Brodski, proving a planted splinter was added to the original wreckage to mislead investigators.
Red wool, blue cotton, and yellow jute fibres from a tablecloth found on the murder weapon in The Case of Oscar Brodski, linking the killing location to the suspect's house.

A musk-scented Norwegian knife, artist's brush-case, and crucible tongs recovered from a hornbeam tree in A Case of Premeditation, the tongs and case used to handle the knife without scenting the killer's clothes.
Bloody open-hand prints on an oak bough in A Case of Premeditation, which Thorndyke reads to show the killer was a short man with both forefingers intact.
A pipe from the lighthouse rack containing shag tobacco and mole hairs in The Echo of a Mutiny, proving it was Jeffreys's pipe placed in James Brown's pocket after the murder.
A handkerchief stained with camel blood — whose oval corpuscles distinguish it from all other mammals — linking suspect Woodthorpe to the Camberwell murder of Caldwell in The Old Lag.
Rubber stamps of a thumb and four fingers forged from an official prison form, used to plant false fingerprint evidence in The Old Lag to frame an innocent man.
Badsham Junction, a rural railway stop in 'The Case of Oscar Brodski' where the road from Silas Hickler's house meets the branch line to Warmington and near which Oscar Brodski is murdered.

The Girdler Sand screw-pile lighthouse, standing on spindle legs at the edge of the Girdler Sand in the Thames Estuary, where Tom Jeffreys murders Amos Todd and Thorndyke traces the crime scene in The Echo of a Mutiny.

A small English town near which Rufus Pembury lived modestly in A Case of Premeditation in the Adventures of Dr Thorndyke, and where Inspector Fox of the Baysford Police investigates the murder of ex-warder Pratt.
Willowdale, a house at Raynesford in A Wastrel's Romance in the Adventures of Dr Thorndyke, the setting of a spinsters' country dance and a chloroform attack on Mrs Chater.
5A King's Bench Walk in the Inner Temple, London, Dr Thorndyke's first-floor chambers shared with Jervis and Polton throughout the Adventures of Dr Thorndyke.

Dockhead, Bermondsey, a south-London industrial district identified by Thorndyke from dust analysis in 'A Wastrel's Romance' as the location of Bailey's flat at Hanover Buildings, adjacent to spice-grinders and rice-mills.

Thorndyke's portable laboratory — a green case one foot square and four inches deep — containing a complete outfit for preliminary forensic investigation, including a microscope, reagent bottles, test-tubes, and a spirit-lamp.

A Norwegian knife purchased as a duplicate of the murder weapon in 'A Case of Premeditation', smeared with rat blood and musk essence and attached by elastic glue to a cane ferrule so that a trained dog would follow its scent trail.

A thumbograph (finger-print album) bearing Reuben Hornby's signature alongside his fingerprints in 'The Old Lag', introduced by Thorndyke as evidence in the forgery case and noted as the album through which Thorndyke first met his wife.
Silas Hickler's motive in 'The Case of Oscar Brodski': an overwhelming desire to seize Brodski's diamonds, which he valued at five thousand pounds, rather than let them leave his reach.

Rufus Pembury's motive in 'A Case of Premeditation': eliminating the blackmailer Pratt to preserve his existence as Francis Dobbs, reasoning that a man who blackmails has forfeited his right to protection from his victim.

Tom Jeffreys's motive in 'The Echo of a Mutiny': his terror that Amos Todd would denounce him for past mutiny-murders committed under his true name of Jeffrey Rorke, forcing him to change identity and hide on the Girdler.

The Vanishing Man (also known as The Eye of Osiris), a 1911 Dr. Thorndyke novel by R. Austin Freeman in which Thorndyke investigates the mysterious disappearance of John Bellingham and the concealment of his body within a museum mummy.

Dr. Paul Berkeley, the young recently-qualified physician who serves as the first-person narrator of The Vanishing Man, introduced as faultlessly attired and newly employed in a medical practice he takes over from Dr. Barnard.

Dr. Jervis, Dr. Thorndyke's companion and former fellow-student who assists in investigations across John Thorndyke's Cases, The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke, and The Vanishing Man, describing himself as having become parasitical on Thorndyke.

John Bellingham, a well-known archaeologist who tattooed the Eye of Osiris in vermilion on his chest, whose unexplained disappearance and presumed death—with his body later found concealed inside the Sebek-hotep mummy—form the mystery of The Vanishing Man.
Godfrey Bellingham, the brother of the missing John Bellingham in The Vanishing Man, a fine-looking invalid of about fifty-five who lives in Woodford and stands to inherit under his brother's will.

Ruth Bellingham, the striking and handsome daughter of Godfrey Bellingham in The Vanishing Man, who serves as both a love interest for the narrator Dr. Berkeley and an important witness to the circumstances surrounding her uncle John's disappearance.
George Hurst, a cousin of the missing John Bellingham in The Vanishing Man, who lives at The Poplars in Eltham and stands to receive five thousand pounds under Bellingham's will.

Arthur Jellicoe, the solicitor and confidential agent of John Bellingham in The Vanishing Man who murders his client and conceals the body inside a museum mummy in order to benefit from the assigned estate.
Phyllis Oman, a young woman who assists Miss Bellingham and is encountered by Dr. Jervis in The Vanishing Man (The Eye of Osiris).
Dr. Norbury, a British Museum Egyptologist who temporarily holds John Bellingham's old post and is an old friend of Bellingham's, key to the mummy identification in The Vanishing Man.
Mr. Marchmont, a pleasant-faced elderly solicitor who takes on the Bellingham inheritance case as a personal favour to Dr. Jervis, in The Vanishing Man.

The John Bellingham disappearance, the central case of The Vanishing Man in which a gentleman vanishes after visiting relatives, his absence eventually traced to the concealment of his murdered body inside a museum mummy.

The murder and mummy concealment scheme, the central crime of The Vanishing Man in which solicitor Arthur Jellicoe murders John Bellingham and conceals the body inside the mummy of Sebek-hotep, which he has donated to the British Museum.
Arthur Jellicoe's scheme to suppress the will's burial clause and assign the inheritance to himself as repayment for a five-thousand-pound unsecured loan, in The Vanishing Man.

Arthur Jellicoe's impersonation of John Bellingham at George Hurst's house—removing his spectacles to alter his appearance—to create a false date for Bellingham's disappearance while concealing his murder, as confessed by Jellicoe in The Vanishing Man.

Arthur Jellicoe's scheme of scattering bones from the Sebek-hotep mummy near the Bellingham estate to simulate the remains of John Bellingham and induce a court to presume his death, foiled when Dr. Thorndyke identifies the bones as ancient mummy fragments in The Vanishing Man.

The disappearance of John Bellingham on 23 November 1902, when he arrived from Paris at Charing Cross, visited his relative George Hurst at The Poplars, Eltham, and was never seen again, in The Vanishing Man.
The accidental discovery of a canvas bag containing human bones in a watercress-bed at Sidcup, which becomes the first physical clue in the John Bellingham disappearance case in The Vanishing Man.
Thorndyke's X-ray examination of the Sebek-hotep mummy at the British Museum which reveals it to contain the body of the missing John Bellingham, in The Vanishing Man.
Arthur Jellicoe's suicide by potassium cyanide, taken from his cigarette-case when he realizes Thorndyke has exposed his scheme, before he can be brought to trial in The Vanishing Man.
The Probate Court hearing examining whether John Bellingham of 141 Queen Square should be legally presumed dead, with the jury finding insufficient reason to grant the presumption.

Coroner's inquest held at Woodford to establish the identity and cause of death in the disappearance of John Bellingham, ultimately adjourned when insufficient facts were available to reach a verdict.
Thorndyke establishes in The Vanishing Man that John Bellingham was last certainly seen alive on 14 October 1902 at 141 Queen Square when he visited his solicitor Jellicoe.
The finding in 'The Vanishing Man' that the third finger of a skeletal left hand has been cut off to remove a ring worn so tightly by John Bellingham that it could never be pulled free in life.

The fact, established by Thorndyke's X-ray examination, that the body of the missing John Bellingham was concealed inside the cartonnage of the Sebek-hotep mummy at the British Museum in The Vanishing Man.

The forensic finding in The Vanishing Man that the scattered bones discovered near the Bellingham estate are not the remains of John Bellingham but fragments of the ancient Egyptian mummy Sebek-hotep from the British Museum.

In The Vanishing Man, Bellingham's will clause 2 requires burial in the St George Bloomsbury parish; Thorndyke satisfies this by arguing that the Egyptian mummy housed in the British Museum — a body in an authorised place within that parish — legally fulfils the condition.

The legal rule applied in 'The Vanishing Man' that the estate turns on the latest provable instant of life, with proof of survivorship by even less than a minute having decided inheritance in past cases.

Dr. Thorndyke's systematic laboratory method, emphasising the development and custody of photographic negatives and the principle of noting all observations rather than only those that appear significant.

X-ray (skiagraph) bone analysis used by Dr. Thorndyke in The Vanishing Man to examine a mummy, identifying metallic silver wire repairs and gold dental stoppings that confirmed the identity of John Bellingham's concealed body.

Thorndyke's identification of the Sebek-hotep mummy as John Bellingham by matching specific gold and tin amalgam dental fillings and missing teeth against the dentist's case-book records in The Vanishing Man.

Dr. Thorndyke's systematic skeletal examination protocol in The Vanishing Man, comprising measurement of bone lengths, assessment of eburnation, observation of adipocere and its localisation, and examination for signs of submersion.

Thorndyke's investigative principle in The Vanishing Man of building conviction through particle-by-particle accumulation of circumstantial evidence, arguing that even individually inconclusive facts yield a highly conclusive total when multiplied.

Dr. Thorndyke's application of legal survivorship analysis in The Vanishing Man, adapted from ordinary presumption-of-death law to the unusual circumstance of John Bellingham's disappearance where only one body is producible.

The mummy of Sebek-hotep, an ancient Egyptian artefact donated to the British Museum by John Bellingham in The Vanishing Man, which Jellicoe exploits as a container to conceal Bellingham's murdered body.

An 18th-dynasty Egyptian ring bearing the device of the Eye of Osiris, worn by John Bellingham on the third finger of his left hand in 'The Vanishing Man', and later found detached from a skeletal hand whose finger had been removed to obtain it.

An 18th-dynasty lapis lazuli scarab bearing the cartouche of Amenhotep III, habitually worn by John Bellingham suspended from his watch-chain and recognised by witnesses in 'The Vanishing Man' as a key identifier of the missing man.

A suit-case bearing the initials J.B. found unclaimed in the Charing Cross cloak-room in 'The Vanishing Man', identified as belonging to John Bellingham and marking the last confirmed point of his presence.
John Bellingham's will of 21 September 1892, a notoriously complex document whose clause 2 conditions on burial in St. Bridget's churchyard create the legal puzzle at the centre of The Vanishing Man.

The X-ray skiagraph of the Sebek-hotep mummy taken by Dr. Thorndyke in The Vanishing Man, which plainly reveals the gold and tin dental fillings, the tattooed Eye of Osiris, and silver suture wires in the kneecaps that prove the mummy conceals John Bellingham's body.
The tomb furniture of Sebek-hotep bequeathed to the British Museum in 'The Vanishing Man', comprising four Canopic jars, Ushabti figures, tomb provisions and models, and a chair and ink-palette.
A finely executed tattooed Eye of Osiris in vermilion on John Bellingham's chest, used as a distinctive identifying mark when Thorndyke X-rays the Sebek-hotep mummy in The Vanishing Man.
An old Pott's fracture of the left ankle, used by Thorndyke in The Vanishing Man to identify discovered skeletal remains as those of John Bellingham.

Three silver suture wires introduced by Sir Morgan Bennet into the kneecaps of John Bellingham during a surgical repair, identified in the Sebek-hotep mummy X-ray and confirmed by the surgeon's own operative notes, in The Vanishing Man.

A patch of eburnation (rheumatoid arthritis lesion) on the head of the right femur in 'The Vanishing Man', matched by Thorndyke to a corresponding lesion in the hip-joint cavity of skeletal remains to confirm identity as John Bellingham.

Snail eggs and worm-tubes on the dorsal surfaces of recovered bones in The Vanishing Man, used by Thorndyke to determine the position in which an arm was submerged and prove the limbs were deposited separately.

The complete absence of adipocere on the discovered bones in The Vanishing Man, proving to Thorndyke that the flesh was removed before burial — consistent only with an Egyptian mummy, not fresh remains.
A ring and mummy finger-bones found at the bottom of a disused well at the Woodford house in The Vanishing Man, proving the scattered bones were from an Egyptian mummy rather than John Bellingham.

Distinctive gold and tin amalgam dental fillings in specific teeth of the Sebek-hotep mummy, matched against John Bellingham's dentist's case-book records to confirm the body's identity in The Vanishing Man.

Jellicoe's financial motive in The Vanishing Man: a collusive scheme in which he stood to gain up to £50,000 from John Bellingham's estate if the inheritance could be secured, balanced against the risk of being hanged.
Nevill's Court, a narrow Fetter Lane alley reached through an archway, where Godfrey Bellingham and his daughter Ruth live in poverty while John Bellingham's estate is disputed in The Vanishing Man.

141 Queen Square, Bloomsbury, John Bellingham's London house and Egyptian collection in The Vanishing Man, bequeathed in his will and occupied by his brother's family throughout the years of his disappearance.
The Poplars, the Eltham home of George Hurst in Kent, the last place John Bellingham is known to have visited before his disappearance on 23 November 1902, in The Vanishing Man.
Godfrey Bellingham's former house near Woodford in 'The Vanishing Man', close to the Cuckoo Pits in Epping Forest, the first destination of police after a human arm is found in the nearby pond.

The British Museum in Bloomsbury, whose Egyptian Department exhibits the mummy of Sebek-hotep concealing the body of John Bellingham, making it a central location in the investigation of The Vanishing Man.
The watercress-bed at Sidcup, Kent, in The Vanishing Man, the site of the first discovered skeletal bones initially taken to confirm the worst fears about John Bellingham's fate.
The Cuckoo Pits in Epping Forest near Woodford, where the bones of a right arm are discovered by police in 'The Vanishing Man', the first physical remains attributed to the missing John Bellingham.

184 New Square, Lincoln's Inn, the office and chambers of solicitor Arthur Jellicoe, where he administers the Bellingham estate and where he ultimately commits suicide by potassium cyanide in The Vanishing Man.
Dr. Paul Berkeley's role in 'The Vanishing Man' as the first-person narrator and Watson-like companion, a recently qualified general practitioner who accompanies and assists Dr. Thorndyke.

Ruth Bellingham's role in 'The Vanishing Man' as the imperilled innocent and love interest of Paul Berkeley, wrongly suspected of murdering her uncle and ultimately exonerated by Thorndyke's evidence.

The British Museum's Egyptian Department in 'The Vanishing Man', which receives John Bellingham's antiquities collection under his will and whose temporary keeper Dr. Norbury is an old friend of the missing man.

The Secret of Father Brown, a G. K. Chesterton short-story collection framed by Father Brown explaining his method of moral empathy to visitors, including cases such as The Actor and the Alibi and The Chief Mourner of Marne.
G. K. Chesterton, the British author and creator of Father Brown, who wrote The Innocence of Father Brown, The Wisdom of Father Brown, and The Secret of Father Brown.

The Father Brown saga, the series of detective short stories by G. K. Chesterton featuring the intuitive Catholic priest Father Brown, collected in The Innocence of Father Brown, The Wisdom of Father Brown, and The Secret of Father Brown.
Grandison Chace, a Boston American traveller who visits Father Brown and presses him to explain his methods, serving as the audience for the framing story of The Secret of Father Brown.

Father Brown's method of solving crimes by imaginatively inhabiting the murderer, explained in the framing story of The Secret of Father Brown as: 'I don't try to get outside the man; I try to get inside the murderer... I am always inside a man, moving his arms and legs.'

The Secret of Father Brown, the framing story of the collection of the same name, in which Father Brown explains to American visitor Grandison Chace his method of solving crimes by imaginatively entering the mind of the murderer.

The Mirror of the Magistrate, a Father Brown story in The Secret of Father Brown in which James Bagshaw and Father Brown investigate the death of Justice Sir Humphrey Gwynne, found with his head in a garden pond, eventually revealing that the murderer Osric Orm shot at his own reflection.
The Man with Two Beards, a story in The Secret of Father Brown told by Father Brown to the criminologist Professor Crake, involving John Bankes and the criminal Michael Moonshine.
The Song of the Flying Fish, a Father Brown story from The Secret of Father Brown centring on Mr. Peregrine Smart, a man whose soul hovers around a single possession and a single joke.

The Actor and the Alibi, a Father Brown story from The Secret of Father Brown in which Father Brown investigates a murder connected to theatrical manager Mr. Mundon Mandeville and a deceptive stage alibi.

The Vanishing of Vaudrey, a story in The Secret of Father Brown in which Father Brown investigates the mysterious death of Sir Arthur Vaudrey and deduces that John Dalmon cut his throat in a barber's chair by sending the barber outside on a pretext.

The Worst Crime in the World, a Father Brown story from The Secret of Father Brown in which Father Brown wanders through a picture gallery and becomes embroiled in a case involving Captain James Musgrave.
The Red Moon of Meru, a Father Brown story from The Secret of Father Brown set at a bazaar at Mallowood Abbey and centring on the disappearance of a great ruby, with Tommy Hunter as a key figure.

The Chief Mourner of Marne, a story in The Secret of Father Brown in which Father Brown reveals that the reclusive Marquis of Marne—Maurice Mair—faked death in a duel and then murdered his brother James, having lived for years in guilt and isolation.

The Secret of Flambeau, the closing frame of The Secret of Father Brown, in which Flambeau confesses to Father Brown that he is still a wanted criminal of two hemispheres and that Father Brown has always known it.
James Bagshaw, a big, dark, good-humoured police detective who is an old friend of Wilfred Underhill's and investigates the Gwynne murder in The Mirror of the Magistrate.
Sir Humphrey Gwynne, the old judge known for his wartime stance on spying, who is murdered via a mirror contrivance in The Mirror of the Magistrate.
Sir Arthur Travers, the brilliant prosecuting advocate who is subsequently shot dead by the same man through a real window rather than a mirror, in The Mirror of the Magistrate.
Osric Orm, a celebrated poet with a remarkable head of golden hair who is present at the scene of Sir Humphrey Gwynne's murder in The Mirror of the Magistrate.
Michael Moonshine, a once-famous burglar who retires and dies of natural causes, whose corpse is then exploited by John Bankes as a stage property to conceal a theft in The Man with Two Beards.
John Bankes, a burly young man who steals the emeralds and other jewels by using the recently deceased criminal Michael Moonshine's corpse as a stage property, in The Man with Two Beards.
Jameson, the dull and unobtrusive head clerk of Mr. Smart's office who is revealed by Father Brown to be the thief of the goldfish in The Song of the Flying Fish.
Mrs. Mandeville, the quiet, pale-faced wife of the theatre manager who uses her superior intelligence to murder her husband by arranging a trap, exposed by Father Brown in The Actor and the Alibi.

John Dalmon, the man generally supposed to be engaged to Sir Arthur Vaudrey's ward, who cuts Vaudrey's throat in the barber's shop while keeping him apparently alive to serve a criminal purpose, in The Vanishing of Vaudrey.
Captain James Musgrave, who murders his own father, conceals the body in the moat, and impersonates him during legal negotiations over the family estate, in The Worst Crime in the World.
Tommy Hunter, an outwardly correct and commonplace man who steals the Red Moon ruby by thrusting his bare arm through a window at a carefully chosen moment, in The Red Moon of Meru.

The Marquis of Marne, in reality Maurice Mair, a brilliantly gifted man who faked being shot in a duel, waited motionless for his brother James to bend over him, then shot James dead, as exposed by Father Brown in The Chief Mourner of Marne.
Hugo Romaine, the greatest actor of his day, who trained the Marquis of Marne in acting and serves as his second in a duel, in The Chief Mourner of Marne.

The Gwynne murder (Mirror of the Magistrate case), a Father Brown case in The Secret of Father Brown in which Justice Gwynne is found dead with his head in a pond, and Father Brown reveals that the murderer Osric Orm shot at his own reflection, with the bullet breaking the hall looking-glass.

The Michael Moonshine / Man with Two Beards case, a Father Brown case in The Secret of Father Brown in which Father Brown establishes that the genuinely reformed ex-criminal Michael Moonshine was murdered by a criminal who exploited his death as a stage property, planting two different false beards on the corpse.

The goldfish theft (Song of the Flying Fish case), a Father Brown case in The Secret of Father Brown in which Father Brown deduces that Jameson, the head clerk, stole Peregrine Smart's golden fishbowl, having had many prior opportunities to do so unobserved.

The Mandeville murder and Actor and the Alibi case, investigated by Father Brown in The Secret of Father Brown, in which the Screen scene from School for Scandal provides the key alibi mechanism that ultimately exposes the real killer.

The disappearance and murder of Sir Arthur Vaudrey, whose throat was cut by John Dalmon in a barber's shop while his white hat was left behind as a clue, investigated by Father Brown in The Vanishing of Vaudrey.

The Worst Crime in the World case, investigated by Father Brown in The Secret of Father Brown, in which he deduces that Captain Musgrave killed his own father, hid the body in armour and then in the moat, and impersonated him.

The Red Moon of Meru ruby theft case, in The Secret of Father Brown, in which Father Brown reveals that he himself had temporarily removed and then returned the great red ruby to prevent a greater crime.
The Marne duel-and-fraud case, investigated by Father Brown in The Chief Mourner of Marne, in which Maurice Mair faked being shot in a duel and then killed his brother James as he bent over him.

Sir Arthur Travers's murder of Justice Sir Humphrey Gwynne by firing a bullet that shattered a mirror—whose falling fragments knocked over a pot and palm—to suppress treasonous documents Gwynne held, investigated by Father Brown in The Mirror of the Magistrate.
John Bankes's unique crime of using the corpse of the retired burglar Michael Moonshine as a stage property to create a false alibi and conceal his theft of emeralds in The Man with Two Beards.
Mrs. Mandeville's murder of her husband through a trap-door alibi in The Actor and the Alibi from The Secret of Father Brown, slipping behind a theatrical screen while appearing to be on stage.

John Dalmon's murder of Sir Arthur Vaudrey by cutting his throat in a barber's chair after sending the barber outside on a pretext, investigated and solved by Father Brown in The Vanishing of Vaudrey.

Captain James Musgrave's murder of his father at Musgrave Moss castle in Northumberland—concealing the body first in the suit of armour, then in the moat—and his impersonation of the dead man to secure the estate, as exposed by Father Brown in The Worst Crime in the World.

Maurice Mair's scheme in The Chief Mourner of Marne of feigning being shot in the duel with James Mair, lying perfectly still until James bent over him, and then shooting James dead to secure the Marne estate.
Tommy Hunter's theft of the Red Moon of Meru ruby in The Secret of Father Brown, using a hand pre-painted pale brown to snatch the stone through a window without leaving a visible trace.

The hall looking-glass in 'The Mirror of the Magistrate' shattered by a bullet during Justice Gwynne's murder, which in the half-light of monochrome walls would have appeared to be the end of the passage rather than a reflective surface.

Michael Moonshine's two beards in 'The Man with Two Beards': an old disguise beard kept after retirement from crime, and a bright-scarlet stage beard planted at the scene to mislead investigators as to his identity.

Peregrine Smart's large, beautifully blown Venetian glass bubble with iridescent colouring in 'The Song of the Flying Fish', used as a fish bowl and the object around which a theft scheme is constructed.
The Red Moon of Meru, a large, brilliant ruby on display at the Mallowood Abbey bazaar in The Secret of Father Brown, which disappears during the event and is later found on the window-sill.
A missing suit of armour at Musgrave's castle in 'The Worst Crime in the World', whose absence from an otherwise exaggeratedly symmetrical room alerts Father Brown to the crime's true nature.
Flambeau's castle in Spain in The Secret of Father Brown framing story, a solid and well-appointed real castle where the reformed master thief has settled in retirement.

Justice Gwynne's irregularly illuminated garden in The Mirror of the Magistrate in The Secret of Father Brown, its scattered electric lights and pale lamp-lit pond creating the dim conditions in which the murderer shot at his own reflection.

Musgrave Moss castle in Northumberland in The Worst Crime in the World in The Secret of Father Brown, a genuine square Norman castle where Father Brown exposes Captain Musgrave's murder of his father and impersonation of him.
Mallowood Abbey, Lady Mounteagle's estate in The Red Moon of Meru in The Secret of Father Brown, a converted former abbey where a charity bazaar is held and the great ruby is stolen.

The Castle of Marne, a melancholy four-towered mansion in The Chief Mourner of Marne in The Secret of Father Brown, home to the reclusive Marquis exposed by Father Brown as the supposed duel victim Maurice Mair.

Travers's motive in 'The Mirror of the Magistrate': killing Justice Gwynne to prevent him from finding treasonable documents stored in the bungalow, documents that would have exposed Travers's wartime treachery.

Vaudrey's revenge scheme in 'The Vanishing of Vaudrey': forcing his daughter Sybil to marry the murderer Dalmon to punish her for objecting to a husband who had been in prison, ensuring she would be bound to a man even worse.

Captain Musgrave's motive in 'The Worst Crime in the World': murdering his own father to secure the estate and post obit bond, and evading Russian blackmail for wartime treason by escaping abroad and assuming a false identity.

Maurice Mair's motive in 'The Chief Mourner of Marne': restless vanity and jealousy of his brother James's engagement, in one of the few matters in which James Mair had outshone him, driving him to fake his own death and then shoot James.
In The Mirror of the Magistrate, Father Brown proves the killer shot at his own reflection in a large looking-glass at the end of the passage, mistaking it in the half-light for Justice Gwynne.
In The Man with Two Beards, Father Brown attests that Michael Moonshine was his genuine penitent and friend, a great sinner who made more of his penitence than most men make of their innocence.
In The Song of the Flying Fish, Father Brown deduces that the sound Boyle heard was Jameson unbarring rather than barring the door, proving Jameson was the disguised Arab who made the exit.

In The Actor and the Alibi, Father Brown realises that the School for Scandal's screen scene was the specific act that created Mrs Mandeville's alibi window, its well-known stage business fixing the critical timing.
In The Worst Crime in the World, Father Brown exposes Captain Musgrave by pointing out that no man of eighty could have leaped the moat — only the young son in his father's disguise could.

Father Brown reveals in The Chief Mourner of Marne that the reclusive Marquis is Maurice Mair — the supposed duel victim — who faked his death, murdered his brother James, and has lived under James's identity ever since.

Two different false beards worn by Michael Moonshine in The Man with Two Beards, the discrepancy revealing that the thief at the scene and the suspect seen earlier were the same man wearing two separate disguises.

A hunter's hand painted brown in The Red Moon of Meru, hidden beneath a glove because the painted hand — not a naturally dark-skinned one — would have been visible to bystanders watching the ruby's theft.
Vaudrey's distinctive white hat left in a barber's shop, spotted by Father Brown in The Vanishing of Vaudrey as the overlooked clue proving Vaudrey had been in the village before his disappearance.
Hugo Romaine's motionless stillness at the Marne duel in The Chief Mourner of Marne, identified by Father Brown as the key clue that Romaine was waiting for Maurice to fall — not firing at him.

Grandison Chace's role in 'The Secret of Father Brown' as the American framing narrator who draws Father Brown into explaining his method of identifying with criminals, asking for concrete examples from recent cases.
The Wisdom of Father Brown, a G. K. Chesterton short-story collection in which Father Brown solves a series of baffling cases, often assisted by his reformed criminal friend Flambeau.

The Absence of Mr Glass, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown exposes the elaborate misreading of evidence by the eminent criminologist Dr. Orion Hood, who has incorrectly theorized what actually turns out to be a magic act rehearsal.

The Paradise of Thieves, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown set in Tuscany, in which Father Brown exposes Samuel Harrogate as a bank embezzler who staged his own brigand kidnapping using hired men.

The Duel of Dr Hirsch, a Father Brown story in The Wisdom of Father Brown set in Paris in which Flambeau and Father Brown become entangled in an apparent espionage affair involving a forged spy note and a noiseless explosive formula.

The Man in the Passage, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown investigates a murder connected to the celebrated actress Aurora Rome and a mysterious figure seen in a passage beside the Apollo Theatre.

The Mistake of the Machine, a Father Brown story in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown and Flambeau sit by Pilgrim's Pond at sunset and Father Brown critiques the unreliability of a psychometric lie-detection machine used by Greywood Usher.

The Head of Caesar, a Father Brown story in The Wisdom of Father Brown set in a Brompton or Kensington avenue in which Father Brown and Flambeau investigate a matter involving Arthur and Christabel Carstairs.
The Purple Wig, a Father Brown story from The Wisdom of Father Brown featuring newspaper editor Mr. Edward Nutt.

The Perishing of the Pendragons, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown and Flambeau investigate arson attacks on the Pendragon tower on the Cornwall coast, revealing an inheritance plot.
The God of the Gongs, a Father Brown story from The Wisdom of Father Brown set on a chilly early winter afternoon.

The Salad of Colonel Cray, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown investigates a mystery involving Colonel Cray and his former mess companion Putnam, and the menace of the Monkey's Feet.
The Strange Crime of John Boulnois, the eleventh story in 'The Wisdom of Father Brown', a short mystery by G. K. Chesterton in which Father Brown investigates a shooting at a country house.
The Fairy Tale of Father Brown, the twelfth story in 'The Wisdom of Father Brown', a collection of Father Brown mysteries by G. K. Chesterton.
Dr. Orion Hood, an eminent criminologist and specialist in moral disorders whose elaborate but entirely wrong interpretation of the evidence in The Absence of Mr Glass is exposed by Father Brown.
James Todhunter, a respectable young man who also performs as Zaladin, World's Greatest Conjurer and Contortionist, whose puzzling room situation is solved by Father Brown in The Absence of Mr Glass.
Ezza Montano, a professional actor who calls himself the King of Thieves and plays the role of a brigand courier to assist Samuel Harrogate's staged kidnapping scheme in The Paradise of Thieves.

Samuel Harrogate, an English bank embezzler who fled to Italy with the funds of the Hull and Huddersfield Bank and arranged a sham brigand kidnapping of himself using his own hired men, arrested by Father Brown in The Paradise of Thieves.

Colonel Jules Dubosc, a French artillery colonel of Belfort who impersonates Dr. Paul Hirsch to steal secret documents in The Duel of Dr Hirsch, moving about a brilliantly lit bedroom while his double is watched.
Dr. Paul Hirsch, a French scientist, publicist, and moralist who has recently discovered a noiseless explosive, whose reputation is endangered by a forged spy document in The Duel of Dr Hirsch.
Aurora Rome, an actress found murdered in her dressing room with her dead face turned upward in her green and gold robes, the victim at the centre of The Man in the Passage.
Greywood Usher, a cadaverous ex-detective and Yankee philosopher who relies on a psychometric pulsometer machine to identify suspects, challenged by Father Brown in The Mistake of the Machine.
Christabel Carstairs, daughter of the late Colonel Carstairs and one of the siblings blackmailed by her brother Arthur in The Head of Caesar.

Arthur Carstairs, a coin collector of singular and criminal kind in The Head of Caesar who sold the Carstairs family collection piece by piece and then blackmailed his own brother and sister while disguised.
Admiral Pendragon, a self-declared atheist who is the subject of a sinister arson scheme at Pendragon Tower designed to fake a family curse, in The Perishing of the Pendragons.
Colonel Cray, a soldier who had been engaged to Audrey abroad and travelled back with her, appearing in The Salad of Colonel Cray from The Wisdom of Father Brown.

The Absence of Mr Glass, a Father Brown case in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown deduces from a silk top hat with no hair traces that the threatening 'Mr Glass' does not exist and that the ventriloquist Todhunter is his own prisoner.

A scheme in which Samuel Harrogate embezzled the funds of the Hull and Huddersfield Bank then staged a sham brigand kidnapping in an Italian Apennine hollow to divert suspicion, exposed by Father Brown in The Paradise of Thieves.

The Hirsch forged spy document affair, a Father Brown case in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown deduces that a forged spy note implicating Dr Paul Hirsch was planted by a man who is simultaneously Hirsch's accusatory double, Dubosc.

The murder of Aurora Rome, a Father Brown case in The Wisdom of Father Brown set at the Apollo Theatre in which the actress Aurora Rome is found dead, with Father Brown deducing the killer's identity through a sliding looking-glass panel that broke an alibi.

The Carstairs Collection theft and blackmail case, in The Head of Caesar, in which Father Brown exposes Arthur Carstairs for secretly selling off the family coin collection piece by piece and then blackmailing his siblings.
Samuel Harrogate's embezzlement of the Hull and Huddersfield Bank followed by a staged kidnapping by fake brigands in Italy to divert suspicion, uncovered by Father Brown in The Paradise of Thieves.

Arthur Carstairs's blackmail of his own brother and sister while in disguise, after secretly selling off the Carstairs family coin collection piece by piece, as exposed by Father Brown in The Head of Caesar.

A scheme of arson against the pitch-and-resin Pendragon tower on the Cornwall coast to manufacture a hereditary curse and eliminate the Pendragon heirs for the sake of the estate's inheritance, uncovered by Father Brown in The Perishing of the Pendragons.
The motive in 'The Perishing of the Pendragons': a wicked uncle's drive to secure the Pendragon estate, pursued through arson designed to fake the family curse and eliminate the rightful heir.
The motive in 'The Paradise of Thieves': an embezzler's scheme to conceal his flight with company funds by arranging his own fake capture by brigands he had hired himself.

Arthur Carstairs's motive in 'The Head of Caesar': wounded pride and resentment at his father's cruel will, which led him to hate the Roman money he had been left and to scheme against his siblings through blackmail.

Father Brown's investigative approach in 'The Mistake of the Machine' of inferring guilt or innocence by reading a person's eyes, voice, domestic happiness, and habitual subjects of conversation rather than relying on mechanical tests.

Father Brown's reasoning that a forger who knows all the true facts would make the forged document wrong in precisely the right ways, used to expose the Hirsch spy-document affair in The Duel of Dr Hirsch.

A deduction technique used by Father Brown in The Man in the Passage, where he realises that the 'man' witnessed in a corridor was his own reflection in a mirror, breaking a false alibi in the murder of Aurora Rome.

Father Brown's critique in The Mistake of the Machine of the lie-detector (psychometric machine), arguing that no machine can lie or tell the truth, and that any machine must be operated by the unreliable agency of man.

Father Brown's reasoning from small contextual details — the way wax spots in certain weather, and the recognition of a distant figure by gait rather than face — applied to unravel the Carstairs Collection theft in The Head of Caesar.
A silk top hat bearing no hair traces whatsoever in The Absence of Mr Glass, revealed by Father Brown to prove it was never worn rather than belonging to a bald man.

A sliding looking-glass panel in the passage beside the Apollo Theatre in The Man in the Passage, which reflected witnesses' own images and resolved the contradictory accounts of Aurora Rome's murder.
A forged spy document in The Duel of Dr Hirsch whose details are systematically wrong — wrong cupboard, wrong ink colour — proving it was fabricated to frame Dr Hirsch.
A bronze Head of Caesar coin, the sole surviving relic of the Carstairs Collection in The Head of Caesar, clenched in Father Brown's fist as he unravels the theft and blackmail scheme.
The Scarborough sea-front in The Absence of Mr Glass in The Wisdom of Father Brown, location of Dr Orion Hood's consulting rooms where Father Brown exposes the non-existence of the supposed Mr Glass.

The Paradise of Thieves, a hidden Apennine hollow in The Paradise of Thieves in The Wisdom of Father Brown, invisible from both road and valley, where a bandit band shelters and a staged kidnapping scheme unravels.

A passage running along the side of the Apollo Theatre in the Adelphi, London, the scene of Aurora Rome's murder in The Man in the Passage, where a sliding looking-glass panel created contradictory witness accounts.
Pilgrim's Pond, the country estate of millionaire Ireton Todd in The Mistake of the Machine in The Wisdom of Father Brown, where a staged shooting is set up and exposed by Father Brown.

Pendragon Tower on the Cornish coast in The Perishing of the Pendragons in The Wisdom of Father Brown, a pitch-and-resin-wood structure whose deliberate burning is used to fake the family curse's flaming-tower omen.

The conjurer's props belonging to Todhunter in 'The Absence of Mr Glass', including ropes, cards, a top hat, and a sword, which Father Brown deduces indicate not a criminal situation but a man practising to become a professional conjurer and ventriloquist.

A pulsometer-based psychometric device in 'The Mistake of the Machine' used by Greywood Usher to test a subject's guilt by monitoring heart rate at the pronunciation of significant words, which Father Brown argues cannot replace moral inference.

Dr. Hirsch's secret formula for a noiseless explosive, carefully guarded by the French government, which becomes the subject of a forged spy document planted to discredit Hirsch in 'The Duel of Dr Hirsch.'
Flambeau's role as a semi-official private investigator in 'The Head of Caesar', sitting opposite Father Brown as the cases are discussed.

Colonel Dubosc disguised as Dr. Hirsch in The Duel of Dr Hirsch from The Wisdom of Father Brown, using a framework of red hair fitted around his face and slipping on a loose black coat to impersonate the scientist.

Arthur Carstairs's disguise as a crooked-nosed blackmailer in The Head of Caesar from The Wisdom of Father Brown, identified by Father Brown as a coin-collector of a singular kind despite his altered appearance.

In The Duel of Dr Hirsch, Father Brown demonstrates that the apparent spy Dubosc and the accused Dr Hirsch are one and the same person, their physical differences explained entirely by padding and disguise.
In The Absence of Mr Glass, Father Brown proves that 'Mr Glass' does not exist: a juggler counting aloud the three glasses he tossed was misheard by witnesses as a two-man conversation.

A Thief in the Night, a Raffles short-story collection by E. W. Hornung in which Bunny Manders narrates further adventures of the amateur cracksman, concluding with Raffles volunteering for the Boer War.

E. W. Hornung, the British author who created the gentleman-burglar A. J. Raffles and his companion Bunny Manders, writing The Amateur Cracksman, Raffles: Further Adventures, A Thief in the Night, and Mr. Justice Raffles.

The Raffles saga, a series of crime stories featuring gentleman-burglar A. J. Raffles and narrated by his accomplice Bunny Manders, comprising The Amateur Cracksman, Raffles: Further Adventures, A Thief in the Night, and Mr. Justice Raffles.

Out of Paradise, a Raffles story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles burglarises Lord Lochmaben's Palace Gardens home to recover diamonds on behalf of Bunny's beloved, drawing on some of their earliest days together.
The Chest of Silver, a story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles hides inside an iron-clamped silver chest to infiltrate a bank strong-room, with Crawshay also appearing.

The Rest Cure, a Raffles story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles occupies a house near Campden Hill under a vagrant disguise, conducting a break-in at Colonel Crutchley's as his personal version of a rest cure.

The Criminologists' Club, a Raffles story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles accepts an invitation to a secret club of criminologists and uses the evening's dinner as cover for burglarising Lord Thornaby's Park Lane house.

The Field of Phillipi, a story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles robs his old school-head Nipper Nasmyth's bank funds as private justice, taking just over a hundred sovereigns earmarked for the Founder's Fund.
A Bad Night, a short story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles and Bunny carry out a burglary of wedding presents at East Molesey, involving a young man named Medlicott.

A Trap to Catch a Cracksman, a Raffles story in A Thief in the Night in which prize-fighter Barney Maguire sets a drugged-whiskey trap to catch a burglar, but Raffles turns it to his own advantage and steals Maguire's trophies.
The Spoils of Sacrilege, a Raffles story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles, disguised in a riding suit, burglarises Mr. Guillemard's country house and carries off old silver.

The Raffles Relics, the concluding story of A Thief in the Night in which Raffles steals his own criminal relics from Scotland Yard's Black Museum, with Bunny Manders learning from Inspector Druce of Raffles's death volunteering for the Boer War.

Lady Lochmaben, the wife of Lord Lochmaben (Hector Carruthers) who keeps her diamonds in the same place as Mrs. Carruthers once did, making her a target considered by Raffles in Out of Paradise from A Thief in the Night.
Hector Carruthers, who rises to become Lord Lochmaben on the Birthday Honours list and appears in Out of Paradise from A Thief in the Night.
Crawshay, called 'the very prince of professional cracksmen,' a dangerous career criminal who appears in The Chest of Silver from A Thief in the Night and whose proximity is a warning to Raffles.
Colonel Crutchley, a Royal Engineers officer and Victoria Cross winner (Rorke's Drift) who becomes the victim of an outrage at his Campden Hill residence in The Rest Cure.
Lord Thornaby, an earl who hosts the Criminologists' Club dinner at Thornaby House, during which Raffles burrows his robes in The Criminologists' Club.
Nipper Nasmyth, a former head of school and trustee of the Founder's Fund who commits a bank robbery, confronted by Raffles in The Field of Phillipi.
Young Medlicott, an ailing young man in A Bad Night from A Thief in the Night, who lies helpless and breathing faintly while Bunny attends to him.

Barney Maguire, the notorious American heavy-weight boxing champion whose trophies Raffles attempts to steal while Maguire is still drunk with his latest victory, as described in A Trap to Catch a Cracksman.
Mr. Guillemard, an enormously built man with a red face who owns a country house whose old silver is targeted by Raffles in The Spoils of Sacrilege.
Inspector Druce, a Scotland Yard detective known for the Chalk Farm case, who pursues A. J. Raffles in The Raffles Relics.

Bunny's unnamed beloved, a woman dearer to Bunny than Raffles himself, whose name is deliberately withheld throughout A Thief in the Night to prevent it from being sullied by association with the criminals' story.
Raffles's attempted burglary of a house in Palace Gardens, Kensington, targeting the Lochmaben diamonds, which ultimately fails as nothing is taken, recounted in Out of Paradise.
A scheme by A. J. Raffles in which he concealed himself inside an iron-clamped silver chest in the strong-room of the City and Suburban Bank in Sloane Street, as described in The Chest of Silver.
Raffles's scheme of secretly occupying Colonel Crutchley's house near Campden Hill under the pretense of a self-styled rest cure, without actually stealing anything, as told in The Rest Cure.

The Thornaby House robes burglary, a Raffles escapade in The Criminologists' Club in which Raffles steals Lord Thornaby's peer's robes and coronet from his Park Lane house during the evening of a criminologists' dinner.
Raffles's robbery of just over a hundred sovereigns from Nipper Nasmyth's bank funds—specifically the Founder's Fund subscription—as a form of private justice, recounted in The Field of Phillipi.

Raffles's burglary of the numerous and intrinsically valuable wedding presents stored at a riverside retreat on the Mole at East Molesey, in which he takes emerald links and a diamond comb, recounted in A Bad Night.

The Barney Maguire trophies theft, a Raffles escapade in A Trap to Catch a Cracksman in which Raffles turns a prize-fighter's drugged-whiskey trap to his own advantage and steals Maguire's collection of trophies.
The Scotland Yard Black Museum Raffles Relics theft, the episode in The Raffles Relics in which Raffles breaks into Scotland Yard and steals back his own criminal tools from the Black Museum display.
Raffles's false-bottomed silver chest hiding device in The Chest of Silver and The Raffles Relics in A Thief in the Night, fitted with a secret inner lid opened by pressing one of the handles down.

Raffles's rooftop entry technique in 'The Criminologists' Club', combining a Manila rope with foot-loops worn around his waist and a telescopic hook fashioned from a series of diminishing canes screwed together.

Raffles's tatterdemalion vagrant and workman disguise in The Rest Cure in A Thief in the Night, used to move around Kensington unrecognised while secretly occupying a targeted house over an extended period.
Raffles's invention in 'The Field of Phillipi' of a thick velvet bag with two elastic mouths to muffle the sound of key-filing, allowing him to cut keys in silence.
Raffles's use of his fame as a first-class England cricketer as social cover in Out of Paradise in A Thief in the Night, keeping himself visible in respectable society while conducting burglaries.
A cover story prepared by Raffles and executed by Bunny in 'A Bad Night', whereby Bunny poses as an insurance detective sent by a Burglary Insurance Company to deflect suspicion during a break-in.

A method used by Raffles in 'A Trap to Catch a Cracksman' whereby he discovers and reverses a drugged whiskey trap set for burglars by switching the decanter labels, turning the device against its setter.
Raffles's escape method in 'The Spoils of Sacrilege' of descending from a building via its lightning conductor, wearing white kid gloves for grip.
Palace Gardens, Kensington, a street of grand politicians' houses in Out of Paradise in A Thief in the Night, the scene of Raffles's burglary of a pompous politician's Lochmaben diamond.
The City and Suburban Bank on Sloane Street, London, site of a brazen strong-room burglary and violent assault in The Chest of Silver in A Thief in the Night.
Colonel Crutchley's residence on Peter Street, Campden Hill, London, secretly occupied by Raffles during The Rest Cure in A Thief in the Night while he stages a prolonged internal burglary.
Thornaby House on Park Lane in The Criminologists' Club in A Thief in the Night, the venue of a Criminologists' Club dinner from which Raffles steals the visiting guests' robes.
Half-moon Street, Mayfair, where boxing promoter Barney Maguire furnished a house in A Trap to Catch a Cracksman in A Thief in the Night, the scene of the drugged-whisky burglary.
East Molesey on the Surrey banks of the River Mole in A Bad Night in A Thief in the Night, where Raffles targets wedding presents at a secluded family house on the night of a wedding.
The Scotland Yard Black Museum, where artefacts from famous criminal cases—including Raffles relics such as the iron-clamped silver chest—are exhibited, as described in The Raffles Relics.
Ham Common on the Surrey-Middlesex border in A Thief in the Night, the idyllic final base from which Raffles and Bunny operate their last series of burglaries before Raffles enlists for the Boer War.

The iron-clamped silver chest used by Raffles to conceal himself and infiltrate the City and Suburban Bank strong-room in The Chest of Silver, later exhibited as a Raffles relic in the Scotland Yard Black Museum.
The Raffles Relics, a collection of A. J. Raffles's burglary tools — including a revolver, life-preserver, brace-and-bit, and bottles — displayed at Scotland Yard's Black Museum after his death.

The three trophies belonging to the American boxer Barney Maguire in 'A Trap to Catch a Cracksman': a jewelled belt presented by the State of Nevada, a Sacramento gold brick, and a silver statuette, all taken by Raffles from under Maguire's drugged nose.
Lady Guillemard's diamond tiara and emerald-and-diamond necklace in 'The Spoils of Sacrilege', stolen by Raffles from her country house.

Lord Thornaby's full peer's robes and coronet in 'The Criminologists' Club', stolen by Raffles during a dinner hosted at Thornaby House and deposited at the Charing Cross cloak-room, with the ticket sent anonymously to Lord Thornaby.

A decanter of drugged whiskey set as a trap for burglars by Barney Maguire in 'A Trap to Catch a Cracksman', the poison obtained from a Native American source and described as ticklish stuff, which Raffles neutralises by switching labels.
Sullivan cigarettes, the one brand exclusively smoked by Raffles in 'Out of Paradise', used as a character marker and recognised immediately by a shop clerk.

The Criminologists' Club in 'The Criminologists' Club', a small and select London dining society for students of crime who meet without a fixed address, whose members invite Raffles to dine and are robbed by him the same night.
Bunny Manders's driving motive in the Raffles stories, expressed in 'Out of Paradise' as personal love for Raffles himself rather than attraction to the criminal life or its rewards.

Raffles's redistributive motive in 'The Field of Phillipi': robbing old Nasmyth to fund the Founder's Fund, contributing twenty-five pounds of his own immediately on returning to town, while refusing to let Bunny share in the execution.
Bunny Manders's role as retrospective narrator of the Raffles saga, recounting his criminal adventures with Raffles from their earliest days together and filling in gaps across all four collections.

Raffles's vagrant and shabby-beard disguise in The Rest Cure from A Thief in the Night, combining a grimy face with a dexterously applied growth of reddish hair and worn-out clothes to pass unrecognised.

Raffles's riding-suit disguise in The Spoils of Sacrilege from A Thief in the Night, worn over his own clothes during the Guillemard escape and supplemented by a youth's kit for Bunny, successfully passing a police officer at the station.
Raffles volunteers for service in the Boer War and dies at the front, as related in The Raffles Relics and The Last Word, his death seen as a final act of atonement.
In The Raffles Relics, items taken from Raffles's Albany rooms after his death are displayed in Scotland Yard's Black Museum as the most complete set of a criminal's tools ever exhibited.
In The Raffles Relics, Scotland Yard officers describe Raffles as the greatest burglar of his era, a master who knocked out the best pre-Raffleite thief before him.
Maguire's man-trap devised to catch the cleverest cracksman alive, which ultimately ensnares A. J. Raffles in A Trap to Catch a Cracksman.
Mr. Justice Raffles, a 1909 Raffles novel by E. W. Hornung in which Raffles takes vigilante justice against the moneylender Dan Levy to protect the indebted Garland family.

Dan Levy, a ruthless moneylender and the principal antagonist of Mr. Justice Raffles, who ensnares the Garland family in usurious debt and is ultimately kidnapped and coerced into restitution by Raffles.

Teddy Garland, a young Cambridge cricketer in Mr. Justice Raffles who falls into Dan Levy's debt trap through a cheque forgery attempt, prompting Raffles to take altruistic action against the moneylender.

Camilla Belsize, a young woman in Mr. Justice Raffles who is engaged to Teddy Garland and refuses to be released from the engagement despite his debts to Dan Levy, her loyalty prompting Raffles's intervention.

Mr. Garland senior, the gentle, elderly father in Mr. Justice Raffles whose Kensington house and business assets have fallen entirely under the control of the moneylender Dan Levy through years of usurious debt.
Mackenzie, the incorrigibly Scottish Scotland Yard detective who pursues Raffles across multiple cases and hurries to Dan Levy's riverside house after his murder in Mr. Justice Raffles.
The foreign assassin, an unnamed man of Latin appearance who pursues Dan Levy to his riverside house and kills him, as recounted in Mr. Justice Raffles.
Lady Laura Belsize, an apparently impecunious widow living in semi-detached circumstances, described as Camilla's mother in Mr. Justice Raffles.

The Maharajah of Hathipur, an Indian prince who has been in the financial clutches of the moneylender Dan Levy for years, whose debt Levy uses to increase his hold over him, mentioned in Mr. Justice Raffles.

The Dan Levy emeralds affair, the opening incident of Mr. Justice Raffles in which Raffles glimpses a valuable emerald necklace under Levy's table at a cricket match at Lord's and allows the moneylender to suspect he has retrieved it.

The Garland debt rescue, a subplot of Mr. Justice Raffles in which Raffles sets out to extricate young Teddy Garland from the financial entanglement with the moneylender Dan Levy, the immediate spur to Raffles's campaign of private justice.

The Burroughs and Burroughs letter theft, an episode in Mr. Justice Raffles in which Raffles breaks into Gray's Inn Square to steal back a compromising letter that Dan Levy has used to blackmail through the magazine Fact.

The kidnapping of Dan Levy, an episode in Mr. Justice Raffles in which Raffles holds the moneylender captive in a tower house on the Thames until he agrees to release the Garland family from their usurious debt.

The usury and debt entrapment of the Garlands, the scheme in Mr. Justice Raffles by which the moneylender Dan Levy ensnares Mr. Garland senior and his son Teddy in escalating promissory notes, taking possession of their Kensington house.
Raffles's theft of the Levy emerald necklace at Carlsbad, accomplished by sliding from his balcony to the one below and threatening the occupant in low German, as told in Mr. Justice Raffles.
Dan Levy's scheme involving a forged letter written on his own office paper purporting to be the crucial document in the libel case against Fact magazine, in Mr. Justice Raffles.

Raffles's scheme of private justice in Mr. Justice Raffles, in which he formally indicts Dan Levy for extortion, sharp practice, cheating, and misleading his customers, demanding financial restitution from him.

Teddy Garland's attempted cheque forgery in Mr. Justice Raffles, discovered when Bunny finds him laboriously copying Raffles's signature into his cheque-book, with a sheet of trial signatures beside him.
The murder of moneylender Dan Levy, shot at the front gates of his riverside house and stabbed by a foreign assassin taking revenge, as reported in Mr. Justice Raffles.

The Cambridge University versus Oxford cricket match at Lord's, at which E. M. Garland keeps wicket for Cambridge and which is mentioned in connection with the Garland debt affair in Mr. Justice Raffles.
Dan Levy's assault on Raffles at the riverside, hurling a glass of crude spirit full in his face and seizing the crucial forged letter back during a confrontation in Mr. Justice Raffles.
Dan Levy's reluctant signing of the mortgage indenture for fifteen thousand pounds in the sound-proof tower room, secured by Raffles's coercion, in Mr. Justice Raffles.
Raffles and Bunny's escape by hansom and then across Europe following the resolution of the Dan Levy affair, setting out on a journey around the world in Mr. Justice Raffles.
In Mr Justice Raffles, Teddy Garland borrowed £300 from Dan Levy for one year and now owes £700 through usurious compounding, the debt Raffles schemes to eliminate.
In Mr Justice Raffles, Dan Levy gloats that the Garland Kensington house and every asset the family appears to own has legally been his through usurious debt for months and years.
In Mr Justice Raffles, it emerges that Dan Levy was murdered not by Raffles but by a foreign creditor who had been lying in wait for him first in Jermyn Street and then on the night of his death.
In Mr Justice Raffles, Dan Levy is revealed to be pursuing a libel action against the magazine Fact over its exposure of his dealings with the Maharajah of Hathipur.
In Mr Justice Raffles, Raffles confesses he was romantically drawn to Camilla Belsize but deliberately stepped aside and brought her together with Teddy Garland.
Raffles's technique in 'Mr Justice Raffles' of taking a sheet of paper and forging a copy of an existing forgery, enabling him to substitute documents and expose or neutralise the original fraud.
Raffles's desperate tactic in 'Mr Justice Raffles' of kidnapping a traitor to compel a legal agreement, a measure he had foreseen and pre-planned against anticipated treachery.

Raffles's technique of immediately changing disguise on returning to the Albany — taking a hansom to the Burlington Gardens entrance and altering his appearance inside — used to evade detection in Mr. Justice Raffles.
Somnol sedation method used by A. J. Raffles in Mr. Justice Raffles, described as 'the latest thing out,' administered covertly in whiskey to incapacitate a victim.
Raffles's torpedo-beard canoeist disguise in Mr. Justice Raffles Chapter XII, in which he poses as a man who has been down the river in a Canadian canoe to explain his movements and evade suspicion.
Raffles's disguise as a well-known military man about town, used in Chapter XVI of Mr. Justice Raffles to enter a bank without arousing suspicion.
Dan Levy's Jermyn Street office, where the moneylender receives clients at an enormous desk and where Raffles and Teddy Garland negotiate a rescue from debt in Mr. Justice Raffles.
Lord's Cricket Ground, where Raffles and Bunny meet to watch the university match in Mr Justice Raffles, the historic ground that is Raffles's natural social habitat as a celebrated cricketer.

The Garland family's Kensington house set in its own grounds in Mr Justice Raffles, effectively owned by Dan Levy through years of usurious debt, Levy's hold confirmed when he declares the freehold his own.
Dan Levy's substantial mansion set on the riverbank beyond wooden gates, where Raffles is assaulted by Levy and where the foreign assassin ultimately murders Levy in Mr. Justice Raffles.
An unoccupied tower house on the Thames, moored to by Raffles and used as a sound-proof location to coerce Dan Levy into signing the mortgage deed in Mr. Justice Raffles.
Gray's Inn Square, Fetter Lane, where the offices of the solicitors Burroughs and Burroughs are located and from which Raffles steals a letter in Mr. Justice Raffles.
Carlsbad, the Bohemian spa town where Raffles sends a telegram and where Dan Levy and his family go for an annual cure, the starting point of the events in Mr. Justice Raffles.

Lady Laura Belsize's semi-detached riverside cottage in Mr Justice Raffles, a dingy stucco property near the Thames to which she retired in impoverished widowhood and where her daughter Camilla resides.
The Levy emerald necklace, a fine piece of jewellery stolen by Raffles at Carlsbad from under Dan Levy's possession, central to the opening of Mr. Justice Raffles.

Teddy Garland's promissory note in 'Mr Justice Raffles', by which he borrowed three hundred pounds from Dan Levy and signed for four hundred and fifty-six, later retrieved by Raffles as evidence against Levy's usurious practices.

The crucial letter held by solicitors Burroughs and Burroughs by which Dan Levy blackmails a client, stolen by Raffles at considerable personal risk as the linchpin of the justice scheme in Mr. Justice Raffles.
A parchment mortgage indenture deed signed by Dan Levy in Mr. Justice Raffles while held captive in a tower, charging a £15,000 sum upon the security of the Garland estate.

Raffles's favourite Manila rope in 'Mr Justice Raffles', noted for its silken flexibility combined with the strength of a hawser, used to descend from and ascend to upper-storey windows during burglaries.
Somnol sedative pills in 'Mr Justice Raffles', described by Raffles as the latest and best thing out, shaken from a tiny bottle and used to drug Dan Levy's food or drink before the kidnapping.

Dan Levy's plated bulldog revolver in 'Mr Justice Raffles', drawn from a desk pedestal and flourished in the electric light when Raffles and Bunny confront him, then countered by Raffles's physical speed.

An enamel coat button recovered by Bunny from an empty house in 'Mr Justice Raffles', identified by Miss Belsize as belonging to Camilla and serving as physical evidence of her presence at the scene of Dan Levy's kidnapping.
Teddy Garland's promissory note and instalment receipts in Mr Justice Raffles, the documentary proof of Dan Levy's usurious lending scheme that Raffles seeks to recover.
A signed confession from Levy acknowledging everything he suborned Raffles's associate to do, obtained by Raffles and used as leverage in Mr Justice Raffles.
A cheque for 1,500 guineas from Dan Levy in Mr Justice Raffles, forming part of the financial evidence Raffles assembles to expose Levy's usurious scheme.

Raffles's stated motive in 'Mr Justice Raffles': refusing to be beaten by a brute like Levy, combined with a practical assessment that kidnapping carried less risk than other methods of settling the debt.

Dan Levy's business motive in 'Mr Justice Raffles': keeping debtors in perpetual arrears because it is the interest rather than the principal that matters to him, giving him continuing power over those he has ensnared.
The motive in 'Mr Justice Raffles': a foreign assassin's personal grievance against Dan Levy for scandalous treatment, escalating from threatening letters to a direct attempt on Levy's life.
Burroughs and Burroughs, the Gray's Inn Square solicitors described in Mr. Justice Raffles as the strongest firm in London and acting as legal counsel to the blackmail magazine Fact.
Fact, a magazine in 'Mr Justice Raffles' that exposes the Maharajah of Hathipur affair and prints letters which Dan Levy claims are forgeries.

Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman, an E. W. Hornung short-story collection presenting more of Raffles and Bunny's escapades, including a burglary at the British Museum and a confrontation with the Camorra.

Raffles's persona as Mr. Maturin, a dying Australian invalid, under which he rents a flat at Earl's Court and advertises for a companion while hiding from the law after his presumed death in No Sinecure.

The Ralph colonial persona adopted by Raffles in The Wrong House from Raffles: Further Adventures, presenting himself to their landlady as Bunny's brother Ralph, newly returned from Australia with a curious affliction.
No Sinecure, a Raffles story in Further Adventures in which Raffles and Bunny return under disguise and carry out a duplicate cigarette-box jewel swindle after their earlier disappearance.
A Jubilee Present, a story in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles and Bunny steal a gold cup from the British Museum's Room of Gold.
The Fate of Faustina, a Raffles story in Further Adventures set near the Bay of Naples in August in which Raffles takes vengeance on Count Corbucci for the murder of a woman named Faustina.

The Last Laugh, a Raffles story in Further Adventures in which Count Corbucci springs a clock-pistol revenge trap on Raffles, who escapes using a flask of cyanide of cacodyl before Corbucci can trigger the mechanism.

To Catch a Thief, a story in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles unmasks Lord Ernest Belville as the perpetrator of a series of audacious society house burglaries carried out from the inside.
An Old Flame, a story in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles stages his own death and funeral to escape a French antagonist named Jacques Saillard.

The Wrong House, a story in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles and Bunny conduct a series of Thames Valley bicycle burglaries and accidentally break into a house belonging to Bunny's brother Ralph.

The Knees of the Gods, a Raffles story in Further Adventures in which Raffles and Bunny enlist in an irregular horse unit during the Boer War, and Raffles arranges to sabotage a rival soldier's horse.
Dr. Theobald, Mr. Maturin's physician in the Raffles story No Sinecure, who arranges a male nurse and attendant for his patient and is about to be married.
Faustina, a young Italian woman of exceptional beauty from an inland family near the Bay of Naples, who dies tragically at Count Corbucci's vineyard in The Fate of Faustina.

Count Corbucci, a former high-ranking Camorra boss and the antagonist of The Fate of Faustina and The Last Laugh in the Raffles Further Adventures, who plots an elaborate clock-pistol revenge trap against Raffles.

Stefano, Count Corbucci's small, ill-favoured confidential body-servant who betrayed the count's ward Faustina, killed by Raffles in a smoky cave as an act of personal vengeance in The Fate of Faustina.

Lord Ernest Belville, a good-looking iron-jawed temperance advocate known as the apostle of Rational Drink, exposed by Raffles as the inside man behind a series of society house burglaries in To Catch a Thief.
Jacques Saillard, a dark and handsome woman of formidable will who operates under a man's name and whom Raffles fears, encountered by him in An Old Flame.
Corporal Connal, a Glasgow-Irish man long resident in South Africa who acts as a Boer spy in the British lines and sabotages horses, identified by Raffles as a spy in The Knees of the Gods.
Captain Bellingham, a cricketer known for his batting averages who appears in The Knees of the Gods and is recognised by Raffles in disguise.

Raffles's jewel swindle in No Sinecure, substituting a pre-packed duplicate cigarette-box for a jeweller's open display box to steal a diamond ring, an emerald necklace, and a diamond star worth nearly £400 in total.

The British Museum gold cup theft (Jubilee gift), a Raffles escapade in Further Adventures in which Raffles steals the ancient gold cup from the Room of Gold on a momentary impulse and eventually sends it to the Queen as a loyal Jubilee present.

Lord Ernest Belville's series of audacious burglaries at London society houses carried out from the inside as a guest, the stolen diamonds concealed in hollow Indian clubs, exposed by Raffles in To Catch a Thief.

The Camorra revenge trap (Corbucci's clock-pistol), the elaborate revenge scheme in The Last Laugh in which Count Corbucci lashes Raffles to a ring-bolt and constructs a grandfather-clock mechanism set to fire a pistol at midnight.
A series of bicycle-assisted night burglaries carried out by Raffles across the Thames Valley from Richmond upward one winter, using a greased Beeston Humber bicycle, as recounted in The Wrong House.
Corporal Connal's act of sabotage on behalf of the Boers by driving British cavalry horses towards the enemy lines, for which he is shot in The Knees of the Gods.
Raffles's killing of Stefano in Count Corbucci's smoky cave using a revolver, enacted as personal vengeance for Stefano's betrayal of the count's ward Faustina, described in The Fate of Faustina.
Raffles's elaborately staged death and sham funeral in An Old Flame in the Further Adventures, engineered with a bribed doctor and nurse and a library of books buried at Kensal Green in his place.

The event in which Raffles and Bunny Manders travel to the Cape of their own accord and enlist in a regiment of irregular horse to fight in the Boer War, with Raffles dyeing his hair ginger as a disguise, as described in The Knees of the Gods.
The death of A. J. Raffles during the Boer War, shot on the South African veldt while serving in an irregular horse unit alongside Bunny, in The Knees of the Gods.

Bunny wounded in the leg on the veldt during the Boer War in The Knees of the Gods from Raffles: Further Adventures, the bullet drilling through his thigh bone and leaving him more or less lame for life.

The Camorra, a powerful and sinister Neapolitan criminal organisation featuring in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman, whose London network of organ-grinders was directed by the capo paranzo Count Corbucci to track Raffles.
The flat at Earl's Court rented by Raffles under the invalid persona of Mr. Maturin, used as his hiding place and base of operations after his supposed death, in No Sinecure.
The Room of Gold in the British Museum, the setting of Raffles's audacious jubilee gold-cup theft in A Jubilee Present in the Further Adventures, approximately 139 yards from the open street.

Count Corbucci's vineyard near the Bay of Naples, a sea-girt wedge of rock thatched with vines on the site of a reputed retreat of Tiberius, where the young Italian woman Faustina meets her fate in The Fate of Faustina.

A cottage on the edge of Ham Common in the Thames Valley rented by Bunny and his brother Ralph, which Raffles uses as a base for his bicycle burglaries after his return from the Boer War in The Wrong House.
The South African veldt during the Boer War, where Raffles and Bunny serve in an irregular horse unit and where Raffles is fatally shot under withering fire in The Knees of the Gods.

A thin, enamel-decorated gold cup from the Museum's Room of Gold, stolen by Raffles in 'A Jubilee Present' and anonymously returned to Queen Victoria via Sir Arthur Bigge as the loyal respects of the thief.
