Lord Ernest Belville's hollow Indian exercise clubs in 'To Catch a Thief', which Raffles discovers are not the same weight and proves on opening to be filled with diamonds and pearls rather than solid wood.

A small flask of cyanide of cacodyl carried by Raffles as a last resort in 'The Last Laugh', described as the deadliest poison known to science, calculated to kill an ordinary roomful of people within five minutes.

Count Corbucci's revenge device in 'The Last Laugh': a grandfather clock fitted with an electric mechanism connecting the hour-hand to a revolver mounted in a vice below the dial, set to fire at the stroke of twelve.

Raffles's motive in 'The Knees of the Gods': a straightforward patriotic desire to serve his country during the Boer War, expressed in the motto 'Dulce et decorum est' when he and Bunny enlist in an irregular unit.

Count Corbucci's vendetta against Raffles in 'The Last Laugh', sworn to repay in kind the humiliation Raffles inflicted on him, backed by the Camorra's threat to kill Raffles if he killed Corbucci first.
Raffles's concealment method in 'No Sinecure' of posing as an invalid in a bath-chair to move about in daylight while officially presumed dead and unable to show his face outdoors.
A. J. Raffles's technique of switching a genuine cigarette box for a pre-packed duplicate, used in the No Sinecure jewel swindle without either victim noticing the exchange.

Bicycle-assisted night burglary method used by A. J. Raffles in the Thames Valley, involving Beeston Humber bicycles with Dunlop tyres, vaseline-coated plated parts to avoid reflection, and an electric torch fitted with a dark hood.
In the Further Adventures, Raffles's hair has turned completely white — no trick — a change he attributes to the long Mediterranean swim after his staged death and Italian exile.
In the Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman, Bunny has already served eighteen months of imprisonment (at Wormwood Scrubbs and a Holloway attic) before the new stories begin.
In the Further Adventures, Raffles lives concealed, knowing the world and Scotland Yard alike believe him drowned in the Mediterranean, his name belonging to a dead man.
His Last Bow, an Arthur Conan Doyle short-story collection that includes Holmes's wartime counter-espionage mission against the German agent Von Bork and several earlier untold cases.
Arthur Conan Doyle, the British author who created Sherlock Holmes and Dr. Watson and wrote all the Holmes novels and short-story collections.
The Sherlock Holmes saga, the complete body of detective fiction by Arthur Conan Doyle featuring Sherlock Holmes and Dr. Watson, encompassing four novels and five short-story collections.

Mycroft Holmes, Sherlock Holmes's elder brother, a government official of omniscient knowledge whose work takes him between his Pall Mall lodgings, the Diogenes Club, and Whitehall, and who occasionally assists in critical cases.

Von Bork, a German agent described as barely matchable among the Kaiser's operatives, who ran a spy network from his English country house and acquired the British Naval Signals codebook before being arrested by Sherlock Holmes in His Last Bow.
Baron Von Herling, the chief secretary of the German legation in His Last Bow, who arrives in his 100-horse-power Benz car confident that Germany will be at war with Britain within a week.
Martha, Holmes's spy-housekeeper whom he placed in Von Bork's household to gather intelligence, the only servant he retains during his undercover operation in His Last Bow.
John Scott Eccles, a figure of conventional British respectability lured to Wisteria Lodge by Don Juan Murillo as a useful unwitting witness, in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge.

Aloysius Garcia, son of a former high dignitary of San Pedro, who is found murdered near Esher with his head smashed, having been killed by Don Murillo's henchman while organising a revenge plot in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge.

Don Juan Murillo (alias Henderson), the fugitive 'Tiger of San Pedro' in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge, a former Central American dictator of notorious brutality who flees justice under a false identity in England.

Miss Burnet, whose real name is Signora Victor Durando and who was the wife of the San Pedro minister in London, who posed as governess in Don Murillo's household to track his movements for the revenge group in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge.
Inspector Baynes, of the Surrey Constabulary, whose instinct and intuition earn Holmes's admiration as he independently investigates the Wisteria Lodge case.

Arthur Cadogan West, a 27-year-old clerk at Woolwich Arsenal in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans, who discovered the theft of the submarine plans but was killed before he could raise the alarm.
Colonel Valentine Walter, who sold the Bruce-Partington submarine plans to the spy Oberstein to pay a Stock Exchange debt, exposed in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans.

Hugo Oberstein, a foreign spy resident at 13 Caulfield Gardens, Kensington, who buys stolen Bruce-Partington submarine plans and is sentenced to fifteen years in prison, in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans.
Miss Violet Westbury, the fiancée of Arthur Cadogan West in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans, who is the last person to see him alive when he leaves her abruptly in the fog.

Mortimer Tregennis, an independent gentleman lodging with a Cornish clergyman who uses Devil's Foot root to drive his siblings mad, making him the criminal in the first tragedy of The Adventure of the Devil's Foot.

Dr. Leon Sterndale, the great lion-hunter and explorer who secretly loved Brenda Tregennis and poisons Mortimer Tregennis with Devil's Foot root in revenge for her death, in The Adventure of the Devil's Foot.
Gennaro Lucca, an Italian-American from New York who kills the Black Hand leader Giuseppe Gorgiano to protect his wife Emilia, in The Adventure of the Red Circle.

Emilia Lucca, a Neapolitan woman born in Posilippo and daughter of Augusto Barelli, who identifies herself as a New York resident entangled in the Red Circle affair in The Adventure of the Red Circle.

Giuseppe Gorgiano, nicknamed Black Gorgiano, a giant Neapolitan who earned the name 'Death' in southern Italy for his record of murders, killed by Gennaro Lucca while running a Red Circle blackmail campaign in The Adventure of the Red Circle.

Lady Frances Carfax, the sole surviving member of the direct family of the Earl of Rufton, who disappears with her remarkable Spanish jewellery and is nearly buried alive in Peters's coffin-burial scheme, in The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax.

Henry Peters, known as Holy Peters, one of the most unscrupulous rogues in Australia who poses as the missionary Rev. Dr. Shlessinger and plots to bury Lady Frances Carfax alive in an oversized coffin, investigated by Holmes in The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax.
The Hon. Philip Green, a fellow-lodger who had known Lady Frances Carfax in her youth and whose reckless past had driven her away, becoming a key witness in The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax.
Culverton Smith, a planter from Sumatra visiting London who uses a deadly tropical disease to murder his nephew Victor Savage, the antagonist in The Adventure of the Dying Detective.

The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge, a His Last Bow story in which Holmes and Inspector Baynes investigate the fugitive Central American dictator Don Juan Murillo, alias Henderson, and the murder of his associate Aloysius Garcia.

The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans, a His Last Bow story set in 1895 in which Holmes and Mycroft investigate the theft of submarine blueprints from Woolwich Arsenal and the murder of Arthur Cadogan West.

The Adventure of the Devil's Foot, a story in His Last Bow set during Holmes's 1897 Cornwall convalescence, in which he investigates the poisoning of the Tregennis siblings by Mortimer Tregennis using Devil's Foot root.

The Adventure of the Red Circle, a His Last Bow story in which Holmes and Inspector Gregson investigate a mysterious lodger in Mrs. Warren's Bloomsbury house and uncover the Italian criminal Giuseppe Gorgiano's pursuit of Gennaro and Emilia Lucca.

The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax, a His Last Bow story in which Holmes investigates the vanishing of a wealthy English lady, eventually tracing her to Henry Peters (Holy Peters) and a drugged-coffin scheme.
The Adventure of the Dying Detective, a Sherlock Holmes story from His Last Bow in which Holmes feigns serious illness to trap the culpable Culverton Smith.

His Last Bow: The War Service of Sherlock Holmes, the title story of the collection set on the night of 2 August 1914 in which Holmes, working undercover as an Irish-American spy named Altamont, delivers the German agent Von Bork to British justice.

The Wisteria Lodge and Oxshott murder case, in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge, in which Holmes investigates the death of Aloysius Garcia near Esher and uncovers the revenge plot against the deposed San Pedro tyrant Don Juan Murillo.

The Bruce-Partington Plans theft, the crime in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans in which seven of the ten submarine blueprint pages are found in the pocket of the murdered clerk Arthur Cadogan West, with the three most essential pages still missing.

The Cornish Horror (Devil's Foot poisoning), a His Last Bow case in which Holmes and Watson, convalescing in Cornwall, investigate a family apparently struck mad by a mysterious agent that proves to be the fumes of the devil's-foot root.

The Red Circle / Gorgiano affair investigated by Sherlock Holmes in His Last Bow, involving the feared Gorgiano, held responsible for at least fifty murders and a leader of the Red Circle secret society.
The disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax, a case investigated by Sherlock Holmes in His Last Bow in which nearly five weeks pass without any word of the missing woman.

The Culverton Smith murder plot in The Adventure of the Dying Detective, in which Sherlock Holmes feigns near-fatal illness from a tropical disease to lure Culverton Smith into confessing the murder of Victor Savage.

The Von Bork German espionage operation, the case in His Last Bow in which Sherlock Holmes, working undercover for two years, dismantles Von Bork's east-coast spy network and delivers the German agent's dossiers to British intelligence on the eve of the First World War.

The murder of Aloysius Garcia near Esher by Don Murillo's henchman Lopez, who ambushed him in the gorse bushes to prevent the revenge plot against Murillo, as investigated by Holmes in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge.

The theft and attempted sale of the secret Bruce-Partington submarine plans, orchestrated by Hugo Oberstein and Colonel Valentine Walter, as investigated by Sherlock Holmes in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans.

Mortimer Tregennis's poisoning of his siblings with Devil's Foot root (Radix pedis diaboli), a substance that stimulates the brain centres governing fear and causes madness or death, investigated by Holmes in The Adventure of the Devil's Foot.
The Red Circle (La Mano Rossa) secret society's campaign of blackmailing wealthy Italians and murdering those who resist, led by Giuseppe Gorgiano, the subject of The Adventure of the Red Circle.

Henry Peters's scheme to chloroform Lady Frances Carfax and conceal her alive inside an oversized coffin intended for another corpse, foiled by Sherlock Holmes at the last moment in The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax.

A German espionage operation centred on Von Bork's country house in England, aimed at acquiring the British Naval Signals codebook, foiled by Sherlock Holmes in His Last Bow: The War Service of Sherlock Holmes.

The Bruce-Partington submarine plans, the most jealously guarded British government secret in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans, comprising thirty separate patents essential to the submarine's working.

Radix pedis diaboli, a West African root shaped like a half-human, half-goatlike foot in 'The Adventure of the Devil's Foot', used by Dr. Sterndale and Tregennis as a poison that drives victims to madness and death when burned.

A copy of every British naval signals code—semaphore, lamp, and Marconi—obtained for Von Bork's collection in 'His Last Bow', described as the gem of his collection and the item for which the setting was prepared.
The disguise Holmes uses when approaching Von Bork in 'His Last Bow': a copy of Practical Handbook of Bee Culture wrapped in string and paper, presented as intelligence material but actually a decoy.

A small black-and-white ivory box with a sliding lid in 'The Adventure of the Dying Detective', sent to Holmes and containing a sharp spring loaded with a tropical disease agent, by which Culverton Smith intended to murder him.

Lady Frances Carfax's collection of old Spanish silver-and-diamond jewellery in 'The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax', to which she was fondly attached and which Peters pawned at Bovington's in Westminster Road after abducting her.
Von Bork's safe full of dossiers on British agents and military secrets in His Last Bow, revealed by Holmes to be largely filled with deliberately falsified intelligence fed to the spy ring.
Cadogan West's body found on the roof of a Metropolitan Railway carriage in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans, proving he was killed elsewhere and his corpse placed on the train.

A series of coded agony-column messages in the Daily Telegraph, signed 'Pierrot', decoded by Holmes to reveal the Bruce-Partington plans handover arrangements in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans.
Gravel on the window sill at the Tregennis vicarage in The Adventure of the Devil's Foot, identified by Holmes as the starting point of his research into the Cornish poisoning.
Residue of Devil's Foot root powder found on the talc smoke-guard of a lamp in The Adventure of the Devil's Foot, confirming the lamp was used to combust the lethal toxin.

A coded note to Garcia using the San Pedro national colours — 'green open, white shut' — to guide him through a house passage in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge, decoded by Holmes with Inspector Baynes's help.

Wisteria Lodge near Esher and Oxshott in Surrey in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge in His Last Bow, home of the murdered Aloysius Garcia and the focal point of Holmes's investigation into the fugitive San Pedro dictator.

Woolwich Arsenal in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans in His Last Bow, the government ordnance establishment where Cadogan West worked as a clerk and from which the stolen submarine plans were taken.

13 Caulfield Gardens, Kensington, the house of the international spy Hugo Oberstein in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans, whose railway-side rear wall was used to place Cadogan West's body on a passing carriage roof.
A small whitewashed cottage near Poldhu Bay at the tip of the Cornish peninsula, rented by Holmes and Watson for a working holiday that becomes the scene of the Devil's Foot poisoning mystery.
Von Bork's long, low, gabled country house on the east coast near Harwich in His Last Bow, the operational base of his German spy network and the scene of his chloroform capture by Holmes.

San Pedro, a fictional Central American republic in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge in His Last Bow, the source nation of the bloodthirsty dictator Don Juan Murillo who fled to England under the alias Henderson.

The Red Circle (La Mano Rossa), a Neapolitan secret society allied to the Camorra in 'The Adventure of the Red Circle', to which Gennaro Lucca had belonged in his youth and which pursues him to London on a lottery of assassination.

Von Bork's German espionage network in 'His Last Bow', centred on his quiet English country house and responsible for half the mischief in England, which has lost five agents since Holmes infiltrated it as Altamont.

Sherlock Holmes's stratagem in 'The Adventure of the Dying Detective' of feigning a deadly tropical illness for three days—without food and using cosmetic effects—in order to lure the murderer Culverton Smith into a confession.

Sherlock Holmes's technique of decoding messages placed in the agony columns of London newspapers, as used in 'The Adventure of the Red Circle' and 'The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans', with Holmes maintaining a filed collection of such columns.

Holmes's deduction that Cadogan West was killed elsewhere and his body placed on the roof of an underground railway carriage — not thrown from the embankment — based on the absence of surface marks, in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans.

Holmes's dangerous self-experiment of burning Devil's Foot powder over a lamp in The Adventure of the Devil's Foot to confirm its lethal properties, nearly killing both himself and Watson before they stagger to the window.

Holmes's two-year deep-cover operation as the Irish-American agent 'Altamont' in His Last Bow, beginning in Chicago and progressing through Irish secret societies before penetrating Von Bork's German espionage network.

Altamont, Holmes's Irish-American spy persona adopted for years in His Last Bow to penetrate German intelligence networks, described by Holmes himself as a fictional identity that vanished once its purpose was served.

The Rev. Dr. Shlessinger missionary persona used by the criminal Holy Peters in The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax, through which he gains the trust of Lady Frances at the Englischer Hof before abducting her.

The motive of Garcia and his circle in 'The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge': vengeance against Don Murillo, the Tiger of San Pedro, for atrocities committed during his tyrannical rule, including the murder of the narrator's husband.
Mortimer Tregennis's motive in 'The Adventure of the Devil's Foot': the calculation that if his siblings were driven insane he might become sole guardian of the family property.

Holmes's motive in 'His Last Bow': setting aside his retirement and bee-keeping on the South Downs to spend two years penetrating Von Bork's spy network out of patriotic duty before the coming German war.
Before the events of His Last Bow, Holmes had retired to a small farm on the South Downs five miles from Eastbourne, dividing his time between philosophy and bee-farming.
In His Last Bow, Holmes — operating undercover as the spy Altamont — chloroforms and captures German master spy Von Bork on the second of August 1914, the eve of the First World War.
Holmes proves in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans that clerk Cadogan West was a patriotic innocent who followed the thief to raise the alarm, not a traitor, and died in the attempt.
The Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes, the final Arthur Conan Doyle short-story collection, presenting twelve late adventures of Sherlock Holmes and Dr. Watson.
The Adventure of the Illustrious Client, a Case-Book story in which Holmes investigates the dangerous Austrian murderer Baron Adelbert Gruner at the request of a powerful unnamed client.

The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier, a Case-Book story narrated by Holmes himself in which he investigates the apparent disappearance of Godfrey Emsworth from his father's house and finds the young man concealing a case of pseudo-leprosy.

The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone, a Case-Book story in which Holmes uses a wax dummy and a gramophone to trick the jewel thief Count Negretto Sylvius into revealing the hiding place of the stolen Crown diamond.

The Adventure of the Three Gables, a Case-Book story set at Harrow Weald in which Holmes investigates a scheme by the wealthy Isadora Klein to purchase and destroy Douglas Maberley's manuscript that fictionalises their affair.

The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire, a Case-Book story in which Holmes investigates Robert Ferguson's apparent vampire wife and discovers she has been sucking curare poison from her baby's wounds, inflicted by her jealous stepson Jack Ferguson.

The Adventure of the Three Garridebs, a Case-Book story in which Holmes exposes the criminal Killer Evans's scheme to forge currency using Prescott's counterfeiting press hidden beneath Nathan Garrideb's floor.

The Problem of Thor Bridge, a Case-Book story in which Holmes investigates the apparent murder of the jealous Maria Gibson on the estate of gold magnate Neil Gibson and exonerates the governess Grace Dunbar.

The Adventure of the Creeping Man, a Case-Book story in which Holmes investigates the bizarre nocturnal behaviour of Professor Presbury, who has been injecting himself with a monkey-gland serum supplied by a Prague professor.

The Adventure of the Lion's Mane, a Case-Book story narrated by Holmes himself in which the bizarre death of swimming instructor Fitzroy McPherson near a Sussex shore is traced to a deadly Cyanea capillata jellyfish.

The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger, a Case-Book story in which Holmes hears the confession of the terribly disfigured Eugenia Ronder, who reveals that her lion-tamer husband was killed by the strongman Leonardo and she was mauled by the lion.

The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place, a Case-Book Sherlock Holmes story in which Holmes investigates the strange behaviour of Sir Robert Norberton at his country estate and uncovers his secret concealment of his sister Lady Beatrice Falder's death.

The Adventure of the Retired Colourman, a Case-Book story in which Holmes investigates the retired paint manufacturer Josiah Amberley, who claims his wife and her lover have vanished but is revealed to have murdered them in a gas-chamber he constructed himself.

Baron Adelbert Gruner, an Austrian murderer and the principal villain of The Adventure of the Illustrious Client, a dangerous and charming man who is ultimately disfigured by a vitriol attack carried out by Kitty Winter.

Violet de Merville, a young, rich, beautiful, and accomplished woman in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client who is dangerously attached to the villainous Baron Adelbert Gruner, whose planned marriage to him is eventually prevented.
Kitty Winter, a woman wronged by Baron Adelbert Gruner who throws vitriol in his face and faces a grave criminal charge, in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client.
Shinwell Johnson, a reformed criminal who served two terms at Parkhurst and now acts as Holmes's informant in the London underworld, assisting in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client.

Sir James Damery, a discreet intermediary who presents the compliments of an illustrious but unnamed client to Holmes in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client, noted for his ability to keep delicate matters out of the papers.

James M. Dodd, a South African veteran who visits Holmes in January 1903 just after the Boer War in The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier to investigate the disappearance of his friend Godfrey Emsworth.

Godfrey Emsworth, Colonel Emsworth's only son and a Lance-Corporal who returned from the Boer War displaying symptoms that alarmed his family and led to his concealment in The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier.
Colonel Emsworth, a Crimean V.C. described as the greatest martinet in the army in his day, who secretly confines his son Godfrey at home in The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier.
Sir James Saunders, the dermatologist brought in by Holmes in The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier who diagnoses Godfrey Emsworth's condition not as leprosy but as pseudo-leprosy or ichthyosis.

Count Negretto Sylvius, a big, swarthy man-about-town and noted game-shot living at 136 Moorside Gardens who steals the Mazarin Stone (crown diamond), pursued by Holmes in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone.

Sam Merton, a heavily built but not malicious prize-fighter who is duped into assisting Count Negretto Sylvius in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone, described by Holmes as not a shark but a great strong fool.

Lord Cantlemere, a stiff and sceptical government representative in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone who is subjected to Holmes's practical joke of producing the recovered diamond from Cantlemere's own coat pocket.

Isadora Klein, a wealthy woman of pure Spanish blood in The Adventure of the Three Gables who schemes to purchase and destroy the manuscript that fictionalises her affair with Douglas Maberley, in order to protect her advantageous marriage prospects.
Mary Maberley, the widow of Mortimer Maberley — an early client of Holmes — who experiences a series of strange incidents at Three Gables and consults Holmes in The Adventure of the Three Gables.
Steve Dixie, a member of the Spencer John gang who visits Holmes in a threatening manner in The Adventure of the Three Gables and has been involved in dirty work directed by Isadora Klein.

Robert Ferguson, a tea broker of Ferguson and Muirhead in Mincing Lane and a former rugby acquaintance of Watson's, who consults Holmes about his wife's strange behaviour in The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire.
Mrs. Ferguson, the Peruvian wife of Robert Ferguson in The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire, who is wrongly suspected of vampirism but whom Holmes declares a very good, loving, and ill-used woman.
Jack Ferguson, the son of Robert Ferguson's first marriage in The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire, whose intense hatred for his Peruvian stepmother leads him to secretly poison the baby.

Killer Evans, also known as James Winter and Morecroft, a forty-four-year-old Chicago-born murderer who impersonates a Garrideb claimant to gain access to a concealed counterfeiting press, investigated by Holmes in The Adventure of the Three Garridebs.

Nathan Garrideb, a tall, loose-jointed, round-backed collector of some sixty years living at 136 Little Ryder Street, who is deceived by the Three Garridebs scheme and whose house conceals a counterfeiting press in The Adventure of the Three Garridebs.
Neil Gibson, the Gold King and American senator in The Problem of Thor Bridge, who implores Holmes to save his wife's governess Grace Dunbar from a murder charge.
Grace Dunbar, the governess to Neil Gibson's two children who becomes the object of his affection and is framed for the murder of Maria Gibson at Thor Bridge, in The Problem of Thor Bridge.

Maria Gibson, née Maria Pinto, the Brazilian-born wife of Gold King Neil Gibson whose jealousy of the governess drives her to shoot herself on Thor Bridge and frame the governess with a planted note, as revealed by Holmes in The Problem of Thor Bridge.

Professor Presbury, a man of blameless European academic reputation whose nocturnal crawling on all fours is investigated by Holmes in The Adventure of the Creeping Man, where it is revealed he has been secretly self-injecting langur monkey serum in search of rejuvenation.

Trevor Bennett, a tall, handsome professional assistant to the great scientist Professor Presbury in The Adventure of the Creeping Man, who lives under the professor's roof and is engaged to his daughter.

Fitzroy McPherson, the science master at Harold Stackhurst's coaching establishment in The Adventure of the Lion's Mane, a fine young man whose life had been crippled by heart trouble, who dies suddenly crying out with his hands raised.
Harold Stackhurst, proprietor of the well-known coaching establishment The Gables, who is Holmes's closest neighbour on the Sussex coast and a trusted friend in The Adventure of the Lion's Mane.

Maud Bellamy, a remarkable woman of Fulworth with whom Fitzroy McPherson conducted an intimate correspondence, remembered by Holmes as a most complete and remarkable woman in The Adventure of the Lion's Mane.
Ian Murdoch, the tall, dark, taciturn mathematical coach at Harold Stackhurst's establishment who is initially a suspect in The Adventure of the Lion's Mane before being exonerated.

Eugenia Ronder, a circus performer who conspired with Leonardo the strongman to murder her brutal husband using a lion-paw club, and later lives as a veiled recluse, in The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger.

Leonardo, the magnificently built circus strongman and professional acrobat who fashions a lion-paw club to kill Ronder and is implicated in Eugenia Ronder's scheme, in The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger.

Sir Robert Norberton, one of the most daredevil riders in England and second in the Grand National, who conceals his dead sister's body to prevent forfeiture of a racing loan in The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place.
Lady Beatrice Falder, the widowed sister of Sir Robert Norberton who controls the Shoscombe estate, and whose death is secretly concealed by her brother in The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place.

Josiah Amberley, the retired colourman and murderer in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman who, in a jealous rage, kills his wife and her lover in a gas-chamber he constructs in his Essex property before feigning their disappearance.
Dr. Ray Ernest, a young chess-playing doctor from Lewisham who carries on an intimacy with Mrs. Amberley and is murdered alongside her by Josiah Amberley in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.

Holmes as narrator, a self-narrated role taken by Sherlock Holmes in two Case Book stories — The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier and The Adventure of the Lion's Mane — when Watson was unavailable and Holmes was obliged to write up the cases himself.

The Illustrious Client case, described in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client as the supreme moment of Holmes's career, in which he works to prevent a young woman from marrying the dangerous and murderous Baron Gruner.

The Blanched Soldier case, narrated by Holmes in The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier, in which he investigates Godfrey Emsworth's mysterious isolation and determines he has pseudo-leprosy (ichthyosis), not true leprosy.

The Mazarin Stone theft case, the crime in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone in which the hundred-thousand-pound Crown diamond is stolen by Count Negretto Sylvius, recovered by Holmes through the use of a wax dummy and hidden surveillance.

The Thor Bridge mystery, in The Problem of Thor Bridge, in which Holmes investigates the death of Maria Gibson found shot on a bridge with a note from the governess, revealing it to be a suicide staged to frame an innocent woman.

The Creeping Man case, in The Adventure of the Creeping Man, in which Holmes investigates Professor Presbury's alarming nocturnal crawling behaviour on a nine-day cycle and discovers he has been self-injecting a langur monkey rejuvenation serum.

The Lion's Mane case, narrated by the retired Holmes from his Sussex cottage at Fulworth, in which he investigates the mysterious agonised death of Fitzroy McPherson and identifies a Cyanea capillata jellyfish as the killer.

The Retired Colourman case, in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman, in which Holmes exposes Josiah Amberley for gassing his wife and her lover Dr. Ray Ernest to death in a sealed strong-room and burying them in his garden.

Kitty Winter's vitriol attack on Baron Adelbert Gruner, throwing the acid in his face from among the leaves outside his house, leading to her police-court prosecution in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client.

The theft of the Mazarin Stone, a great Crown diamond, by Count Negretto Sylvius in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone, who carries the stone in a secret pocket intending to have it cut and smuggled out of England before Holmes can recover it.
The Three Gables burglary in The Adventure of the Three Gables, in which Mrs. Maberley is chloroformed and the house ransacked on behalf of Isadora Klein in order to steal and destroy a manuscript.

The Three Garridebs counterfeit press scheme in The Adventure of the Three Garridebs, devised by Killer Evans to lure Nathan Garrideb away from his house in order to recover the hidden counterfeiting press beneath it.
Maria Gibson's scheme to frame Grace Dunbar for her own suicide at Thor Bridge, arranging a false note from Miss Dunbar and staging the scene to implicate her rival, in The Problem of Thor Bridge.

Professor Presbury's clandestine self-experimentation with a langur monkey serum from Lowenstein, causing him to exhibit creeping animal behaviour, investigated by Holmes in The Adventure of the Creeping Man.

Eugenia Ronder and Leonardo's murder of Mr. Ronder using a leaden club fitted with five long steel nails to simulate a lion's paw attack, inside the circus at night in The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger.

Sir Robert Norberton's scheme to conceal his sister's natural death by moving her body to the church crypt and using an actor to impersonate her, to avoid losing a racing loan, in The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place.

Josiah Amberley's double murder of his wife and Dr. Ray Ernest by feeding gas through a pipe into his sealed strong-room at The Haven in Lewisham, the victims leaving a dying scrawl 'We we----' on the wall, as solved by Holmes in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.
Baron Gruner's locked brown leather diary containing his own written record of crimes against women, stolen by Holmes and used to break his engagement in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client.
A genuine Ming dynasty egg-shell saucer used by Holmes as a distraction prop to gain entry to Baron Gruner's house in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client.
The great yellow Mazarin Stone, a Crown diamond stolen by Count Sylvius and recovered by Holmes in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone by switching it while the count played the violin.

A wax bust of Holmes made by the French modeller Tavernier, used in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone to deceive Count Sylvius into thinking Holmes was seated by the window while Holmes moved freely about.

Douglas Maberley's roman à clef manuscript in The Adventure of the Three Gables, in which he described his own story of betrayal under disguised names, providing Isadora Klein's motive for stealing it.
Curare-tipped arrows from a small South American blowpipe, discovered by Holmes in The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire as the true source of the infant's wounds, not the mother's bite.
A counterfeiter's printing press and two thousand forged Prescott banknotes hidden beneath a floorboard in The Adventure of the Three Garridebs, the object of Nathan Garrideb's elaborate ruse.
A chip in the stone parapet at Thor Bridge in The Problem of Thor Bridge, made by a pistol tied to a weight that pulled it into the water after firing, proving Maria Gibson staged her own death.

Cyanea capillata, the Lion's Mane jellyfish, identified by Holmes as the creature responsible for Fitzroy McPherson's death, described as more dangerous and painful than a biting reptile, in The Adventure of the Lion's Mane.
A small blue bottle of prussic acid kept by Eugenia Ronder in The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger, a temptation Holmes defuses by persuading her to choose to live.

A hidden gas pipe running along the skirting into Josiah Amberley's strong-room, discovered by Holmes as the murder weapon used to asphyxiate his wife and her companion in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.

Sherlock Holmes's systematic method of eliminating the impossible so that whatever remains must be the truth, combined with training himself to notice what he sees, applied in the Blanched Soldier case.

Holmes's undercover disguise and surveillance technique used in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone, where he successively posed as a workman and an old woman, convincingly deceiving those under observation.
Microscopic evidence analysis used by Sherlock Holmes in The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place to identify substances such as glue from crime-scene samples.

A deduction method used by Sherlock Holmes in The Adventure of the Creeping Man, identifying a nine-day interval pattern in Professor Presbury's erratic behaviour to suggest regular ingestion of a powerful drug.

Alibi-breaking technique used by Sherlock Holmes in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman, verifying the box-office chart at the Haymarket Theatre to prove that Amberley had not attended the performance he claimed as his alibi.

Baron Adelbert Gruner's motive in 'The Adventure of the Illustrious Client': safeguarding his engagement to a wealthy young woman by suppressing evidence of his criminal past, including the 'lust diary' that catalogued his crimes against women.

Isadora Klein's motive in 'The Adventure of the Three Gables': suppressing a roman-à-clef manuscript written by a former lover who portrayed her as the wolf, to prevent a scandal that would endanger her impending marriage to the young Duke of Lomond.

Maria Gibson's motive in 'The Problem of Thor Bridge': a frantic soul-jealousy over her husband's love for Miss Dunbar that drove her to contrive her own suicide in such a way as to make the governess appear guilty of murder.

Professor Presbury's motive in 'The Adventure of the Creeping Man': an untimely love affair with the much younger daughter of his colleague Professor Morphy, driving him to seek Lowenstein's langur serum to appear younger.
Josiah Amberley's motive in 'The Adventure of the Retired Colourman': a frantic, miser-like jealousy over his wife's suspected infidelity that drove him to a mediaeval Italian-style revenge.

Thor Bridge, a single broad stone span across a narrow lake at the Gibson estate in The Problem of Thor Bridge in the Case Book, where Maria Gibson's body is found and a chip in the parapet proves her suicide-as-murder scheme.

Camford, the fictional university town in 'The Adventure of the Creeping Man' where Professor Presbury holds his chair and to which Holmes and Watson travel to investigate the professor's disturbing nocturnal behaviour.
The village of Fulworth on the Sussex coast in The Adventure of the Lion's Mane, near Holmes's retirement villa on the southern Downs commanding a broad view of the Channel.

Shoscombe Old Place, Sir Robert Norberton's famous racing estate in The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place in the Case Book, where Holmes uncovers a scheme to conceal a death until after a key Epsom race.
The Haven in Lewisham in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman, Josiah Amberley's retirement house that conceals the gas-chamber strong-room where he murdered his wife and Dr Ray Ernest.

Lowenstein of Prague's langur-monkey serum in 'The Adventure of the Creeping Man', tabooed by the medical profession because its maker refused to reveal the formula, self-administered by Professor Presbury to arrest ageing.

The racehorse Shoscombe Prince in 'The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place', the best colt in England according to his trainer, who ultimately wins the Derby and nets his owner eighty thousand pounds in bets.
The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier is narrated by Holmes himself at Watson's urging, noting wryly that without Watson's ejaculations of wonder he misses an element of artful presentation.
The Adventure of the Lion's Mane is narrated directly by Holmes from his Sussex retirement, acting as his own chronicler since Watson had passed almost beyond his ken by that period.
By the time of The Adventure of the Lion's Mane, Holmes had withdrawn entirely to his little Sussex home near Fulworth, with only an old housekeeper and his bees for company.

The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, an Arthur Conan Doyle short-story collection that includes the first appearance of Professor Moriarty and culminates in Holmes's apparent death at the Reichenbach Falls.

Professor Moriarty, the 'Napoleon of crime' in the Sherlock Holmes stories, an extremely tall and thin criminal mastermind who organises much of London's undetected crime and becomes Holmes's most dangerous adversary.

Silver Blaze, a Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes story in which Holmes investigates the disappearance of the racehorse Silver Blaze and the murder of its trainer John Straker, solving the case partly through the curious incident of the dog in the night.

The Adventure of the Cardboard Box, a story in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes and Lestrade investigate the sending of two severed human ears to Miss Susan Cushing of Cross Street, Croydon, with Jim Browner identified as the killer.

The Yellow Face, a story in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes notable as one of Holmes's rare failures, in which his confident but mistaken deductions about Grant Munro's wife Effie and the yellow-masked figure in the cottage are corrected by the truth.
The Stockbroker's Clerk, a Sherlock Holmes story from The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes in which Watson, newly settled in Paddington, investigates the suspicious employment of Hall Pycroft.

The Gloria Scott, the fifth story in 'The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes', in which Holmes recounts his earliest case involving a convict ship, mutiny, and a coded message that destroys an old man's peace.

The Musgrave Ritual, a Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes story in which Holmes decodes an ancient family ritual document at Hurlstone Manor in Sussex, leading to the discovery of the English Crown of Charles I and the fate of butler Richard Brunton.
The Reigate Squires, a Sherlock Holmes story from The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes instructs Inspector Lestrade to arrest two men for the murder of their coachman, William Kirwan.

The Crooked Man, a story in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes investigates the death of Colonel James Barclay and concludes that a just providence—and the sudden appearance of the disfigured Henry Wood—killed him.

The Resident Patient, the ninth story in 'The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes', in which a patient named Blessington is found hanged in his room, a suicide that Holmes exposes as murder by former associates.

The Greek Interpreter, a story in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes first introduces Mycroft Holmes and investigates the abduction of the Greek interpreter Mr. Melas, forced to serve as unwitting intermediary in a criminal scheme.

The Naval Treaty, a Sherlock Holmes story from The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes investigates the theft of a secret treaty between England and a foreign power, recovered on behalf of Percy Phelps.

The Final Problem, the story in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes first names Professor Moriarty as the Napoleon of Crime and both men plunge into the Reichenbach Falls, leaving Watson to believe Holmes dead.

The Silver Blaze case, in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, in which Holmes investigates the disappearance of the Wessex Cup favourite Silver Blaze and the murder of its trainer John Straker at King's Pyland stables on Dartmoor.
The Cardboard Box severed ears mystery in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, in which Sherlock Holmes investigates the delivery of a cardboard box filled with two human ears.
The theft of a secret Anglo-Italian naval treaty from Percy Phelps's desk at the Foreign Office, investigated by Sherlock Holmes in The Naval Treaty.

Sherlock Holmes's investigation of Professor Moriarty's criminal organisation in The Final Problem, uncovering a deep organising power behind half the evil in London and planning a coordinated arrest for the following Monday.
John Straker, the retired jockey turned trainer at King's Pyland who conspires to secretly lame Silver Blaze for betting gain and is fatally struck by the horse's hoof, in Silver Blaze.
Colonel Ross, the well-known sportsman and owner of the racehorse Silver Blaze in the Sherlock Holmes story of the same name, who praised his trainer Straker as an excellent servant.
Fitzroy Simpson, a well-born man who has squandered a fortune on betting and wagered five thousand pounds against Silver Blaze, making him a suspect in the horse's disappearance in Silver Blaze.
Jim Browner, a Liverpool steward who murders his unfaithful wife and her lover Alec Fairbairn and sends their severed ears in a cardboard box, in The Adventure of the Cardboard Box.
Grant Munro, a hop merchant earning seven or eight hundred a year who consults Holmes about his wife Effie's secret in The Yellow Face.
Effie Munro, née Hebron, who had lived in Atlanta before her marriage to Grant Munro and whose devotion to her husband is central to the misunderstanding in The Yellow Face.
Hall Pycroft, a former clerk at Coxon and Woodhouse of Drapers' Gardens who is lured into a fraudulent job offer, becoming the client in The Stockbroker's Clerk.
Reginald Musgrave, the owner of Hurlstone and keeper of the Musgrave family ritual, who consults Holmes about the disappearance of his butler Brunton in The Musgrave Ritual.
Richard Brunton, the butler at Hurlstone Manor who is caught consulting the Musgrave Ritual by candlelight and subsequently disappears, in The Musgrave Ritual.

Colonel James Barclay, commanding officer of the Royal Mallows at Aldershot, found dead under suspicious circumstances in The Crooked Man — he had fallen with his feet over an armchair and his head on the ground near the fender.
Henry Wood, a former Corporal known as 'the smartest man in the regiment' who returned from India disfigured and crooked, the 'Crooked Man' at the centre of the Holmes story of that name.

Mr. Melas, a Greek interpreter who earns his living partly as interpreter and partly as guide to wealthy Orientals, and who is kidnapped and held captive in The Greek Interpreter from The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.
Percy Phelps, a Foreign Office clerk in The Naval Treaty who receives the secret treaty through his uncle Lord Holdhurst's influence and whose honour is saved when Holmes recovers it.
Trainer John Straker's plot to secretly nick the tendons of racehorse Silver Blaze to throw the Wessex Cup, motivated by debts from keeping a secret mistress, as uncovered by Holmes in Silver Blaze.

Professor Moriarty's criminal empire in The Final Problem, described by Holmes as the Napoleon of crime who organises nearly all the evil that goes undetected in London, directing every crime at one remove through his web of agents.
The theft of the secret naval treaty between England and Italy from the Foreign Office in 'The Naval Treaty', in which the document is snatched from Phelps's desk while he briefly left the room.

Jim Browner's double murder of his unfaithful wife Mary and her lover Alec Fairbairn, the severed ears of both victims being sent in a cardboard box to Miss Cushing, in The Adventure of the Cardboard Box.

The confrontation between Sherlock Holmes and Professor Moriarty at the Reichenbach Falls in Switzerland, from which two sets of footmarks lead toward the edge but none return, ending with both men's apparent deaths in The Final Problem.
Professor Moriarty's direct visit to Holmes at 221B Baker Street in The Final Problem, demanding he drop his investigation and warning of lethal consequences if he does not.

Sherlock Holmes's core investigative method, articulated in 'Silver Blaze', of separating absolute undeniable fact from theorists' embellishments and then applying imagination to reconstruct what happened.

Holmes's celebrated deduction from a dog that did not bark in the night — proving the stable intruder was known to the animal — which identifies the trainer John Straker as the culprit in Silver Blaze.

Holmes's analysis of the Reigate quarrel note to determine it was written by two people alternating words, and that the two hands share family characteristics showing the writers are blood-relatives, in The Reigate Squires.
Geometric decoding of shadow lengths and compass bearings used by Sherlock Holmes in The Musgrave Ritual to locate the hidden crown of Charles I by following the ancient family ritual's instructions.

Holmes's forensic method of identifying the recipient of a cardboard box by comparing the anatomical characteristics of the enclosed severed ear with Miss Cushing's own ear, establishing a family connection in The Adventure of the Cardboard Box.

Silver Blaze, a famous racehorse of Isonomy stock with a white forehead and mottled off-foreleg in 'Silver Blaze', stolen and hidden by his trainer Straker as part of a scheme to maim him for betting profit.

The crown of King Charles I, concealed beneath Hurlstone Manor and recovered through the Musgrave Ritual in 'The Musgrave Ritual', subsequently retained by the Musgrave family after legal proceedings and the payment of a considerable sum.
A grey roll of paper constituting the secret treaty between England and Italy in 'The Naval Treaty', stolen from the Foreign Office and recovered by Holmes.

A cardboard box sent to Miss Cushing in 'The Adventure of the Cardboard Box' containing two freshly severed but mismatched human ears, which Holmes uses to trace the murder of Jim Browner's wife and her lover.

The ancient Musgrave family ritual document — a cryptic question-and-answer verse beginning 'Whose was it? His who is gone' — which, decoded by Holmes, reveals the hiding place of the Crown of King Charles I in The Musgrave Ritual.
Dartmoor and King's Pyland training stables in Silver Blaze in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, the moorland setting where the champion racehorse vanishes and trainer John Straker is found dead.

The Reichenbach Falls in Switzerland in The Final Problem in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, the fearful torrent-swollen abyss into which Holmes and Moriarty both fall during their struggle on the ledge.

The Diogenes Club on Pall Mall in The Greek Interpreter in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, the eccentric club where Mycroft Holmes spends his fixed daily hours and where members are forbidden to address one another.

Hurlstone Manor, the famous ancient manor house in Sussex that is the seat of the Musgrave family and the site of the buried Crown of King Charles I uncovered by Sherlock Holmes in The Musgrave Ritual.

Professor Moriarty's criminal syndicate in 'The Final Problem', described by Holmes as the organiser of half that is evil and of nearly all that is undetected in London, directed by Moriarty from a central position of apparent respectability.
Holmes's disguise as a decrepit aged Italian priest, adopted to travel safely with Watson to the Continent while evading Moriarty's agents in The Final Problem.
Straker's motive in 'Silver Blaze': financial ruin incurred by a secret mistress who had plunged him into debt, leading him to plot maiming Silver Blaze to profit from a bet on the race.

Professor Moriarty's personal motive in 'The Final Problem': to devote his entire energies to destroying Holmes in revenge for Holmes's exposure of his criminal network, having warned Holmes directly that he would do so.
Dr Watson narrates The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, explaining in The Yellow Face that he publishes short sketches based on the cases in which Holmes's singular gifts have involved them both.

In The Greek Interpreter, Mycroft Holmes is described as auditing government accounts and, when exceptional synthesis is needed, serving as a de facto clearing-house for all British government intelligence.
The Return of Sherlock Holmes, an Arthur Conan Doyle short-story collection in which Holmes reappears after the Reichenbach affair and resumes investigations including the Empty House murder.

Colonel Sebastian Moran, formerly of the 1st Bangalore Pioneers and described as the second most dangerous man in London, who serves Professor Moriarty and uses a Von Herder air-gun to commit murder in The Adventure of the Empty House.

Inspector Stanley Hopkins, a young Scotland Yard detective in whom Holmes had high hopes, who called Holmes in seven times on fully justified cases, including The Adventure of Black Peter, The Adventure of the Abbey Grange, and The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez.

The Adventure of the Empty House, the Return of Sherlock Holmes story set in 1894 in which Holmes reappears after three years, having survived the Reichenbach Falls, and captures the assassin Colonel Sebastian Moran.

The Adventure of the Norwood Builder, a Sherlock Holmes story from The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes investigates a case brought to him after the perceived death of Professor Moriarty, with Inspector Lestrade involved.

The Adventure of the Dancing Men, a Return of Sherlock Holmes story in which Holmes deciphers a series of cryptic stick-figure messages and uncovers the American criminal Abe Slaney's pursuit of Elsie Cubitt.

The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes concerning Miss Violet Smith, who is mysteriously followed on her weekly cycle rides near Charlington, leading Holmes to expose Jack Woodley's abduction scheme.

The Adventure of the Priory School, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes investigates the abduction of the only son of the Duke of Holdernesse from his preparatory school, with James Wilder revealed as the instigator.

The Adventure of Black Peter, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes and Inspector Stanley Hopkins investigate the harpooning murder of retired sea captain Peter Carey, with Patrick Cairns revealed as the killer.

The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes and Watson break into the blackmailer Milverton's home—described as the king of all blackmailers—and witness his murder by one of his victims.

The Adventure of the Six Napoleons, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes and Lestrade trace the systematic smashing of plaster Napoleon busts to Beppo's concealment of the Black Pearl of the Borgias inside one of them.
The Adventure of the Three Students, a story in 'The Return of Sherlock Holmes' in which Holmes investigates which of three students tampered with a university scholarship examination paper.

The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez, a Return of Sherlock Holmes story in which Holmes and Inspector Stanley Hopkins investigate the stabbing of a secretary at a professor's Kent home, tracing the murder to the professor's concealed wife Anna.

The Adventure of the Missing Three-Quarter, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes investigates the disappearance of Cambridge rugby star Godfrey Staunton—described as the hinge the whole team turns on—with Dr. Leslie Armstrong protecting the secret.

The Adventure of the Abbey Grange, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes set in the winter of 1897 in which Holmes and Watson investigate the death of Sir Eustace Brackenstall at Abbey Grange, with Inspector Stanley Hopkins and Captain Jack Crocker involved.

The Adventure of the Second Stain, a Return of Sherlock Holmes story described as the most important international case of the series, in which Holmes recovers a stolen diplomatic letter with the help of Lady Hilda Trelawney Hope.

Ronald Adair, the Honourable son murdered by Colonel Sebastian Moran with an expanding bullet fired from an air-gun through an open window in Park Lane, whose death opens The Adventure of the Empty House.

Von Herder's custom air-gun in 'The Adventure of the Empty House', built by a blind German mechanic, noiseless and of tremendous power, used by Colonel Moran to shoot at Holmes's wax dummy and subsequently confiscated for the Scotland Yard Museum.

A perfectly executed wax facsimile of Sherlock Holmes placed in the Baker Street window in 'The Adventure of the Empty House', designed by Holmes during his afternoon visit to draw Moran's fire and expose his position.
Camden House in 'The Adventure of the Empty House', the vacant building directly opposite 221B Baker Street from which Colonel Moran fires at Holmes's wax dummy, enabling Holmes to capture him.
Reichenbach Fall, the Swiss gorge where Moran dropped rocks on Holmes after Moriarty's plunge, referenced by Moran himself in The Adventure of the Empty House in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
Holmes's dramatic reappearance to a stunned Watson across his study table in The Adventure of the Empty House, revealing he had survived the Reichenbach Falls by feigning death for three years.
The Park Lane Mystery, the investigation by Sherlock Holmes into the murder of the Honourable Ronald Adair under unusual and inexplicable circumstances, described in The Adventure of the Empty House.

Holmes's step-by-step chain of deductive inferences in The Adventure of the Dancing Men and others, each inference depending on its predecessor, a chain Holmes claims he has forged and tested at every link.
A substitution cipher using stick-figure 'dancing men' to represent English letters, decoded by Holmes using frequency analysis in The Adventure of the Dancing Men.
Hilton Cubitt, the Norfolk squire of Riding Thorpe Manor who is found shot through the heart in The Adventure of the Dancing Men, the case involving the dancing men cipher sent by Abe Slaney.
Abe Slaney, described as the most dangerous crook in Chicago, who is condemned to death at the Norwich winter assizes for his crimes in The Adventure of the Dancing Men.
Elsie Cubitt, née Patrick, an American woman who had fled to England to escape Abe Slaney and married Hilton Cubitt in The Adventure of the Dancing Men.
Miss Violet Smith, the solitary cyclist of Charlington in The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist, who eventually inherits a large fortune and becomes the wife of Cyril Morton after her ordeal.
Jack Woodley, a bully and villain in The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist who abducts Violet Smith and is sentenced to ten years' imprisonment for abduction and assault.
The Duke of Holdernesse, described as 'the greatest and perhaps the wealthiest' peer in The Adventure of the Priory School, holding the titles of Baron Beverley and Earl of Carston among others.
James Wilder, secretary to the Duke of Holdernesse in The Adventure of the Priory School, who is revealed to be the Duke's illegitimate son.
Captain Peter Carey, known as Black Peter — a swarthy, violent seal and whale fisher born in 1845 — whose murder by harpoon in his cabin is investigated by Holmes in The Adventure of Black Peter.
Patrick Cairns, a harpooner with twenty-six voyages who kills Captain Black Peter Carey with a harpoon when Carey drew a knife, as investigated in The Adventure of Black Peter.
Charles Augustus Milverton, described as the worst man in London and the king of all blackmailers, whose operations are the subject of The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton.
Beppo, an Italian piece-work man with a criminal record who concealed the Borgia Pearl inside a plaster Napoleon bust and broke into houses to retrieve it in The Adventure of the Six Napoleons.

The most famous pearl in existence in 'The Adventure of the Six Napoleons', recovered by Holmes from its hiding place inside one of the plaster busts of Napoleon, having been concealed there after its theft from the Princess of Colonna.

Professor Coram (real name Sergius), a Russian revolutionary living under a false identity at Yoxley Old Place, whose hidden past is exposed when his long-concealed wife emerges in The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez.
Anna, the Russian wife of the man calling himself Professor Coram, who had been hiding in the concealed room of Yoxley Old Place for years before emerging in The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez.
A golden pince-nez with broken black silk ends, discovered at the murder scene in The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez and used by Holmes to reconstruct the killer's physical appearance.

Godfrey Staunton, the Cambridge and England rugby three-quarter described as the hinge the whole team turns on, whose sudden disappearance triggers the investigation in The Adventure of the Missing Three-Quarter.

Dr. Leslie Armstrong, one of the heads of the Cambridge medical school and a thinker of European reputation, who protects his patient's secret in The Adventure of the Missing Three-Quarter and is characterised by Holmes as a man of energy and character.
Lady Brackenstall, born Mary Fraser of Adelaide, the graceful and beautiful wife of the brutal Sir Eustace Brackenstall in The Adventure of the Abbey Grange.
Captain Jack Crocker, a newly made captain and officer of magnificent record in the naval fleet, who appears in The Adventure of the Abbey Grange.

Lady Hilda Trelawney Hope, described as the loveliest woman in London, who visits Holmes in The Adventure of the Second Stain to conceal her role in the disappearance of her husband's secret state document.
Trelawney Hope, the Right Honourable Secretary for European Affairs and the most rising statesman in the country, whose missing state document is the subject of The Adventure of the Second Stain.

Colonel Sebastian Moran's murder of Ronald Adair, shot with an expanding bullet from an air-gun through an open window to prevent Adair from exposing his card-cheating, as investigated by Holmes in The Adventure of the Empty House.

Charles Augustus Milverton's blackmail operation in The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton, in which he pays high sums for compromising letters from wealthy and powerful people and then squeezes them dry with smiling ruthlessness.

Beppo's theft and concealment of the Borgia Pearl in The Adventure of the Six Napoleons, in which Beppo hid the pearl by pressing it into wet plaster inside a Napoleon bust in the workshop where he was employed.

The theft of a secret letter from a foreign potentate out of Secretary Trelawney Hope's dispatch-box, carried out by Lady Hilda using a duplicate key obtained from the spy Eduardo Lucas, in The Adventure of the Second Stain.

Colonel Moran's motive for murdering Ronald Adair in 'The Adventure of the Empty House': silencing the only person who had discovered Moran's card cheating, since exposure would have meant expulsion from his clubs and financial ruin.

Eduardo Lucas's hold over Lady Hilda Trelawney Hope in 'The Adventure of the Second Stain', consisting of an indiscreet pre-marital letter which he used to coerce her into stealing a confidential document from her husband's despatch-box.
Holmes's disguise as an elderly deformed book collector in The Adventure of the Empty House, used to approach Watson unrecognised before casting off the frockcoat and old books to reveal himself.
Holmes's disguise as Escott the plumber in The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton, under which he courts Milverton's housemaid by walking out with her each evening to gain access to the house.

Holmes's application of English letter-frequency analysis to the dancing-men cipher in The Adventure of the Dancing Men, using the preponderance of E as the key to bootstrap identification of the full alphabet.

Sherlock Holmes's identification technique in 'The Adventure of the Priory School' of distinguishing bicycle tire impressions—identifying Dunlop, Palmer, and other makes—to trace a route and identify a victim.

Holmes's detection technique in 'The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez' of scattering cigarette ash across a floor space in front of a suspect's hiding place to record whether she emerged in his absence.

Sherlock Holmes's safe-cracking technique, described in 'The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton', using drills, a jemmy, and skeleton keys, with safe-opening being a particular hobby of Holmes's.

After the Reichenbach confrontation, Holmes spent three years in disguise — including two years in Tibet visiting Lhassa and a period in Persia and France — before returning in The Adventure of the Empty House.
Colonel Sebastian Moran served as Professor Moriarty's chief of staff before being absorbed into the criminal organisation, as Holmes reveals in The Adventure of the Empty House.
The Sign of the Four, the 1890 Sherlock Holmes novel by Arthur Conan Doyle in which Holmes and Watson investigate the Agra treasure mystery involving Jonathan Small and the criminal pact of four.

Mary Morstan, the blonde young woman in The Sign of the Four who engages Holmes to investigate the annual pearls sent to her and the disappearance of her father, and who subsequently becomes Dr. Watson's wife.

Thaddeus Sholto, the nervous, perpetually jerking and smiling son of Major Sholto who contacts Mary Morstan to reveal his father's secret and guide Holmes and Watson to the Agra treasure in The Sign of the Four.
Bartholomew Sholto, a clever man who discovers the location of the Agra treasure hidden in his house at Pondicherry Lodge, and is found dead in a wooden armchair — murdered in The Sign of the Four.

Major John Sholto, a former Indian army officer stationed at the Andaman Islands who betrayed his partner Captain Morstan and kept the entire Agra treasure, his deathbed confession setting the plot of The Sign of the Four in motion.
Jonathan Small, a one-legged ex-soldier from Worcestershire and one of the four conspirators in the Agra treasure pact who pursues revenge against those who betrayed him in The Sign of the Four.
Tonga, a small Andaman Islander who accompanies Jonathan Small in The Sign of the Four and kills Bartholomew Sholto with a poisoned blowpipe dart.
Mahomet Singh, one of the four signatories of the Sign of Four criminal pact, a tall, fierce old fighting-man who conspires with Jonathan Small to seize the Agra treasure in The Sign of the Four.
Abdullah Khan, one of the four signatories of the Sign of Four criminal pact, a tall and fierce fighting-man who conspires with Jonathan Small to seize the Agra treasure in The Sign of the Four.
Dost Akbar, one of the four signatories of the Sign of Four criminal pact who conspires with Jonathan Small, Abdullah Khan, and Mahomet Singh to seize the Agra treasure in The Sign of the Four.
Athelney Jones, a stout, red-faced, burly Scotland Yard inspector who is called to the Sholto case and initially arrests Thaddeus Sholto in The Sign of the Four.
Mordecai Smith, the owner of the steam launch Aurora whose boat is hired by Jonathan Small and Tonga for their escape down the Thames in The Sign of the Four.

Captain Arthur Morstan, a British army officer stationed at the Andaman Islands with Major Sholto who disappears after arriving in London, his fate and his share of the Agra treasure forming the mystery at the heart of The Sign of the Four.
Wiggins, the leader of the Baker Street Irregulars street-urchin network who reports directly to Sherlock Holmes and coordinates the search for the steam launch Aurora in The Sign of the Four.
Mrs. Cecil Forrester, Mary Morstan's employer in Lower Camberwell in The Sign of the Four, on whose behalf Holmes had previously unravelled a domestic complication.
Achmet, a merchant travelling under a false name who carries the secret of the Agra treasure and is murdered by the Four conspirators inside the Agra fortress in The Sign of the Four.

The Sholto treasure case, the central investigation of The Sign of the Four in which Mary Morstan engages Holmes to explain the origin of the pearls she has been sent annually and the murder of Bartholomew Sholto.

The murder of Bartholomew Sholto, a crime in The Sign of the Four in which Tonga shoots the discoverer of the Agra treasure with a poisoned blowpipe dart, leaving the card 'The sign of the four' at the scene.

The theft of the Agra treasure by Jonathan Small and his three Sikh confederates, who murdered the merchant Achmet at the Agra fortress and buried his body, the crime recounted in Small's confession in The Sign of the Four.

Major John Sholto's betrayal of the Four pact, keeping the Agra treasure for himself without fulfilling his agreement with Jonathan Small, which drives Small's decades-long pursuit of revenge in The Sign of the Four.

Jonathan Small's consuming motive in 'The Sign of the Four': vengeance against Major Sholto who stole the Agra treasure from the Four, an obsession he nursed by day and night that became his sole purpose.

The driving motive behind the Sign of the Four pact: Jonathan Small's insistence that the Agra treasure belongs only to the three surviving conspirators who shared in its acquisition, making its recovery a matter of honour and right.
Dr. Watson's love for Mary Morstan in 'The Sign of the Four', which prevents him speaking while the treasure exists and which he declares openly to her once the treasure is lost to the Thames.

The Agra treasure, the vast collection of jewels valued at not less than half a million sterling in The Sign of the Four, including one hundred and forty-three first-water diamonds, which Jonathan Small and his three co-conspirators seized from an Indian merchant.
A solid iron chest of Indian workmanship, central to The Sign of the Four, which had contained the Agra treasure but was found empty when Holmes and Watson recovered it on deck.

A diagram found in 'The Sign of the Four' showing the plan of a large building with numerous halls, corridors, and passages, marked in the corner with a hieroglyphic of four crosses whose arms touch, and the words 'The Sign of the Four.'

The six fine pearls sent annually in unmarked boxes to Mary Morstan since her father's disappearance, ultimately traced by Holmes to the Sholto family's guilt over the Agra treasure in The Sign of the Four.
A long dark blowpipe thorn with a gummy poison on its tip, found above Bartholomew Sholto's ear in The Sign of the Four, identified as Tonga's murder weapon.
Wooden-leg stump prints alongside a heavy boot-mark on the window sill at Pondicherry Lodge in The Sign of the Four, used by Holmes to identify Jonathan Small as one of the two intruders.
Tiny bare footprints covering the roof garret floor at Pondicherry Lodge in The Sign of the Four, at first mistaken for a child's prints but identified by Holmes as belonging to Tonga.
A trail of creosote left by Tonga's foot across the ground at Pondicherry Lodge in The Sign of the Four, detected by Holmes's nose and used to track the small-footed killer.
The card reading 'The sign of the four' left beside Bartholomew Sholto's body in The Sign of the Four, placed by Jonathan Small as a mark of revenge for the broken Agra treasure pact.

Toby dog creosote scent tracking used by Sherlock Holmes in The Sign of the Four, following a trail across London in the Sholto treasure case, with Holmes valuing Toby's help above the entire detective force.

Holmes's unofficial network of Baker Street street-urchin informants who can go everywhere and overhear everyone, deployed by Wiggins to locate the steam launch Aurora during the Sholto treasure case in The Sign of the Four.

Holmes's old-sailor disguise in The Sign of the Four — comprising wig, whiskers, and eyebrows — used to gather intelligence along the Thames waterfront without being recognised by the criminal classes who know his face.

A body of specialist detective literature written by Sherlock Holmes, described in 'The Sign of the Four', including a monograph distinguishing the ashes of various tobaccos and another on the influence of trade upon the form of the hand.
The Sign of Four, the criminal pact sworn by Jonathan Small, Mahomet Singh, Abdullah Khan, and Dost Akbar to share the Agra treasure equally, a compact later betrayed by Major Sholto.

The Baker Street Irregulars, a gang of street boys led by Wiggins and employed by Sherlock Holmes as an informal detective division, used in The Sign of the Four to search for the steam launch Aurora.

Agra fortress, an immense, labyrinthine old fort full of great deserted halls, winding passages, and long corridors where the Agra treasure is hidden and Achmet is murdered by the Four conspirators in The Sign of the Four.

A British penal colony in the Bay of Bengal, 340 miles north of Sumatra, where Captain Morstan, Major Sholto, and Jonathan Small all served, and where Tonga the Andaman Islander was found, as described in The Sign of the Four.

The River Thames in The Sign of the Four, the setting of the steam-launch Aurora river chase ending in Tonga's death and Jonathan Small's capture, and the permanent watery grave of the scattered Agra treasure.
The Lyceum Theatre in the Strand in 'The Sign of the Four', the seven-o'clock rendezvous point at the third pillar from the left of the side entrance where Mary Morstan meets Holmes and Watson.
The steam launch Aurora, a fast black boat with two red streaks owned by Mordecai Smith, used to flee down the Thames in The Sign of the Four and pursued by Holmes and Watson in the river chase.

Toby, an ugly long-haired lop-eared tracking dog of mixed spaniel and lurcher breeding in 'The Sign of the Four', borrowed from Sherman in Pinchin Lane and praised by Holmes as more useful than the entire detective force for his extraordinary scent.
Tonga's blowpipe and short, dark-wood poisoned spines used to murder Bartholomew Sholto in 'The Sign of the Four', clapped to his lips and discharged while concealed aboard the Aurora.
Watson's declaration of love to Mary Morstan on the night the Agra treasure is lost to the Thames, with Mary accepting him, marking the romantic resolution of The Sign of the Four.
The killing of the treasure-carrier Achmet inside Agra fort by the four conspirators — a Sikh stabbing him twice — as recounted by Jonathan Small in Chapter XII of The Sign of the Four.

The police steam launch's pursuit of the steam launch Aurora down the Thames, in which Holmes urges the stokers to heap it on and burn the boat if necessary, to recapture Jonathan Small in The Sign of the Four.

Jonathan Small and his faithful companion Tonga's nighttime escape by boat from the Andaman Islands penal settlement, as Small hopped on his wooden leg to reach the waiting vessel, in The Sign of the Four.

Jonathan Small throws the entire Agra treasure overboard into the Thames when cornered in The Sign of the Four, declaring that nobody else shall have it, leaving it scattered over five miles of riverbed.
The iron Agra treasure chest, when finally opened by Holmes and Watson in The Sign of the Four, proves to be completely empty — Jonathan Small having already thrown the jewels into the Thames.
The Sign of the Four recounts that the three Sikh conspirators received life sentences and Jonathan Small was condemned to death (commuted to transportation) for the Agra murder of Achmet.
The Valley of Fear, a Sherlock Holmes novel by Arthur Conan Doyle in which the murder at Birlstone Manor is linked to the criminal organisation the Scowrers in the Pennsylvania coalfields.
Dr. John H. Watson, Sherlock Holmes's trusted companion and chronicler, present with Holmes at the investigation of the Birlstone Manor murder in The Valley of Fear.

John Douglas, the popular American-born resident of Birlstone Manor in The Valley of Fear, whose apparent murder is later revealed to be a staged killing and who is identified as the former Pinkerton agent Birdy Edwards.

Birdy Edwards, a Pinkerton detective who infiltrates the Scowrers under the aliases John McMurdo and Steve Wilson and gathers evidence that breaks up their criminal organisation in The Valley of Fear.

Boss McGinty (Black Jack McGinty), the Bodymaster of Vermissa Lodge and leader of the Scowrers in The Valley of Fear, a ruthless criminal who controls all violence in the Pennsylvania coalfields and eventually meets his fate on the scaffold.

Ted Baldwin, a violent Scowrer in The Valley of Fear whose attempt to murder John Douglas at Birlstone results in his own death by a double-barrelled shotgun, his body being used to stage Douglas's apparent death.

Cecil Barker, a tall, loose-jointed acquaintance of John Douglas, resident at Hales Lodge, Hampstead, who is a familiar figure in Birlstone village and is present at the manor on the night of the apparent murder in The Valley of Fear.
Ivy Douglas, the beautiful and much younger wife of John Douglas at Birlstone Manor, who later sends a telegram reporting his death overboard off St. Helena, in The Valley of Fear.
Ettie Shafter, daughter of the landlord Shafter in Vermissa Valley, who falls in love with the undercover agent Birdy Edwards and eventually marries and escapes with him in The Valley of Fear.
Inspector Alec MacDonald, a young but trusted Scotland Yard detective who has distinguished himself in several cases and leads the Birlstone Manor murder inquiry in The Valley of Fear.
White Mason, the quiet, comfortable-looking local Sussex officer who is a personal friend and calls in Scotland Yard and Sherlock Holmes to the Birlstone Manor murder in The Valley of Fear.

Fred Porlock, a pseudonymous informant within Moriarty's criminal organisation who is driven by rudimentary moral aspirations to send Holmes advance warnings, described as a shifty and evasive personality in The Valley of Fear.
Captain Marvin, a stern officer of the Mine Police in Vermissa Valley who pursues the Scowrers and whose rifle-sight gleams behind Birdy Edwards at the climax of The Valley of Fear.
Brother Morris, an elderly, clean-shaved Scowrer in the Vermissa Valley who, troubled by Catholic conscience over the organisation's murders, attempts to counsel moderation in The Valley of Fear.
Mike Scanlan, a member of Scowrers Lodge 341 in Vermissa Valley who befriends Birdy Edwards on his arrival and unwittingly carries a sealed note to Ettie Shafter, in The Valley of Fear.

John McMurdo, the alias used by undercover Pinkerton detective Birdy Edwards when he infiltrated Lodge 29 in Chicago in The Valley of Fear, claiming brotherhood membership to gain the trust of the Scowrers.
Steve Wilson, a secondary alias used by Birdy Edwards when he lodged at Hobson's Patch in The Valley of Fear during the course of his undercover mission.
The Birlstone Manor murder, the central case of The Valley of Fear in which John Douglas is apparently killed at his Sussex manor house, a crime ultimately linked to Professor Moriarty.

The Scowrers' intimidation and murder campaign, the decade-long reign of terror by the Eminent Order of Freemen in Vermissa Valley, Pennsylvania, in The Valley of Fear, resulting in numerous murders without a single conviction before Birdy Edwards's infiltration.

The Scowrers, the violent secret society operating under the guise of the Eminent Order of Freemen in the Pennsylvania coalfields in The Valley of Fear, led by Boss McGinty and responsible for a decade of unsolved murders and intimidation.
Birlstone Manor House, Sussex, a venerable Jacobean house of liver-coloured brick surrounded by a moat with a drawbridge, where the staged murder of John Douglas takes place in The Valley of Fear.
Vermissa Valley, Pennsylvania, a prosperous coal and iron district whose central township is dominated by the violent Scowrers under McGinty, forming the setting for Part II of The Valley of Fear.

McGinty's Union House saloon in Vermissa, Pennsylvania, in Part II of The Valley of Fear, the rough gathering place dominated by Black Jack McGinty where the Scowrers conduct their violent criminal business.
A card inscribed 'V.V. 341' found beside the Birlstone Manor corpse in The Valley of Fear, later decoded to stand for Vermissa Valley Lodge 341 of the Scowrers criminal society.
A Pennsylvania Small Arms Company shotgun with its barrel sawn off and its triggers wired together, found at Birlstone Manor in The Valley of Fear as evidence of an American-style killing.

A wedding ring absent from the Birlstone Manor corpse's finger in The Valley of Fear, significant because the substituted dead man could not wear Douglas's tight-fitting ring, revealing the body-swap.
A tattooed brand of a triangle within a circle on the forearm of John Douglas, marking him as a former member of the Scowrers, which Sherlock Holmes observes as a key clue in The Valley of Fear.

A weighted bundle containing a dumb-bell, knife, boots, and a grey tweed suit hidden at the bottom of Birlstone Manor's moat, recovered by Holmes to expose the staged-death scheme in The Valley of Fear.
A book-cipher warning message sent by Porlock to Holmes in The Valley of Fear, deciphered from Whitaker's Almanac to reveal that a man named Douglas at Birlstone House faces mortal danger.
The book-cipher method applied by Holmes in The Valley of Fear to decode Fred Porlock's warning, reasoning that the cipher key must be Whitaker's Almanac—a large, double-column book in common use.
Holmes's ruse of issuing a notice about draining the Birlstone moat so that whoever hid evidence in the water would retrieve it at nightfall and so be caught, in The Valley of Fear.

Birdy Edwards's Pinkerton infiltration method in 'The Valley of Fear', in which he joined the Freemen lodge in Chicago and then the Scowrers at Vermissa, sharing their councils, to gather evidence against the criminal organisation from within.

Professor Moriarty's role as hired consultant in 'The Valley of Fear': engaged by Scowrer exiles abroad to locate Birdy Edwards in England, with the commission arriving through one of Moriarty's lieutenants.

The Scowrer survivors' sworn motive in 'The Valley of Fear': a solemn oath to kill Birdy Edwards in revenge for their destroyed organisation, pursuing him from Chicago with two near-successes before Moriarty is brought in.

The moment Birdy Edwards reveals his true identity to the Scowrers assembled at the lodge meeting, simultaneously springing the police ambush with rifles breaking through every window in The Valley of Fear.

The staged murder at Birlstone in which John Douglas kills Ted Baldwin and substitutes Baldwin's similarly built body — disfigured beyond recognition — for his own, to fake his own death in The Valley of Fear.
In Part II of The Valley of Fear, John Douglas of Birlstone Manor is revealed to be the Pinkerton detective Birdy Edwards, who had infiltrated and broken up the Scowrers gang under deep cover.

In the Epilogue to The Valley of Fear, Holmes deduces that Moriarty arranged for John Douglas to be thrown overboard in a gale off St Helena, ensuring the Pinkerton detective would never give evidence against him.

Whitaker's Almanac, the key used to decode the book-cipher message in 'The Valley of Fear', selected by Holmes as the most likely common reference book of sufficient pages and wide circulation for a coded communication.

The moat and drawbridge of Birlstone Manor in 'The Valley of Fear', the only approach to the house, raised every evening and lowered every morning—a security feature that becomes central to the murder plot.
